extent of wetlands on site.
This location overlaps the following wetlands:
Data limitations
The Service's objective of mapping wetlands and deepwater habitats is to produce reconnaissance level
information on the location, type and size of these resources. The maps are prepared from the analysis of high
altitude imagery. Wetlands are identified based on vegetation, visible hydrology and geography. A margin of error
is inherent in the use of imagery; thus, detailed on-the-ground inspection of any particular site may result in
revision of the wetland boundaries or classification established through image analysis.
The accuracy of image interpretation depends on the quality of the imagery, the experience of the image analysts,
the amount and quality of the collateral data and the amount of ground truth verification work conducted.
Metadata should be consulted to determine the date of the source imagery used and any mapping problems.
Wetlands or other mapped features may have changed since the date of the imagery or field work. There may be
occasional differences in polygon boundaries or classifications between the information depicted on the map and
the actual conditions on site.
Data exclusions
Certain wetland habitats are excluded from the National mapping program because of the limitations of aerial
imagery as the primary data source used to detect wetlands. These habitats include seagrasses or submerged
aquatic vegetation that are found in the intertidal and subtidal zones of estuaries and nearshore coastal waters.
Some deepwater reef communities (coral or tuberficid worm reefs) have also been excluded from the inventory.
These habitats, because of their depth, go undetected by aerial imagery.
Data precautions
Federal, state, and local regulatory agencies with jurisdiction over wetlands may define and describe wetlands in a
different manner than that used in this inventory. There is no attempt, in either the design or products of this
inventory, to define the limits of proprietary jurisdiction of any Federal, state, or local government or to establish
the geographical scope of the regulatory programs of government agencies. Persons intending to engage in
activities involving modifications within or adjacent to wetland areas should seek the advice of appropriate federal,

A full description for each wetland code can be found at the National Wetlands Inventory website

March 21, 2022 
SUBJECT:
Facility Naming Policy – Adoption 
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to approve the Parks, Open Space Lands and Public Facility Naming Policy. 
BACKGROUND:
In fall 2021, staff received several inquiries from individuals requesting consideration to name a park facility in memory/in 
honor of someone they believed was deserving.  In reviewing the criteria by which parks, open spaces and other park 
facilities can be named, staff determined that the existing policy from 1979 appeared out of date and lacked the specificity 
that may be helpful to staff, the Park and Recreation Commission (“Commission”) and the City Council when considering 
requests for facility naming (Attachment 2). 
The existing policy distinguishes between the criteria for the naming of parks and open spaces versus public facilities or 
amenities within parks such as buildings, ballfields, trails, etc.  Parks are named after their geographic location or unusual 
natural features, whereas facilities or amenities may be named after selected individuals in their honor.  In general, park 
naming has followed this protocol with only three exceptions, King Park, dedicated in honor of Dr. Martin Luther King; 
Bayside Joinville Park, in which the Joinville name was added in honor of Sgt. Gordon Joinville, a San Mateo police officer 
who died in the line of duty; and Trinta Park,  named in honor of Manuel Trinta, who donated the land for use as a Little 
League field.  Requests for facility or amenity naming for selected individuals has been intermittent over the years but has 
included dedications such as the John Lee Dog Park, the Gary Yates Bocce Court Complex, Fitzgerald Field at Central Park, 
and Chanteloup Field at Beresford Park. 
The Commission had several discussions, first to consider general policy direction and then a second to review and 
comment on a first draft before approving a final version of the policy at its February meeting by a vote of 5-0.  The final 
version being presented to the City Council reflects the following general policy recommendations of the Commission: 

Parks and open spaces should continue to be named after their geographical locations or by significant or unusual 
natural, cultural, geographical, historical or environmental features.  Renaming of parks where existing names 
have common community acceptance is strongly discouraged.  Naming of parks after individuals or organizations 
may be considered under special circumstances, i.e. significant donation of land or in recognition of an individual 
who contributed extraordinary service to the City.

Public facilities or amenities within parks may be named after selected individuals and should be considered a 
special honor in recognition of their outstanding contributions that may include a donation of land or a significant 
financial contribution to improve a facility, a record of meaningful volunteer or community service, or noteworthy 
public service as a community member. 

The current review and approval process for naming remains largely unchanged, i.e. review and recommendation 
261 of 662

--- page 262 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
by the Director of Parks and Recreation, review and recommendation by the Commission, and final consideration 
and action by the City Council. It should be noted, however, that both the Commission and the City Council must 
each conduct a public hearing under the proposed policy, which, if approved, would be a new procedural 
requirement.

As an alternative to facility naming, the City may choose to recognize individuals through the placement of objects 
such as benches, flagpoles, monument rocks, etc. within a park and include dedication signage as appropriate. 
If approved, the Resolution will rescind existing Resolution 49 (1979) and adopt the updated Policy (Attachment 1). 
BUDGET IMPACT:
There is no budgetary impact for taking this action.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
Adoption of an updated parks, open spaces, and public facilities naming policy is not a project subject to CEQA because it is 
an organization or administrative activity that will not result in direct or indirect physical changes to the environment  
(CEQA Section 15378 (b) (5). 
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

RESOLUTION NO. ____ ( 2022)
PARKS, OPEN SPACE LANDS AND PUBLIC FACILITY NAMING POLICY 
WHEREAS, the existing Policy for Naming City-Owned Park and Open Space Lands, Public Facilities and 
Places dates from 1979 ; and
WHEREAS, staff determined that the policy was outdated and needed better criteria to assist staff, the 
Park and Recreation Commission and the City Council when evaluating the merit of facility naming requests;           
and
WHEREAS,  the Park and Recreation Commission provided general policy direction and comments before 
approving the proposed updated policy at their meeting on February 2, 2022 by a vote of 5-0 ; and
WHEREAS, the Park and Recreation Commission is recommending that the City Council approve the new 
policy in order to provide better guidelines for staff, the Commission and the City Council when evaluating 
facility naming requests. 
NOW, THEREFORE, BE IT RESOLVED THAT THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, 
hereby: 
1.
Rescinds Resolution No. 49 (1979) Establishing Policy and Procedures for Naming City-Owned Park 
and Open Space Lands, Public Facilities and Places; and
2.
Approves the Parks, Open Space Lands and Public Facility Naming Policy in substantially the formed 
attached to this Resolution.  
263 of 662

--- page 264 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
PARKS, OPEN SPACE LANDS AND PUBLIC FACILITY NAMING POLICY
Section I:  Purpose and Application 
A. Purpose: To provide guidelines and procedures for the naming of City Parks, Open Space 
lands and public facilities and amenities within parks. 
B. Application: This policy has been created to guide staff, the Park and Recreation 
Commission and the City Council in taking action concerning requests to name City parks, 
open space lands and public facilities and amenities within parks.  This process does not 
apply to the naming of streets or other non-park civic facilities. 
C. The City Council shall have the final authority to approve names of parks, open space lands 
and public facilities and amenities within parks after review and recommendation by the 
Park and Recreation Commission and Department of Parks and Recreation staff.  
Section II: Definitions 
A. Parks: Real property owned and managed by the City used for public recreation purposes, 
whether developed or undeveloped, including lands identified as open space. 
B. Public Facilities and Amenities: City owned facilities/amenities that are located within a park 
or within part of a facility.  Examples include, but are not limited to, athletic/ball fields, 
recreation buildings, picnic areas, trails, sports courts, playgrounds, gardens and meeting 
rooms within a facility.  
Section III: Policy
A. Parks:
1)  Each park and open space shall be named after its geographical location or by its 
significant or unusual natural, cultural, geographical, historical or environmental 
feature.  Names shall not be duplicated and shall be compatible with the area in 
which the park and open space is located. 
2) Naming parks after individuals or organizations may be considered under special 
circumstances such as a significant donation of land to the City and Department of 
Parks and Recreation or in recognition of an individual who contributed 
extraordinary service to the City of San Mateo. 
3) Renaming of parks where existing names have common community usage and 
acceptance is strongly discouraged and will be considered only under rare 
circumstances. 
B. Public Facilities and Amenities: 
264 of 662

--- page 265 ---
1) Public facilities and amenities within parks may be named after selected individuals 
or organizations in their honor.  Naming is a special recognition that is considered 
for persons and organizations deserving exceptional recognition for their unique, 
substantial or outstanding contribution to the Department of Parks and Recreation 
or to a specific park or amenity.  Criteria that will be considered include, but are not 
limited to: donation of land or significant financial gifts to improve a facility; a record 
of meaningful volunteer service to the Department of Parks and Recreation or to a 
facility; and substantial and noteworthy public service as a community member.
2) Satisfying one or more of the criteria listed above does not assure approval of any 
naming recommendation. 
3) The preference is for an individual to be deceased, but this is not a requirement. 
4) In the case where an existing named facility is demolished or decommissioned, the 
City Council may choose to discontinue the use of the name, re-use the name for a 
replacement facility or re-assign the name to another similar facility. 
Section IV: Process for Naming Parks and Public Facilities and Amenities 
A.  Any member of the public may submit a written request for naming a Park or Public Facility 
or Amenity to the Director of Parks and Recreation for consideration by the Park and 
Recreation Commission and City Council. Nominators should provide  supporting 
background and justification  for one or more of the criteria outlined above in Section B.1. 
The Director will conduct any research necessary to make an informed recommendation to 
the Park and Recreation Commission. The Commission will review the proposed naming 
recommendation, conduct a public hearing and make a recommendation to the City Council 
to either support or deny the request
The City Council will review the proposed recommendation, conduct a public hearing and 
vote whether to approve or deny the naming.  Nothing in the policy shall preclude either 
the Park and Recreation Commission or City Council from initiating a naming request on 
their own.    
B. Upon approval by the City Council, the site shall officially be designated as such on all maps 
and records. 
C. A dedication plaque consistent with the existing setting and landscape shall be placed at 
each named facility and where possible, include information on the contributions of the 
individual.  Responsibility for the purchase and installation of any dedication plaque shall be 
borne by the requesting party unless otherwise negotiated with the Department of Parks 
and Recreation.  On delivery to the City, the dedication plaque shall become the property of 
the City. The location, size and type of recognition objects shall be approved by the 
Department of Parks and Recreation. 
The City will make reasonable effort to preserve any dedication plaque or other object 
placed at the site.  If required, due to repair of the surrounding area, construction or re-
265 of 662

--- page 266 ---
design of a facility or any other reason, the plaque may be relocated, removed, stored or 
otherwise disposed in the sole discretion of the City  
Section V: Naming Rights as Part of a Fundraising Campaign 
A. When undertaking major park or facility renovations and/or new construction, the 
Department of Parks and Recreation may choose to engage in fundraising campaigns  in 
order to raise community awareness and provide financial assistance to the City.  This policy 
allows for Naming Rights to fund amenities within a park, such as buildings, playgrounds, 
athletic/ballfields, picnic areas, etc.  Naming Right opportunities and levels of sponsorship 
shall be identified in advance and publicized as part of the fundraising campaign.  
Recognition should be commensurate with said donations or funding.  
An agreement between the City and major donors shall be negotiated which outlines the 
terms of the donation, including the length of time the Naming Right shall be in effect. 
 
B. Naming Rights shall be aligned with the mission and values of the Department of Parks and 
Recreation and the City of San Mateo and shall avoid products, services or affiliations that 
promote political or religious organizations,  alcohol, tobacco and cannabis products, or are 
otherwise considered derogatory or offensive by community standards. The City Council 
shall have the right to approve or deny any proposed facility name. 
Section VI: Non-Facility Naming Opportunities
As an alternative to the facility naming opportunities described above, the City may choose 
to recognize individuals, either living or deceased or organizations by the placement of 
objects such as public benches, flagpoles, rocks, trees, or other features within a park or 
facility.  The approval, placement and identification of these dedications will be at the 
discretion of the Parks and Recreation Department staff.  A dedication plaque, appropriate 
to the setting and landscape will be installed.      
Section VII: Miscellaneous
A. No park, open space or facility shall be named after a seated, elected or appointed official. 
B. This policy encourages naming of parks and facilities in a manner which reflects the City’s 
ethnic and cultural diversity and elicit a positive image for all residents.

; - RESOLUTION NO. 49 (1979)

ESTABLISHING POLICY AND PROCEDURES FOR
NAMING CITY-OWNED PARK AND OPEN SPACE
LANDS, PUBLIC FACILITIES AND PLACES

RESOLVED, by the Council of the City of San Mateo, California,
that: ;

WHEREAS, the City of San Mateo has many parks and open space
lands, public facilities and places which are either unnamed or
designated by the general geographical location, with some duplica-
tions, resulting in staff and public confusion as to the precise
location of each site; and :

. WHEREAS, no uniformly, consistent policy has been followed
in naming said sites as they have been named or designated ona
Site-by-site basis, or simply referred to by geographical area,
and there is a need to establish a policy and procedure for naming
such sites to enable the public to locate them readily and to permit
permanent designation on maps and plans; and

WHEREAS, a study of the problem has been made by the Park
and Recreation Commission, and after a hearing thereon on March 7,
1979, has recommended the adoption of policies and procedures for
~Maming such lands, facilities ana places, which appear necessary
and appropriate in the public interest and should, therefore, be
adopted for immediate implementation;

NOW, THEREFORE, IT IS HEREBY DETERMINED and ORDERED, that:

_ i. This Council finds that it is a matter of public interest,
convenience and necessity that a policy and procedures be established
for naming public lands, facilities and places to facilitate proper
reference and location for all purposes.

2. The policy and procedures for naming city-owned park and
open space lands, public facilities and public places shall be as
follows:

a. Each park and open space land shall be named after
its geographical location, without duplication in the
geographical area, or by its significant or unusual natural
features.

b. Each public facility or place within parks, such as
ball-playing fields, groves, walkways, trails and buildings
may be named after selected individuals in their honor as
desired and appropriate.

places shall first be submitted to the Director -of Parks

SUBJECT:
Central Park Playground and Memorial Grove Final Design and Construction Management Service – Agreement 
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve an agreement with RRM Design Group for final design, construction documents and construction administration 
services for the Central Park Playground and Memorial Grove improvement project in the amount of $570,239; establish a 
contingency reserve of $50,000 for a total of $620,239; and authorize the Director of Parks and Recreation to execute the 
agreement in substantially the form presented and issue change orders within the contingency amount. 
BACKGROUND:
In April 2018, the City Council approved the updated Master Plan for Central Park.  In March 2019, an RFP was distributed 
seeking design development landscape architect services for first phase improvements, which include renovation of the 
children’s play area and the creation of a Fallen Heroes Memorial Grove to honor San Mateo residents who died in 
combat, as well as public safety personnel who died while in service to the San Mateo community.  RRM Design Group was 
awarded the agreement for services in July 2019 and over the past several years has worked with staff and the community 
to create preferred designs for the new play area as well as for the Fallen Heroes Memorial Grove.  Both of these designs 
have been reviewed by the public, the Park and Recreation Commission, as well as the City Council.  Although the July 
2019 agreement and scope of services included a task for construction drawing preparation and bidding and construction 
management services, those items were not funded as the cost would be contingent upon the final design selected, its 
complexity and site requirements and the customization of the playground equipment.  Now that the designs for both the 
playground and Memorial have been developed, RRM Design Group has prepared the cost of services in order to bring 
both projects to execution.  In accordance with San Mateo Municipal Code Section 3.60.040(c), competitive bidding is not 
required for these professional services. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
There is sufficient balance in the Central Park Capital Improvement Project to fund the proposed scope of work.  
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
The preparation of construction documents and construction administration services is not a project subject to CEQA as 
these are organizational or administrative activities that will  not result in direct or indirect physical changes in the 
environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378 (b) (5).  The environmental impacts of implementing the Central Park 
Master Plan were previously evaluated in a Negative Declaration approved in April 2018. 
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

The services to be performed under this Agreement shall commence on April 1, 2022 and be 
completed on or about December 31, 2025.  
 
SECTION 5 - PAYMENT 
 
 
Payment shall be made by CITY only for services rendered and upon submission of a payment 
request upon completion and CITY approval of the work performed.  In consideration for the full 
performance of the services set forth in Exhibit A, CITY agrees to pay CONSULTANT a fee in an amount 
of $570,239, pursuant to rates stated in Exhibit A to this Agreement, attached and incorporated by 
reference. 
 
SECTION 6 - TERMINATION 
 
 
Without limitation to such rights or remedies as CITY shall otherwise have by law, CITY shall have 
the right to terminate this Agreement or suspend work on the Project for any reason, upon ten (10) days' 
written notice to CONSULTANT.  CONSULTANT agrees to cease all work under this Agreement upon 
receipt of said written notice. 
 
 
Upon termination and upon CITY's payment of the amount required to be paid, documents 
become the property of CITY, and CONSULTANT shall transfer them to CITY upon request without 
additional compensation. 
 
SECTION 7 - OWNERSHIP OF DOCUMENTS 
 
 
All documents prepared by CONSULTANT in the performance of this Agreement, although 
instruments of professional service, are and shall be the property of CITY, whether the project for 
which they are made is executed or not. 
 
SECTION 8 - CONFIDENTIALITY 
 
 
All reports and documents prepared by CONSULTANT in connection with the performance of 
this Agreement are confidential until released by CITY to the public.  CONSULTANT shall not make any 
such documents or information available to any individual or organization not employed by 
CONSULTANT or CITY without the written consent of CITY before any such release.

SECTION 11 - INDEMNITY 
 
 
CONSULTANT agrees to hold harmless and indemnify CITY, its elected and appointed officials, 
employees, and agents from and against any and all claims, loss, liability, damage, and expense arising 
out of CONSULTANT’s negligence, recklessness or willful misconduct to the maximum extent permitted 
by state law.  To the extent permitted by Civil Code section 2782.8, CONSULTANT agrees to defend City, its 
elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents against any such claims. 
 
SECTION 12 - INSURANCE 
 
CONSULTANT shall procure and maintain for the duration of the contract and three years 
thereafter the insurance specified in Exhibit B to this Agreement. 
 
SECTION 13 - NONASSIGNABILITY

and cannot be transferred, assigned, or subcontracted by CONSULTANT without the prior written 
consent of CITY.

skilled in the professional calling necessary to perform the work agreed to be done under this 
Agreement and that CITY relies upon the skill of CONSULTANT to do and perform the work in the most 
skillful manner, and CONSULTANT agrees to thus perform the work.  The acceptance of CONSULTANT's 
work by CITY does not operate as a release of CONSULTANT from said obligation.

SECTION 17 - COSTS AND ATTORNEY FEES 
 
 
Attorney fees in total amount not exceeding $5000, shall be recoverable as costs (by the filing 
of a cost bill) by the prevailing party in any action or actions to enforce the provisions of this 
Agreement.  The above $5000 limit is the total of attorney fees recoverable whether in the trial court, 
appellate court, or otherwise, and regardless of the number of attorneys, trials, appeals, or actions.  It 
is the intent of this Agreement that neither party shall have to pay the other more than $5000 for 
attorney fees arising out of an action, or actions to enforce the provisions of this Agreement. 
 
SECTION 18 - NON-DISCRIMINATION 
 
 
CONSULTANT warrants that it is an Equal Opportunity Employer and shall comply with 
applicable regulations governing equal employment opportunity.  Neither CONSULTANT nor any of its 
sub-consultants shall discriminate in the employment of any person because of race, color, national 
origin, ancestry, physical handicap, medical condition, marital status, sex, or age, unless based upon a 
bona fide occupational qualification pursuant to the California Fair Employment and Housing Act. 
 
SECTION 19 - MEDIATION 
 
 
Should any dispute arise out of this Agreement, any party may request that it be submitted to 
mediation.  The parties shall meet in mediation within 30 days of a request.  The mediator shall be 
agreed to by the mediating parties; in the absence of an agreement, the parties shall each submit one 
name from mediators listed by either the American Arbitration Association, the State Mediation and 
Conciliation Service, or other agreed-upon service.  The mediator shall be selected by a blind draw. 
 
 
The cost of mediation shall be borne equally by the parties.  Neither party shall be deemed the 
prevailing party.  No party shall be permitted to file a legal action without first meeting in mediation 
and making a good faith attempt to reach a mediated settlement.  The mediation process, once 
commenced by a meeting with the mediator, shall last until agreement is reached by the parties but 
not more than 60 days, unless the maximum time is extended by the parties.

DESCRIPTION OF WORK TO BE PERFORMED  
Task A: Playground Final Design    
RRM will utilize the design development (DD) package prepared during Task E of the Central  
Park Master Plan Improvements Phase 1 agreement as the basis to prepare final construction  
documents, also known as plans, specifications, and estimates (PS&E). The PS&E package will  
comply with City of San Mateo Municipal Code 27.71.080 Submittals Required for a Building  
Permit Application. The contents of the PS&E package will be as follows:  
  
Title and Reference Sheets  
Title and reference sheets pursuant to City standards.  
  
Demolition and Staging Plan  
Demolition and staging plans at 20 scale for all structures and elements requiring removal in  
order to implement the playground design. The plans, notes, and legend will locate, identify, and  
describe the removal and provide instructions for disposal. Additionally, the plans will indicate  
items to salvage or stockpile, such as topsoil, for later use in construction. Construction staging,  
fencing, pedestrian circulation, signage, and haul routes will also be included and meet mitigation  
measures MM AIR-1, MM BIO-1, and MM BIO-2 of the ISMND.  
  
Tree Preservation Plan  
Tree preservation plan at 20 scale for all trees to remain and those to be removed. The plan,  
notes, and legend will locate and identify all trees and provide removal and preservation  
instructions per the arborist report, San Mateo MC 13.40.010, and San Mateo MC 27.71.  
  
Erosion Control Plan  
Erosion control plan at 20 scale for the proposed design that illustrates erosion prevention,  
sediment control, and stormwater quality management during construction per the City of San  
Mateo’s Stormwater Pollution Prevention Program (SEPPP) and NPDES provisions C.3  
requirements. Plans, notes, and legend to include temporary provisions such as the location of a  
stabilized crushed aggregate construction entrance/exit, sediment trapping devices and barriers  
where required, fiber rolls where required, storm drain inlet protection, soil stockpile locations,  
and construction staging area.  
  
ADA Accessibility Plan  
The plans at 20 scale for the proposed accessibility of the project site. The plans, notes, and  
legend will illustrate the dedicated accessible route, accessible signage, and accessible parking for  
the project. Note: this task assumes that a compliant path of travel exists and does not include  
ADA upgrades beyond the limits of the playground area.   
  
DocuSign Envelope ID: A4E6D1A4-B1C8-4FAF-B458-476E54F8EAAA
279 of 662

--- page 280 ---
Design Professional Services 2020 
 
Page 10 of 29 
Rev 7/21 
 
 
  
Central Park  Pla yg round and Memorial Grove  Final Design  
Proposed Scope of Services  
January 13, 2022  
Page  3 of 17   
  
Horizontal Control Plans  
The plans at 20 scale will establish all horizontal control for the playground. The plans, notes,  
and legend will also identify construction layout control points and a coordinate system for  
locating site elements. Note that the horizontal control plans are not included in the 75% PS&E  
submittal.  
  
Site Utility Plans  
Utility plans at 20 scale to illustrate the existing and proposed utilities for the project site. The  
plans, notes, and legend will layout the water, sewer, and storm drains beginning at the  
upstream ends of each utility within the project limits to the downstream connections at the  
existing service mains, assumed to be directly adjacent to the project. This scope assumes that  
the existing off-site and/or on-site utility systems have adequate capacity for the proposed  
project – analysis of existing utilities’ capacities and design of any improvements beyond the  
point of connection are not included in this scope.  
  
Grading, Drainage and Stormwater Plans  
Grading plans at 20 scale will demonstrate the grading and drainage of the project site. The  
plans, notes, and legend will show all vertical control of the project elements, contour grading,  
spot elevations, and cut and fill estimates within the project site. The plans will detail all drainage  
facilities within site. This scope assumes that the existing off-site or downstream storm drain  
infrastructure has adequate capacity for the proposed site development, and analysis or  
upgrades to this infrastructure is not included.  
  
This project is assumed to create and/or replace more than 10,000 square feet of impervious  
surface and is a C.3 regulated project. RRM will design permanent stormwater treatment in  
accordance with the County C.3 Regulated Projects Guide (C.3 Guide, January 2020). The  
design will utilize a combination of low impact development (e.g., interceptor tree credits, self- 
retaining areas, self-treating areas) and stormwater treatment measures (e.g., bioretention,  
pervious pavements). RRM will prepare a C.3 and C.6 Development Review Checklist and  
accompanying C.3 sizing calculations. Per Appendix H of the C.3 Guide, this project is not  
subject to hydromodification requirements and will be sized for stormwater treatment only.  
  
RRM will perform drainage analyses (hydrology and hydraulic) for the project drainage and  
stormwater designs. The C.3 calculations and exhibits, storm drain hydrologic/hydraulic  
calculations and exhibits, reference documentation, and project narrative will be provided in a  
comprehensive drainage and stormwater control plan.   
  
RRM will submit a draft version of the drainage and stormwater control plan and the C.3 and  
C.6 Development Review Checklist with the 75% PS&E and the final version will be submitted  
with the 95% PS&E.  
DocuSign Envelope ID: A4E6D1A4-B1C8-4FAF-B458-476E54F8EAAA

Page  4 of 17   
  
  
Stormwater Pollution Prevention Plan (SWPPP)  
A SWPPP is required for this project (more than one acre of disturbed land) .  RRM will prepare  
plans for temporary erosion control facilities. Temporary facilities will be proposed for the  
protection of graded areas and drainage devices. Details and appropriate notes for construction  
best management practices (BMP) and guidelines for implementation are proposed to be in  
conformance with the State of California Water Resources Board requirements and the  
approved staff report, including sediment control recommendations.  
  
The temporary erosion control plans will be incorporated into the project improvement plans  
as well as into the project SWPPP. RRM will conduct a risk determination based on the project’s  
topography, soil, schedule, location, and receiving water and will then prepare the SWPPP based  
on the project-specific risk level. The SWPPP will be based on the 100% PS&E submittal. This  
task includes preparing permit registration documents using the SMARTS database for  
certification by the Client and submittal to the Water Boards. The City will initiate the project  
in SMARTS. This task does not include construction monitoring, QSP services, or SWPPP  
updates during construction.  
  
The SWPPP is included with the 95% PS&E submittal.   
  
Construction Plan  
Construction plans at 20 scale for the overall playground design layout with a keynoting system  
that identifies all construction items, references to appropriate details, and drawing series  
directions. This plan will include and annotate all site features, walls, hardscape, paving materials,  
ramps, stairs, fences, handrails, and lighting.  
  
Play Equipment Plan and Graphics  
Play equipment plans at 10 scale illustrating the play features in the project area. The plans,  
notes, and legend will include the layout of all off-the-shelf and custom play equipment including  
play equipment use zones and information regarding critical fall height.  
  
Restroom Building Plans  
This scope assumes that the new restroom will be pre-engineered and provided by a  
commercial restroom manufacturer such as Romtec, Public Restroom Company, or similar. The  
restroom will be designed during the design development phase of the project (Task E) and will  
include a custom layout and materials to fit the design goals and site aesthetic.   
DocuSign Envelope ID: A4E6D1A4-B1C8-4FAF-B458-476E54F8EAAA

Page  5 of 17   
  
  
Architectural plans, if needed, for a Site Plan and Architectural Review (SPAR) submittal, will be  
completed during Task E of the current contract pursuant to San Mateo Municipal Code  
27.71.070 . Custom architecture is not included in this scope.  
  
Restroom plan sheets will include floor plans and elevations of the building for bidding purposes.  
Actual shop drawings will be supplied by the manufacturer as a deferred submittal after the  
contract has been awarded.   
  
Site Electrical/Lighting Plans   
Electrical service distribution plans, circuiting, fixture schedules, panel schedules, and electrical  
calculations as required to provide electrical service for site lighting and security purposes,  
irrigation controller, and the restroom building. Preparation of Title 24 Lighting compliance for  
exterior lighting is included. This scope assumes that the existing on-site electrical service is  
adequate for the proposed improvements and a new utility application for the extension of new  
service is not required.   
  
Materials and Site Amenities Plan  
Materials and site amenities plan for the materials and furnishings in the proposed design. This  
plan will show the type and colors of all surfacing, including hardscape, resilient, and synthetic  
surfacing, as well as make, model, and color of all site furnishings  
  
Construction Details  
Details for all construction items illustrated on the construction plans. The details will specify  
materials, dimensions, colors, and finishes and will be keyed to the plan.  
  
Structural Details and Calculations  
Structural plans, details, and calculations for all required features in the playground such as the  
site walls and footings of the boardwalk, towers, and shade structure.   
  
Irrigation Plans and Details  
Irrigation plans at 20 scale illustrating the irrigation design. The plans, notes, and legend will  
detail the system layout, mainline, automatic controller, head layout, piping, filter, and backflow  
device for potable irrigation and/or recycled water irrigation. It is assumed that this system will  
tie into the park’s existing irrigation system. In support of the irrigation plans, we will prepare  
irrigation details that specify the method of construction and proper irrigation system  
component procedures. The irrigation plan will comply with the City park standards, San Mateo  
MC 27.71.080, and the requirements in the Water Conservation in Landscaping project  
information and submission checklist.  
  
DocuSign Envelope ID: A4E6D1A4-B1C8-4FAF-B458-476E54F8EAAA

Planting Plans and Details  
Planting plans at 20 scale demonstrating landscape design per San Mateo MC 27.71.100, MC  
27.71 , and City of San Mateo, Cal., Ord. No. 2021-26. Plans, notes, and legend will include  
layout of all planting indicating plant species, quantity, size and location of all shrubs, ground  
cover, and trees. In support of the planting plans, planting details that specify the method of  
construction and proper planting procedures will also be included. The planting package will also  
include all requirements in the City of San Mateo Water Conservation in Landscaping project  
information and submission checklist.   
  
Specifications  
Technical specifications (CSI format, Division 02 through 32, as applicable) will be provided as  
part of this submittals listed in the tasks below. This scope assumes the City will provide and  
assemble all general and special provisions related to this project. We will review the general  
and special provisions for compatibility with the technical specifications. 75% PS&E submittal will  
include outline specifications only.  
  
Construction Cost Opinions (75%, 95% and Bid-Ready submittals)  
RRM’s subconsultant will prepare a construction cost opinion for the playground at the 75%,  
95 % and final bid-ready stage. The detailed construction cost opinion will break out each  
component of the design with item descriptions and unit costs. Due to many variables  
surrounding bidding and construction conditions, this opinion will not represent a guarantee  
that bids received will be equal to the opinion.  
  
Subtask A.1: PS&E Coordination and Meetings  
RRM will prepare for and attend up to eight virtual Client meetings throughout the course of  
the final design phase. These meetings are intended to provide an opportunity for staff to  
preview and provide input on the design progress, to address issues that may arise, and for  
general coordination. These meetings will be scheduled as needed throughout the process. This  
task also includes preparation, internal coordination, and meeting minutes time. We will also  
coordinate with a City selected artist to meet the City’s public art requirement.  
  
Deliverables:   
•   Prepare for and attend up to eight (8) virtual meetings with City staff  
  
Subtask A.2: Quality Control Review  
RRM will perform an internal review of the construction documents concurrent with the City’s  
review of the 95% complete submittal. This QA/QC review process will be performed by  
senior-level professionals that are not directly involved in the project to provide an independent  
perspective.

Deliverables:

Subtask A.3: 75% PS&E  
RRM will prepare a 75% progress submittal for Parks and Recreation Department review. The  
intent of this submittal is to verify that the design direction is aligned with the City’s  
expectations. The City will provide comments in writing for RRM to respond to.  
  
Deliverables:   
•   75 % PS&E submittal  
  
Subtask A.4: 95% PS&E  
RRM will prepare 95% PS&E package for submittal to the City for building permit review. The  
City is responsible for submitting to all relevant departments, consolidating the various  
comments into a single list/document, and reconciling conflicting comments from different plan  
reviewers.   
  
Deliverables:   
•   95 % PS&E submittal  
  
Subtask A.5: 100% PS&E  
RRM will update the PS&E package based on City plan-check comments and our internal  
QA/QC review and resubmit to the City for back-checking.   
  
Deliverables:   
•   100 % PS&E submittal  
  
Subtask A.6: Bid-Ready PS&E  
When the plans are approved through all relevant departments, RRM will prepare and package  
the bid-ready PS&E submittal and submit electronically to the City. Please note that ‘front-end’  
specifications are the responsibility of the City: general provisions, special provisions, and bid  
forms. We will assist the City in the preparation of the bid schedule including any bid alternates.  
  
Deliverables:   
•   Bid-Ready PS&E submittal

Subtask A.7: Permit Processing Assistance   
RRM will assist the City’s project manager during the permit review process. This assistance  
could include providing written clarifications, preparing exhibits, or attending extra meetings  
( such as with the City Building Official or Fire Marshal, etc.) at the request of the City.   
  
Due to the unknown nature of the level of effort that might be required, the task allows for 24- 
hours of support.   
  
Deliverables:   
•   24- hours of support during permitting review process   
  
Subtask A.8: Play Equipment Manufacturer Coordination   
The design of the playground includes custom playground equipment that requires close  
coordination between playground equipment manufacturers. This task includes coordination  
time between these manufacturers and RRM.   
  
Deliverables:   
•   Custom play equipment coordination   
  
Task B: Playground Bidding and Construction Support   
Subtask B.1: Bidding Assistance  
RRM will support the City during the bidding process. We will respond to bidder questions and  
pre-bid substitution requests forwarded to us by the City and issue addenda as deemed  
necessary and reasonable by the City to clarify design-related issues. As part of this task, we will  
attend one pre-bid conference.  
  
Deliverables:  
  
•   Attend one (1) pre-bid conference  
•   Response to bidder questions, pre-bid substitution request review, and addenda  
  
Subtask B.2: Construction Administration  
Following the award of the general construction contract, RRM will support the City during the  
construction phase of the project.  
  
We will review and respond to requests for information (RFI), change orders (CO), submittals,  
and contractor supplied shop drawings. Under this task, RRM will also prepare and issue  
supplemental instructions as necessary to clarify technical details. RRM will also coordinate with  
consultants, agency, and City staff as necessary.

We assume that the City’s project manager and/or construction manager will filter and respond  
directly to RFI that are straightforward and that not all correspondence will require attention  
and response by RRM.  
  
The fee for this scope is derived from an anticipated construction duration of 12-months at an  
average of 4 hours/week; if construction goes beyond that, we will negotiate a contract  
amendment to continue to provide services.  
  
Deliverables:  
  
•   RFI, CO, submittal reviews, shop drawings responses, supplemental instructions, and general  
record keeping documents  
  
Subtask B.3: Construction Meetings and Observation  
RRM’s project manager and/or designated representative will prepare for and attend up to 12  
construction meetings; due to the unpredictable nature of construction, we assume that the  
meeting frequency will vary based on current activity. This scope anticipates a construction  
duration of up to 12 months. The purpose of these meetings is to generally coordinate with the  
City and contractor and to address issues that are topical.   
  
While in attendance at these construction meetings, RRM will conduct field observations. The  
field construction observation is not intended to be an exhaustive check or a detailed inspection  
of the contractor’s work but is rather to allow RRM to become generally familiar with the work  
in progress and to determine, in general, if the work is proceeding in accordance with the  
contract documents. RRM shall not supervise, direct, or have control over the contractor’s  
work nor have any responsibility for the construction means, methods, techniques, sequences,  
or procedures selected by the contractor, nor for the contractor’s safety precautions or  
programs in connection with the work. These rights and responsibilities are solely those of the  
contractor in accordance with the contract documents.  
  
RRM shall not be responsible for any acts or omissions of the contractor, subcontractor, any  
entity performing any portion of the work, or any agents or employees of any of them. RRM  
does not guarantee the performance of the contractor and shall not be responsible for the  
contractor’s failure to perform work in accordance with the contract documents or any  
applicable laws, codes, rules, or regulations.  
  
Deliverables:  
•   Up to twelve (12) construction meetings/field observations  
•   Field observation memoranda

Subtask B.4: Project Close-Out  
RRM will attend two punch item walks with the contractor and the City, preliminary and final,  
and prepare a memorandum of punch list items for the contractor to address.   
  
Deliverables:  
  
•   Attend two (2) punch item walks and prepare punch list memoranda  
  
Subtask B.5: Record Drawings  
Upon completion of construction work, the design team will compile a digital set of record  
drawings in PDF format based upon the marked-up record drawings, addenda, change orders,  
and other data furnished by the contractor. These record drawings will show significant changes  
made during construction. Because these record drawings are based on unverified information  
provided by a third party, which RRM shall assume will be reliable, RRM cannot and does not  
warrant their accuracy. Note this task does not include re-drafting the contractor markups in  
AutoCAD.  
  
Deliverables:  
  
•   One (1) digital set of record drawings (PDF markups)  
  
Task C: Memorial Grove Final Design    
RRM will prepare final construction documents, also known as final plans, specifications, and  
estimates (PS&E) for the preferred design of the Memorial Grove at Central Park. The Memorial  
Grove is considered a separate project from the Playground (Task J) in regard to funding and  
construction. The PS&E package will comply with City of San Mateo Municipal Code 27.71.080  
Submittals Required for a Building Permit Application.  
  
The description of the contents of the PS&E package are listed in Task J above. However,  
because the scope of this work is smaller than the Playground, some sheets listed above are not  
relevant. The sheets included in this set include:  
•   Title and reference sheets  
•   Demolition and construction staging plan  
•   Tree preservation plan  
•   Erosion control plan  
•   Grading and drainage plan  
•   Utility plan  
•   Construction plan  
•   Construction details  
•   Structural details and calculations

Page  11 of 17   
  
•   Site electrical/lighting plans  
•   Specifications (75% submittal as outline specifications)   
•   Construction cost opinions (95% and bid-ready submittals)   
  
Stormwater  
The Memorial Grove project is assumed to create and/or replace 2,500 square feet (sf) to 5,000  
sf and will implement one of the site-design measures for “small project” as defined in section  
2.3.3 and Appendix L of the C.3 Regulated Projects Guide (January 2020). RRM will prepare a  
stormwater checklist for small projects to meet the San Mateo Countywide Water Pollution  
Prevention Program requirements.   
  
A project Stormwater Pollution Prevention Plan ( SWPPP) is not required for this plan (less than  
one acre of disturbed land); RRM will prepare an erosion control plan for temporary  
construction BMP.  
  
This project is assumed separate from the Playground project (Task J) based on differences in  
anticipated funding sources, construction schedules, and hydrologic conditions.  
  
Subtask C.1: PS&E Coordination and Meetings  
RRM will prepare for and attend up to three Client meetings throughout the course of the final  
design phase. These meetings are intended to provide an opportunity for staff to preview and  
provide input on the design progress, to address issues that may arise, and for general  
coordination. These meetings will be scheduled as needed throughout the process. This task  
also includes preparation, internal coordination, and meeting minutes time.  
  
Deliverables:   
•   Prepare for and attend up to three (3) meetings with City staff (may be virtual meetings)  
  
Subtask C.2: 75% PS&E  
RRM will prepare a 75% progress submittal for internal Parks and Recreation Department  
review. The intent of this submittal is to verify the direction of the design is aligned with the  
City’s expectations and to receive formal comments and direction regarding the site layout and  
general design solutions.  
  
Deliverables:   
•   75 % PS&E submittal

When the plans are approved through all relevant departments, RRM will prepare and package  
the bid-ready PS&E submittal and submit electronically to the City. Please note that ‘front-end’  
specifications are the responsibility of the City: general provisions, special provisions, and bid  
form. We will assist the City in the preparation of the bid schedule including any bid alternates.  
  
Deliverables:   
•   Bid-Ready PS&E submittal   
  
Task D: Memorial Grove Bidding and Construction Support   
Subtask D.1: Bidding Assistance  
RRM will support the City during the bidding process. We will respond to bidder questions and  
pre-bid substitution requests forwarded to us by the City and issue addenda as deemed  
necessary and reasonable by the City to clarify design related issues. As a part of this task, we  
will attend one pre-bid conference.  
  
Deliverables:  
  
•   Attend one (1) pre-bid conference  
•   Response to bidder questions, pre-bid substitution request review, and addenda

Subtask D.2: Construction Administration  
Following the award of the general construction contract, RRM will support the City during the  
construction phase of the project. We will review and respond to RFI, CO, submittals, and  
contractor supplied shop drawings. Under this task, RRM will also prepare and issue  
supplemental instructions as necessary to clarify technical details. RRM will also coordinate with  
consultants, agency, and City staff as necessary.  
  
We assume that the City’s project manager and/or construction manager will filter and respond  
directly to RFI that are straightforward and that not all correspondence will require attention  
and response by RRM.  
  
Deliverables:  
  
•   RFI, CO, submittal reviews, shop drawings responses, supplemental instructions, and general  
record keeping documents  
  
Subtask D.3: Construction Meetings and Observation  
RRM’s project manager and/or designated representative will prepare for and attend up to four  
construction meetings at the site. The purpose of these meetings is to generally coordinate with  
the City and contractor and to address issues that are topical.   
  
While in attendance at these construction meetings, RRM will conduct field observations. The  
field construction observation is not intended to be an exhaustive check or a detailed inspection  
of the contractor’s work but is rather to allow RRM to become generally familiar with the work  
in progress and to determine, in general, if the work is proceeding in accordance with the  
contract documents. RRM shall not supervise, direct, or have control over the contractor’s  
work nor have any responsibility for the construction means, methods, techniques, sequences,  
or procedures selected by the contractor, nor for the contractor’s safety precautions or  
programs in connection with the work. These rights and responsibilities are solely those of the  
contractor in accordance with the contract documents.  
  
RRM shall not be responsible for any acts or omissions of the contractor, subcontractor, any  
entity performing any portion of the work, or any agents or employees of any of them. RRM  
does not guarantee the performance of the contractor and shall not be responsible for the  
contractor’s failure to perform work in accordance with the contract documents or any  
applicable laws, codes, rules, or regulations.   
  
Deliverables:  
•   Up to four (4) construction meetings and field observations

EXHIBIT B 
 
INSURANCE REQUIREMENTS 
 
 
MINIMUM SCOPE OF INSURANCE  
Coverage shall be at least as broad as: 
1. Commercial General Liability (CGL):  Insurance Services Office (ISO) Form CG 00 01 12 07 
covering CGL on an “occurrence” basis, including products-completed operations, personal & 
advertising injury, with limits no less than $2,000,000 per occurrence. If a general aggregate limit 
applies, either the general aggregate limit shall apply separately to this project/location or the 
general aggregate limit shall be twice the required occurrence limit. 
2. Automobile Liability:  ISO Form Number CA 00 01 covering any auto (Code 1), or if CONSULTANT 
has no owned autos, hired, (Code 8) and non-owned autos (Code 9), with limit no less than 
$1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury and property damage. 
3. Workers’ Compensation: as required by the State of California, with Statutory Limits, and 
Employer’s Liability Insurance with limit of no less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury 
or disease.  
4. Professional Liability (Errors and Omissions): Insurance appropriate to the CONSULTANT’s 
profession, with limit no less than $1,000,000 per occurrence or claim, $2,000,000 aggregate 
If the CONSULTANT maintains higher limits than the minimums shown above, the City requires and shall 
be entitled to coverage for the higher limits maintained by the CONSULTANT. 
Other Insurance Provisions 
The insurance policies are to contain, or be endorsed to contain, the following provisions: 
Additional Insured Status 
The City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents are to be covered as insureds on 
the auto policy for liability arising out of automobiles owned, leased, hired or borrowed by or on behalf 
of the CONSULTANT; and on the CGL policy with respect to liability arising out of work or operations 
performed by or on behalf of the CONSULTANT including materials, parts or equipment furnished in 
connection with such work or operations. General liability coverage can be provided in the form of an 
endorsement to the CONSULTANT’s insurance (at least as broad as ISO Form CG 20 10, 11 85 or both 
CG 20 10 and CG 20 37 forms if later revisions used). 
Primary Coverage 
For any claims related to this contract, the CONSULTANT’s insurance coverage shall be primary 
insurance as respects the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents. Any 
insurance or self-insurance maintained by the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, or 
agents shall be excess of the CONSULTANT’s insurance and shall not contribute with it. 
Notice of Cancellation

298 of 662

--- page 299 ---
Design Professional Services 2020 
 
Page 29 of 29 
Rev 7/21 
Each insurance policy required above shall provide that coverage shall not be canceled, except after 
thirty (30) days’ prior written notice (10 days for non-payment) has been given to the City. 
Waiver of Subrogation 
CONSULTANT hereby grants to City a waiver of any right to subrogation which any insurer of said 
CONSULTANT may acquire against the City by virtue of the payment of any loss under such insurance.  
CONSULTANT agrees to obtain any endorsement that may be necessary to effect this waiver of 
subrogation, but this provision applies regardless of whether or not the City has received a waiver of 
subrogation endorsement from the insurer.   
Deductibles and Self-Insured Retentions 
Any deductibles or self-insured retentions must be declared to and approved by the City. The City may 
require the CONSULTANT to purchase coverage with a lower deductible or retention or provide proof 
of ability to pay losses and related investigations, claim administration, and defense expenses within 
the retention.   
Acceptability of Insurers 
Insurance is to be placed with insurers with a current A.M. Best’s rating of no less than A:VII, unless 
otherwise acceptable to the City. 
Verification of Coverage 
CONSULTANT shall furnish the City with original certificates and amendatory endorsements or copies 
of the applicable policy language effecting coverage required by this clause.  All certificates and 
endorsements are to be received and approved by the City before work commences.  However, failure 
to obtain the required documents prior to the work beginning shall not waive the CONSULTANT’s 
obligation to provide them.  The City reserves the right to require complete, certified copies of all 
required insurance policies, including endorsements required by these specifications, at any time.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Agenda Report
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo CA 94403
www.cityofsanmateo.org
Agenda Number:  11
Section Name: CONSENT CALENDAR
File ID: 22-5847
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Drew Corbett, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Information Technology Department
MEETING DATE:
March 21, 2022 
SUBJECT:
Enterprise Resource Planning Software Licensing, Maintenance and Support Services – Agreement
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to approve an alternative purchasing procedure and approve a contract with Tyler Technologies, Inc. 
for Eden financial, human resources, and payroll annual maintenance and support in the amount of $109,355.46, and 
authorize Information Technology Director to execute the contract in substantially the form presented.
BACKGROUND:
The City annually renews maintenance and support for the Eden enterprise resource planning (ERP) software. Renewal 
ensures the City uses a fully-licensed financial and human resources information system and payroll processing system, 
along with vendor-supplied technical and end-user support. Contract renewal requires Council attention due to annual 
rate increases.
In accordance with San Mateo Municipal Code section 3.60.050(b), calling for bids would not have been feasible for this 
agreement.  Eden has been utilized by the City for many years as its financial support software, and it has been fully 
integrated into various City systems.  Continued use of Eden software is required to support mission critical City systems, 
and Tyler Technologies provides the best price point for acquiring support of the Eden software.
BUDGET IMPACT:
Annual maintenance and support for Eden is included in the annual operating budget for the Information Technology 
Department.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This software maintenance renewal contract is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or 
administrative activity that will not result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines 
Section 15378(b)(5).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.
ATTACHMENTS
Att 1 – Proposed Resolution 
Att 2 – Agreement 
316 of 662

--- page 317 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
STAFF CONTACT
Jonathan Strange, Systems Analyst II
jstrange@cityofsanmateo.org
(650) 522-7119

CITY OF SAN MATEO
RESOLUTION NO. ____ (2022)
APPROVING ALTERNATIVE PURCHASING PROCEDURE FOR AN AGREEMENT WITH TYLER TECHNOLOGIES, INC. 
FOR A ONE-YEAR MAINTENANCE AGREEMENT FOR ENTERPRISE RESOURCE PLANNING SOFTWARE
($109,355.46)
WHEREAS, the city requires the use of Tyler Technologies, Inc.’s Eden enterprise resource planning 
software for financial, payroll, and human resources software essential to City operations; 
WHEREAS, the City will benefit from software maintenance and support through annual renewal of the 
maintenance agreement; 
WHEREAS, in accordance with Municipal Code section 3.60.050(b) agreements may be awarded without 
competitive bids if calling for bids would be impracticable, unavailing or impossible and the City Council sets 
forth findings designating the reasons; and
WHEREAS, City staff recommends approval of the agreement with Tyler Technologies, Inc. for one-year 
maintenance for enterprise resource planning software.
NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, HEREBY FINDS and
RESOLVES that:
1. This software maintenance renewal contract is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an
organizational or administrative activity that will not result in direct or indirect physical changes in the 
environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)
2. In accordance with San Mateo Municipal Code section 3.60.050(b), calling for bids would be 
impracticable, unavailing, or impossible because:
a.
Eden enterprise resource planning software has been utilized by the City for many years as 
its support software.
b.
Eden software has been fully integrated into various City systems.
c.
Tyler Technologies, Inc. assisted the City in Eden software implementation, provided 
continued software maintenance support, and has foundational knowledge of the City’s 
systems.
d.
Continued use of Eden software is required to support mission critical City systems.
e.
A maintenance agreement with Tyler Technologies, Inc. provides the best price point for 
acquiring support of the Eden software
3. The agreement with Tyler Technologies, Inc., for a one-year maintenance agreement for
enterprise resource planning software, in the amount of $109,355.46 is approved.
4. The Director of the Department of Information Technology is authorized to execute the agreement on 
behalf of the City.

LICENSE AND SERVICES AGREEMENT
This License and Services Agreement is made between Tyler Technologies, Inc. and Client.
WHEREAS, Client and Tyler are parties to a License and Services Agreement dated January 1, 2021, by 
which Client licensed certain software products from Tyler and purchased certain services from Tyler 
and Tyler provided said license and services in accordance therewith (the “Initial Agreement”);
WHEREAS, Client and Tyler desire to replace the Initial Agreement with updated terms to reflect the 
nature of their ongoing relationship;
NOW THEREFORE, in consideration of the foregoing and of the mutual covenants and promises set forth 
in this Agreement, Tyler and Client agree as follows:
SECTION A – DEFINITIONS

“Agreement” means this License and Services Agreement.

“Business Travel Policy” means our business travel policy.  A copy of our current Business Travel 
Policy is attached as Schedule 1 to Exhibit B.

“Client” means the City of San Mateo and its employees, contractors, and agents.

“Defect” means a failure of the Tyler Software to substantially conform to the functional 
descriptions set forth in our written proposal to you, or their functional equivalent.  Future 
functionality may be updated, modified, or otherwise enhanced through our maintenance and 
support services, and the governing functional descriptions for such future functionality will be 
set forth in our then-current Documentation.

“Developer” means a third party who owns the intellectual property rights to Third Party 
Software.

“Documentation” means any online or written documentation related to the use or 
functionality of the Tyler Software that we provide or otherwise make available to you, including 
instructions, user guides, manuals and other training or self-help documentation. 

“Effective Date” means January 1, 2022.

“Force Majeure” means an event beyond the reasonable control of you or us, including, without 
limitation, governmental action, war, riot or civil commotion, fire, natural disaster, or any other 
cause that could not with reasonable diligence be foreseen or prevented by you or us.

“Initial Agreement” means our prior agreement by which we licensed Tyler Software to you and 
performed certain services related thereto.

“Investment Summary” means the agreed upon cost proposal for the software, products, and 
services attached as Exhibit A.

“Invoicing and Payment Policy” means the invoicing and payment policy.  A copy of our current 
Invoicing and Payment Policy is attached as Exhibit B.

“Maintenance and Support Agreement” means the terms and conditions governing the 
provision of maintenance and support services to all of our customers.  A copy of our current

SECTION B – SOFTWARE LICENSE
1.
Termination of the Initial Agreement.
1.1 As of the Effective Date, the Initial Agreement is hereby terminated by mutual agreement of the 
parties.  The parties’ relationship shall be governed solely by the terms of this Agreement.
2.
License Grant and Restrictions.
2.1 We grant to you a license to use the Tyler Software for your internal business purposes only, in 
the scope of the internal business purposes disclosed to us as of the Effective Date.  You may 
make copies of the Tyler Software for backup and testing purposes, so long as such copies are 
not used in production and the testing is for internal use only.  Your rights to use the Tyler 
Software are perpetual but may be revoked if you do not comply with the terms of this 
Agreement.
2.2 The Documentation is licensed to you and may be used and copied by your employees for 
internal, non-commercial reference purposes only.
2.3 You may not: (a) transfer or assign the Tyler Software to a third party; (b) reverse engineer, 
decompile, or disassemble the Tyler Software; (c) rent, lease, lend, or provide commercial 
hosting services with the Tyler Software; or (d) publish or otherwise disclose the Tyler Software 
or Documentation to third parties.
2.4 The license terms in this Agreement apply to updates and enhancements we may provide to you 
or make available to you through your Maintenance and Support Agreement.
320 of 662

--- page 321 ---
3
2.5 The right to transfer the Tyler Software to a replacement hardware system is included in your 
license.  You will give us advance written notice of any such transfer and will pay us for any 
required or requested technical assistance from us associated with such transfer.
2.6 Where applicable with respect to our applications that take or process card payment data, we 
are responsible for the security of cardholder data that we possess, including functions relating 
to storing, processing, and transmitting of the cardholder data and affirm that, as of the 
Effective Date, we comply with applicable requirements to be considered PCI DSS compliant and 
have performed the necessary steps to validate compliance with the PCI DSS. We agree to 
supply the current status of our PCI DSS compliance program in the form of an official 
Attestation of Compliance, which can be found at https://www.tylertech.com/about-
us/compliance, and in the event of any change in our status, will comply with applicable notice 
requirements.
2.7 We reserve all rights not expressly granted to you in this Agreement.  The Tyler Software and 
Documentation are protected by copyright and other intellectual property laws and treaties.  
We own the title, copyright, and other intellectual property rights in the Tyler Software and the 
Documentation.  The Tyler Software is licensed, not sold.
3.
License Fees.  There shall be no further license fees owed by you for the Tyler Software you licensed 
purchased under the Initial Agreement.  Should you license additional Tyler Software through this 
Agreement or by subsequent amendment thereto, you agree to pay us the license fees in the 
amounts set forth in the applicable Investment Summary.  Those amounts are payable in 
accordance with our Invoicing and Payment Policy.
4.
Escrow.  We maintain an escrow agreement with a third party under which we place the source 
code for each major release of the Tyler Software.  You may be added as a beneficiary to the escrow 
agreement by completing a standard beneficiary enrollment form and paying the applicable annual 
beneficiary fee.  You will be responsible for maintaining your ongoing status as a beneficiary, 
including payment of the then-current annual beneficiary fees.  Release of source code for the Tyler 
Software is strictly governed by the terms of the escrow agreement.  
5.
Limited Warranty.  We warrant that the Tyler Software will be without Defect(s) as long as you have 
a Maintenance and Support Agreement in effect.  If the Tyler Software does not perform as 
warranted, we will use all reasonable efforts, consistent with industry standards, to cure the Defect 
as set forth in the Maintenance and Support Agreement.

Cancellation. We make all reasonable efforts to schedule our personnel for travel, including 
arranging travel reservations, at least two (2) weeks in advance of commitments.  Therefore, if you 
cancel services less than two (2) weeks in advance (other than for Force Majeure or breach by us),

associated with cancelled professional services if we are unable to reassign our personnel.  We will

scheduled commitments.

with industry standards.  In the event we provide services that do not conform to this warranty, we

further agree to provide a reasonably suitable environment, location, and space for the installation 
of the Tyler Software and any Third Party Products, including, without limitation, sufficient electrical 
circuits, cables, and other reasonably necessary items required for the installation and operation of 
the Tyler Software and any Third Party Products.  
7.
Client Assistance.  You acknowledge that the implementation of the Tyler Software is a cooperative 
process requiring the time and resources of your personnel.  You agree to use all reasonable efforts 
to cooperate with and assist us as may be reasonably required to meet the agreed upon project 
deadlines and other milestones for implementation.  This cooperation includes at least working with 
us to schedule the implementation-related services outlined in this Agreement.  We will not be 
liable for failure to meet any deadlines and milestones when such failure is due to Force Majeure or 
to the failure by your personnel to provide such cooperation and assistance (either through action 
or omission).
8.
Background Checks.  For at least the past twelve (12) years, all of our employees have undergone 
criminal background checks prior to hire. All employees sign our confidentiality agreement and 
security policies.

Third Party Services.  If you have purchased Third Party Services, those services will be provided 
independent of Tyler by such third-party at the rates set forth in the Investment Summary and in

the defect or issue, to the extent it relates to our interface with the Third Party Software; and/or (b) 
facilitate resolution with the Developer, unless that Developer requires that you have a separate, 
direct maintenance agreement in effect with that Developer.  In all events, if you do not have a 
Maintenance and Support Agreement in effect with us, you will be responsible for resolving defects 
and other issues related to the Third Party Software directly with the Developer.
SECTION F – INVOICING AND PAYMENT; INVOICE DISPUTES
1.
Invoicing and Payment.  We will invoice you for all fees set forth in the Investment Summary per our 
Invoicing and Payment Policy, subject to Section F(2).  
2.
Invoice Disputes.  If you believe any delivered software or service does not conform to the 
warranties in this Agreement, you will provide us with written notice within thirty (30) days of your 
receipt of the applicable invoice.  The written notice must contain reasonable detail of the issues 
you contend are in dispute so that we can confirm the issue and respond to your notice with either a 
justification of the invoice, an adjustment to the invoice, or a proposal addressing the issues 
presented in your notice.  We will work with you as may be necessary to develop an action plan that 
outlines reasonable steps to be taken by each of us to resolve any issues presented in your notice.  
You may withhold payment of the amount(s) actually in dispute, and only those amounts, until we 
complete the action items outlined in the plan.  If we are unable to complete the action items 
outlined in the action plan because of your failure to complete the items agreed to be done by you, 
then you will remit full payment of the invoice.  We reserve the right to suspend delivery of all 
services, including maintenance and support services, if you fail to pay an invoice not disputed as 
described above within fifteen (15) days of notice of our intent to do so.
SECTION G – TERMINATION
1.
Termination.  This Agreement may be terminated as set forth below.  In the event of termination, 
you will pay us for all undisputed fees and expenses related to the software, products, and/or 
services you have received, or we have incurred or delivered, prior to the effective date of 
termination.  Disputed fees and expenses in all terminations other than your termination for cause 
must have been submitted as invoice disputes in accordance with Section F(2).
1.1 For Cause.  If you believe we have materially breached this Agreement, you will invoke the 
Dispute Resolution clause set forth in Section I(23).  You may terminate this Agreement for 
cause in the event we do not cure, or create a mutually agreeable action plan to address, a 
324 of 662

--- page 325 ---
7
material breach of this Agreement within the thirty (30) day window set forth in Section I(3).  
1.2 Force Majeure.  Either party has the right to terminate this Agreement if a Force Majeure event 
suspends performance of this Agreement for a period of forty-five (45) days or more.
1.3 Lack of Appropriations.  If you should not appropriate or otherwise receive funds sufficient to 
purchase, lease, operate, or maintain the software or services set forth in this Agreement, you 
may unilaterally terminate this Agreement upon thirty (30) days written notice to us.  You will 
not be entitled to a refund or offset of previously paid license and other fees.  You agree not to 
use termination for lack of appropriations as a substitute for termination for convenience. 
SECTION H – INDEMNIFICATION, LIMITATION OF LIABILITY AND INSURANCE
1.
Intellectual Property Infringement Indemnification.  
1.1 We will defend you against any third party claim(s) that the Tyler Software or Documentation 
infringes that third party’s patent, copyright, or trademark, or misappropriates its trade secrets, 
and will pay the amount of any resulting adverse final judgment (or settlement to which we 
consent).  You must notify us promptly in writing of the claim and give us sole control over its 
defense or settlement.  You agree to provide us with reasonable assistance, cooperation, and 
information in defending the claim at our expense.
1.2 Our obligations under this Section H(1) will not apply to the extent the claim or adverse final 
judgment is based on your: (a) use of a previous version of the Tyler Software and the claim 
would have been avoided had you installed and used the current version of the Tyler Software, 
and we provided notice of that requirement to you; (b) combining the Tyler Software with any 
product or device not provided, contemplated, or approved by us; (c) altering or modifying the 
Tyler Software, including any modification by third parties at your direction or otherwise 
permitted by you; (d) use of the Tyler Software in contradiction of this Agreement, including 
with non-licensed third parties; or (e) willful infringement, including use of the Tyler Software 
after we notify you to discontinue use due to such a claim.
1.3 If we receive information concerning an infringement or misappropriation claim related to the 
Tyler Software, we may, at our expense and without obligation to do so, either: (a) procure for 
you the right to continue its use; (b) modify it to make it non-infringing; or (c) replace it with a 
functional equivalent, in which case you will stop running the allegedly infringing Tyler Software 
immediately.  Alternatively, we may decide to litigate the claim to judgment, in which case you 
may continue to use the Tyler Software consistent with the terms of this Agreement.
1.4 If an infringement or misappropriation claim is fully litigated and your use of the Tyler Software 
is enjoined by a court of competent jurisdiction, in addition to paying any adverse final 
judgment (or settlement to which we consent), we will, at our option, either: (a) procure the 
right to continue its use; (b) modify it to make it non-infringing; (c) replace it with a functional 
equivalent; or (d) terminate your license and refund the license fees paid for the infringing Tyler 
Software, as depreciated on a straight-line basis measured over seven (7) years from the 
Effective Date of the Initial Agreement.  We will pursue those options in the order listed herein.  
This section provides your exclusive remedy for third party copyright, patent, or trademark 
infringement and trade secret misappropriation claims.

SECTION I – GENERAL TERMS AND CONDITIONS
1.
Additional Products and Services.  You may purchase additional products and services at our then-
current list price, also by executing a mutually agreed addendum.  The terms of this Agreement will 
control any such additional purchase(s), unless otherwise specifically provided in the addendum.
2.
Dispute Resolution.  You agree to provide us with written notice within thirty (30) days of becoming 
aware of a dispute.  You agree to cooperate with us in trying to reasonably resolve all disputes, 
including, if requested by either party, appointing a senior representative to meet and engage in 
good faith negotiations with our appointed senior representative.  Senior representatives will 
convene within thirty (30) days of the written dispute notice, unless otherwise agreed.  All meetings 
and discussions between senior representatives will be deemed confidential settlement discussions 
not subject to disclosure under Federal Rule of Evidence 408 or any similar applicable state rule.  If 
we fail to resolve the dispute, then the parties shall participate in non-binding mediation in an effort 
to resolve the dispute.  If the dispute remains unresolved after mediation, then either of us may 
assert our respective rights and remedies in a court of competent jurisdiction.  Nothing in this 
section shall prevent you or us from seeking necessary injunctive relief during the dispute resolution 
procedures.
3.
Taxes.  Unless otherwise expressly indicated therein, the fees in the Investment Summary do not 
include any taxes, including, without limitation, sales, use, or excise tax.  If you are a tax-exempt 
entity, you agree to provide us with a tax-exempt certificate.  Otherwise, we will pay all applicable 
taxes to the proper authorities and you will reimburse us for such taxes.  If you have a valid direct-
pay permit, you agree to provide us with a copy.  For clarity, we are responsible for paying our 
income taxes, both federal and state, as applicable, arising from our performance of this Agreement.      
 
4.
Nondiscrimination.  We will not discriminate against any person employed or applying for 
employment concerning the performance of our responsibilities under this Agreement.  This 
discrimination prohibition will apply to all matters of initial employment, tenure, and terms of 
employment, or otherwise with respect to any matter directly or indirectly relating to employment 
concerning race, color, religion, national origin, age, sex, sexual orientation, ancestry, disability that 
is unrelated to the individual's ability to perform the duties of a particular job or position, height, 
weight, marital status, or political affiliation.  We will post, where appropriate, all notices related to 
nondiscrimination as may be required by applicable law.
