SCOPE OF SERVICES 
The Consultant will provide engineering consulting services for all project elements listed in this 
Scope of Services. The project and fee negotiations will be executed in three phases for each 
lift station project as listed below and further defined in this Scope of Services: 
Phase 1 
• 
Task 1 - General Services (Mobilization and Supporting Task 2) 
• 
Task 2 - Verification of Rehabilitation and Basis of Design 
Phase 2 
• 
Task 1 - General Services (Supporting Tasks 3 through 6) 
• 
Task 3 - 50% Design 
• 
Task 4 - 90% Design 
• 
Task 5 – 100% Design and Bid Documents 
• 
Task 6 - Bid Period Assistance 
Phase 3 
• 
Task 1 - General Services (Supporting Task 7) 
• 
Task 7 – Construction Phase Services 
Typical tasks that are required of the Consultant under these categories are detailed in the 
following sections. Throughout the design of this project, the Consultant will submit deliverables 
for review and approval by the City and other governing agencies as required. A preliminary 
description of proposed deliverables has been provided in this Scope of Services and will be 
finalized during contract and fee negotiations. 
The deliverables required by the Consultant will vary depending on the necessary 
improvements to the pump stations and force mains defined for each project. All drawings shall 
be drawn using AutoCAD and PDFs capable of plotting at a legible scale both 11x17 and on 22” 
x 34” sheets shall be provided. Additional deliverable information is provided in the various task 
descriptions below. 
For the basis of design, 50%, 90%, and 100% submittals, the Consultant shall attend a review 
meeting with City staff where the submittals are presented to the City and it is demonstrated that 
previous City comments were addressed in the submittal. 
The City reserves the right to discontinue, alter, or postpone services at any time. 
SANITARY SEWER LIFT STATION REHABILITATION PROJECTS 
This scope assumes that one set of design documents will be prepared for all lift stations, force 
mains, and gravity sewer mains. This scope assumes that the design will include the following 
items, as further described in the basis of design report. 
1. Replace Los Prados #1 lift station and force main 
a. Extend force main to the Flint-Norfolk Pump Station 
b. Reconfigure the intersection of Los Prados and La Selva (layout alternative 2, 
bulb out) 
403 of 662

--- page 404 ---
Attachment A, Scope of Services ‐ Sanitary Sewer Pump Station Rehabilitation Design Services 
Page 3 of 15 
 
 
 
 
2. Abandon Los Prados #2 lift station 
3. New deeper gravity sewer main between the Los Prados #1 and #2 lift stations 
4. Replace Kehoe Kelly lift station and force main 
a. Extend wet weather force main to the Flint Force Main near the intersection of 
Kehoe and Lodi) 
b. Extend dry weather force main to the adjacent gravity sewer system. 
5. Rehabilitate the Arroyo Lift Station and Replace the Force Main 
Key design elements for each project are identified below. 
Kehoe Kelly Pump Station and Force Main Rehabilitation 
The Kehoe Kelly Pump Station is a sanitary sewer lift station serving residential neighborhoods 
and is located at the northeast corner of the Kehoe Avenue and Kelly Street intersection. The lift 
station’s existing firm capacity is 75 gallons per minute (gpm) at 10 feet total dynamic head 
(TDH). It has a dry well and wet well, and discharges through a 6-inch-diameter cast iron / 
asbestos-cement force main approximately 160 feet in length. 
 
Improvements at this lift station include: 
• 
Regrading of surface to correct uneven topography. 
• 
Repaving of the improved site area. 
• 
Replace wet well with a deeper wet well to prevent high water operating level from 
reaching influent lines. 
• 
Replace existing pumps and motors with higher capacity equipment to meet Peak Wet 
Weather Flow (PWWF) demands. 
• 
Replace the force main with appropriate pipe material and diameter to accommodate 
higher capacity pumps. Extend wet weather force main to the Flint-Norfolk force main. 
o This scope assumes the force main will be installed within the roadway at the 16th 
Avenue Channel crossing, between the top of the box culvert and the existing 
road surface. 
o Environmental permitting for force main crossing of the 16th Avenue Channel, if 
deemed necessary. 
o Relocation of the existing water main on the north side of the channel crossing is 
included as an optional task, if deemed necessary. 
• 
Include a 3rd smaller dry weather pump and separate force main that extends to the 
adjacent gravity sewer system. 
• 
Replace the main electrical distribution panel and replace electrical enclosure. 
• 
Include odor control equipment as defined by others. 
• 
Provide a thermographic study of the new electrical equipment per code to determine 
where warning labels shall be installed per NFPA 70E. 
• 
Design temporary sanitary sewer bypass system required to construct improvements. 
• 
Provide shroud or security cover for gate and electrical enclosure padlocks. 
 
Los Prados No. 1 Pump Station and Force Main Rehabilitation Project 
Los Prados No. 1 Pump Station is a sanitary sewer lift station serving residential neighborhoods 
and commercial areas, including restaurants. It is located in the southwest street median of the 
404 of 662

--- page 405 ---
Attachment A, Scope of Services ‐ Sanitary Sewer Pump Station Rehabilitation Design Services 
Page 4 of 15 
 
 
 
 
intersection of Los Prados Street, South Northfolk Street, and La Selva Street. The lift station’s 
existing firm capacity is 460 gpm at 21 feet TDH. It has a dry well, wet well, and aboveground 
portions and discharges through an 8-inch-diameter asbestos-cement force main approximately 
478 feet in length. Fats, oils, and grease are a major issue with this pump station. 
 
Improvements at this lift station include: 
• 
Relocate pump station to the southwest corner of the intersection. 
• 
Reconfigure intersection to have a bulb out for the pump station (BODR intersection 
reconfiguration Alternative 2). 
• 
New deeper wetwell to eliminate surcharging in upstream gravity system. 
• 
Replace the force main with appropriate pipe material and diameter to accommodate 
pump station flow capacity. Extend force main to the Flint-Norfolk Pump Station. 
• 
Upgrade the main electrical distribution panel and replace electrical enclosure. 
• 
Provide a thermographic study of the existing or new electrical equipment per code to 
determine where warning labels shall be installed per NFPA 70E. 
• 
Design temporary sanitary sewer bypass system required to construct improvements. 
• 
Provide shroud or security cover for gate and electrical enclosure padlocks. 
 
Los Prados No. 2 Pump Station Elimination and Gravity Main Between LP1 and LP2 
Los Prados No. 2 Pump Station is a sanitary lift station located across from 3161 Los Prados 
Street, in the driveway between El Parque Circle and Bahia Street, serving residential 
neighborhoods. The lift station’s existing firm capacity is 237 gpm at 8 feet TDH. It has a dry 
well, wet well, and aboveground portions and discharges through an 8-inch-diameter asbestos- 
cement force main approximately 55 feet in length. 
 
Improvements at this Pump Station include: 
• 
Eliminate and abandon Los Prados No. 2 Pump Station 
o Remove all equipment 
o Abandon below grade structures in place 
o Eliminate electrical service. 
• 
Install new deeper gravity sewer main between Los Prados No. 2 and Los Prados No. 1 
pump stations to accommodate pump station elimination. 
El Parque Court Gravity Sewer Replacement 
The existing gravity sewer main in El Parque Court has a significant sag and remains fully 
surcharged at all times. 
 
Improvements include: 
• 
Replace the gravity sewer main within El Parque Court to eliminate the sag. 
 
Arroyo Court Pump Station and Force Main Rehabilitation Project 
Arroyo Court Pump Station is a sanitary sewer lift station serving the adjacent residential 
neighborhood. It is located on Arroyo Court, just south of El Camino Real. The lift station’s 
existing firm capacity is 398 gpm at 30 feet TDH. It has a dry well, wet well, and aboveground 
405 of 662

--- page 406 ---
Attachment A, Scope of Services ‐ Sanitary Sewer Pump Station Rehabilitation Design Services 
Page 5 of 15 
 
 
 
 
control panel.  The force main is 8-inch asbestos concrete and discharges at a manhole near 
the intersection of Arroyo Court and El Camino Real. 
 
Improvements at this lift station include: 
• 
Convert the existing drywell into wetwell. Rehabilitate structure and replace top slab as 
necessary to accommodate the new pump station layout. 
• 
Expand pump station limits to accommodate new electrical equipment and generator. 
• 
Environmental permitting for pump station expansion and tree removal. 
• 
Obtain title reports for adjacent properties to identify limits of existing easements. 
Easement acquisition services are included as optional tasks herein. 
• 
Modify and/or rehabilitate existing wetwell to convert to a flow through structure. 
• 
Replace the force main with appropriate pipe material and diameter to accommodate 
pump station flow capacity. 
• 
Upgrade the main electrical distribution panel and replace electrical enclosure. 
• 
Provide a thermographic study of the existing or new electrical equipment per code to 
determine where warning labels shall be installed per NFPA 70E. 
• 
Design temporary sanitary sewer bypass system required to construct improvements. 
• 
Provide shroud or security cover for gate and electrical enclosure padlocks. 
 
TASK 1 – General Services 
General services include tasks that will be performed by the Consultant throughout the project. 
Task 1 will cover all general services required for Phase 1, Phase 2 and Phase 3 for all of the lift 
station projects awarded to a single Consultant. These services include: 
• 
Project Management 
• 
Project Execution Plan 
• 
Risk Management 
• 
Workshops and Meetings 
• 
Quality Control 
• 
Management of Subconsultants and Subcontractors 
• 
Agency Coordination and City Meeting Support 
• 
Permitting 
• 
Health and Safety Plan 
Further information regarding expectations for these services will be issued as a separate 
program standard in the Design Standards and Guidelines, prior to the Phase 1 Negotiations. 
Task 1.1 – Project Management 
Task 1.1.1 – General Management 
The  Consultant  will  be  responsible  for  detailed  management  of  the  project,  including 
subconsultants and subcontractors, and will keep the City appraised of the status of the project. 
406 of 662

--- page 407 ---
Attachment A, Scope of Services ‐ Sanitary Sewer Pump Station Rehabilitation Design Services 
Page 6 of 15 
 
 
 
 
The Consultant will provide the key management personnel as described in their proposal on this 
project. The Consultant will not reassign the key project personnel without prior approval of the 
City per the Standard Agreement for Professional Consultant Services. 
Task 1.1.4 – Progress Report 
The Consultant will prepare and submit monthly progress reports that include a narrative of the 
work completed by task, project decision log, project action item log, upcoming work, project 
issues, budget and schedule status, percent complete, cost to complete, earned value versus 
actual spent analysis, potential scope or budget changes, and other important project information. 
Task 1.1.5 – Project Invoices 
The Consultant will prepare and submit monthly invoices in accordance with Program standards. 
The invoices will document the man-hours and billing rates for staff for each task in the WBS, as 
well as overhead, profit and any direct costs. The current and previous billing period invoicing, as 
well as a summary of the budget spent, budget remaining, cost to complete, and all backup 
documentation for expenses will be included. The monthly progress report and project schedule 
will be submitted with the project invoice as part of the monthly request for payment. 
Task 1.1.7 – Progress Schedule 
The Consultant will prepare a cost loaded project schedule in significant detail to calculate a 
monthly earned value of work performed. The selection of tasks and task durations will be 
approved by the City. The Consultant will prepare a planned value curve for the Project. Prior to 
invoice submittal, the Consultant will evaluate the percent complete for each activity worked on 
during the billing period. The Consultant will calculate the earned value for the month. The earned 
value will be compared to the planned value and the actual invoice value for the month. The 
Consultant will need to justify any requests for monies over the planned value and explain how 
the overall budget will be met over the life of the project. 
Task 1.1.8 – Logs 
The Consultant will develop and maintain a Decision Log. The log will be used to track project 
decisions and how they were made. The Consultant will submit a copy of the updated Decision 
Log with each monthly Progress Report. The log should include the following information: 
• 
Assign a Decision Number 
• 
Describe the Decision 
• 
Identify the date of the Decision 
• 
Identify the City and Consultant staff party to the Decision 
• 
Identify the effect of the Decision and any impacts to the project scope, schedule, and 
budget 
The Consultant will develop and maintain an Action Item Log. The Consultant will submit an 
updated copy of the Action Item Log at progress meetings and with each monthly Progress 
Report. The log should include the following information: 
• 
Assign an Action Item Number 
• 
Describe the Action Item 
407 of 662

--- page 408 ---
Attachment A, Scope of Services ‐ Sanitary Sewer Pump Station Rehabilitation Design Services 
Page 7 of 15 
 
 
 
 
• 
Identify the originator of the Action Item 
• 
Identify the initiation date of the Action Item 
• 
Describe the problem, issue or interest the Action Item is intended to address 
• 
Specify who is responsible for completing the Action Item 
• 
Specify the Action Item due date 
• 
Identify when the Action Item was completed 
• 
Identify any linked subtasks required for successful completion of the Action Item 
• 
Identify the results of completing the Action Item and any impacts to the project scope, 
schedule, and budget 
Task 1.3 – Workshops and Meetings 
The Consultant will prepare and submit to the City an agenda and meeting minutes for each 
formal meeting or workshop. 
Task 1.3.2 Progress Meetings 
The Consultant will conduct monthly project meetings with the City to review the Consultant 
Project Manager’s report and the status of the project scope, schedule, budget and any issues 
which may affect completion of the project. Meetings should be arranged so that the monthly 
progress report, invoice, and schedule can be submitted at each meeting. Acceptance of the 
monthly progress report, invoice, and schedule at this meeting is not mandatory; the City has up 
to five (5) workdays to review and approve the submittal. 
Other meetings will be scheduled on an as-needed basis. 
Task 1.3.3 Workshops and Focused Meetings 
The Consultant will hold meetings and workshops throughout the project to keep the City 
appraised of the job, review work-in-progress, share information, discuss project submittals, 
present findings of technical analyses, discuss constructability and operation concepts, receive 
and resolve comments, and obtain decisions and direction by City staff. 
For the basis of design, 50%, 90% and 100% design submittals, the Consultant will conduct a 
review meeting with City staff where the submittals are presented to the City and it is 
demonstrated that previous City comments were addressed in the submittal. The City will be able 
to provide new comments to the Consultant. The City review period is typically two (2) weeks for 
each major submittal. At the end of the review period, the Consultant will meet with City staff to 
receive and review City comments. Final adjudication of comments will be documented. 
This scope of services also includes two additional workshops/meetings with City staff as deemed 
necessary throughout the design. 
Task 1.4 – Quality Control 
The Consultant will develop a Quality Assurance/Quality Control (QA/QC) Plan for implementation 
of the Scope of Work. The Consultant’s QA/QC Plan will be reviewed and approved by the City 
Project Manager and will include or reference all the controls necessary for implementation. 
QA/QC Plans shall include detailed requirements for providing the required integrity for all field 
work, including conducting the explorations, sampling, and sample custody and transport. All 
408 of 662

--- page 409 ---
Attachment A, Scope of Services ‐ Sanitary Sewer Pump Station Rehabilitation Design Services 
Page 8 of 15 
 
 
 
 
QA/QC procedures for laboratory work shall be included. Each submittal, or partial submittal of 
testing results shall be accompanied by a written certification, signed by the Consultant’s Project 
Manager and the responsible individual from the applicable subconsultant, stating that all QA/QC 
procedures have been complied with for the information or submittal being delivered to the City. 
Submittals that do not include the written certification of compliance or that contain gross 
deficiencies or errors requiring a significant amount of the City staff time for checking will be 
returned without review until the City is satisfied that a thorough Consultant’s review, compliance 
with standards, checking and correction for coherence, consistency, spelling, etc., has been 
performed. 
Task 1.5 – Management of Subconsultants 
If the Consultant requires the services of any subconsultants, then the Consultant will include a 
list of them in the proposal, explain why they are needed, and provide a general description of 
their effort. 
The Consultant will manage the activities of subconsultants during performance of the project. All 
requirements of the contract shall also apply to subconsultants. 
Task 1.6 – Agency Coordination 
The Consultant will assist the City in coordinating the project with various local/county, 
state/regional, and federal agencies. This task includes attending meetings, as needed, with 
applicable agencies, providing meeting minutes, and providing project information and exhibits to 
the agencies. This task is established as an allowance task and shall only be used when 
specifically requested by the City in writing. Costs for this task shall be invoiced as a separate line 
item and shall include a copy of the authorization from the City. 
Task 1.7 – Permitting 
For all applicable project elements of this Scope of Work, the Consultant will provide contract 
documents, which ensure that facility features and performance and construction procedures 
comply with all conditions of existing permits and permits required to construct this project. 
Construction drawings, specifications and supplemental drawings will be prepared, as necessary 
in the format required to obtain all permits. 
The Consultant will identify and obtain all local/county, state/regional, and federal permits required 
for the projects, including but not limited to environmental permits, encroachment permits, building 
permits, etc. The Consultant will complete all permit applications, prepare supporting 
documentation for the permit applications as required by the issuing agency, furnish the required 
number of copies of all construction drawings and exhibits, and attend meetings with permitting 
agencies as required. The Consultant will coordinate with the City before meeting with any 
permitting agencies. The Consultant will ensure all permit applications are submitted in a timely 
fashion to ensure that design and construction are unimpeded by the permitting process. A critical 
path schedule for all permits shall be included in the schedule developed by the consultant team. 
All permit fees will be paid directly by the City and will not be deemed part of Consultant’s fee. 
The City has prepared a Final Programmatic Environmental Impact Report to comply with the 
California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA). The Consultant will prepare the Project Description 
and assist the City to determine the appropriate level of CEQA review for the project. This scope 
assumes that the project will qualify for categorical exemption. The Consultant will prepare a 
Notice of Exemption. If it is determined that the project does not qualify for categorical exemption,

--- page 410 ---

the Consultant will prepare an Initial Study/Mitigated Negative Declaration as detailed in the 
optional tasks below. 
The environmental permitting scope includes the following for the Arroyo Court and Kehoe Kelly 
Force Main locations. It is assumed that all other work will qualify for categorical exemption and 
will not require additional permitting. 
1. Project coordination and meetings 
2. Preparation of Project Description and CEQA Documentation

10. Riparian Mitigation (likely required for tree removal at Arroyo Court)

b. Coordinate with City to identify off-site mitigation options, if necessary 
c. Develop mitigation concepts and negotiate w/ the Water Board and CDFW. 
d. Prepare Riparian Mitigation and Monitoring Plan (RMMP) 
 
 
Task 3 –50% Design 
This task shall be conducted independently for each lift station; however, one set of design 
documents will be prepared for all lift stations, force mains, and gravity sewer mains.

deliverable including, depending on the specific project, the following at a 50% level of

• 
Civil drawings, including general facility layout and site plan, plus partial plans, sections, 
and details needed to depict the work. 
• 
Demolition drawings for items requiring demolition of existing features 
•

may be applicable 
• 
Structural drawings for all structural modifications or new structures or structural 
components

• 
Statement signed by Consultant’s Project Manager that all deliverables have been

All design work shall be in conformance with the Clean Water Program design guidelines and

The 50% design deliverable shall include 8 hard copies (using 11 x 17 drawings) and one print

3.1.2 – Site Visits 
Site visits will be conducted throughout the design process to document existing conditions, 
take field measurements, document visible utilities, and to review the proposed improvements. 
3.1.2 - Topographic Surveying and Utility Research 
The Consultant and Consultant’s surveying subconsultant shall conduct additional field surveys 
as required to generate topographic and planimetric information and base mapping for drawings 
to support the design. 
The field survey shall establish both horizontal and vertical alignment of the facilities and will note 
all survey monuments, topographic features, surface features (pavements, curbs, retaining walls, 
trees, etc.), utilities, right-of-way limits, property lines, property information, and elevations. The 
Consultant will be responsible for obtaining all permits and access agreements to perform the 
survey, and providing any necessary traffic control. 
3.1.3 - Title Report and Title Review 
Title reports will be obtained for up to four properties, two adjacent to Arroyo Pump Station and 
two on the Kehoe Avenue Force Main crossing at the 16th Avenue Channel. Existing easements 
will be identified and shown on the project drawings and the need for additional easements will 
be identified, where applicable. 
3.1.4 – Geotechnical – Update Report 
The previously prepared geotechnical report will be updated with additional geotechnical data 
obtained during the pump station alternative phase and to reflect the current project scope. 
3.1.5 – Hydraulic Modeling and Analysis 
Hydraulic modeling and analysis will be conducted to identify project impacts to the upstream and 
downstream gravity sewers, force mains, and pump stations. The City’s existing hydraulic model 
will be updated with additional available data and pump curves. The proposed improvements will 
be added to the model for comparing existing and proposed conditions. The proposed impacts 
will be identified and summarized in a brief memorandum. 
Task 4 – 90% Design 
This task shall be conducted independently for each lift station; however, it is assumed that one 
set of design documents will be prepared for all lift stations, force mains, and gravity sewer 
mains. All design work shall be in conformance with the Clean Water Program design guidelines 
and standards which will be provided to the Consultant during the negotiation phase. 
For  the  90%  Design  Submittal,  the  major  design  elements  shall  be  well-established  and 
essentially complete. All supplementary/auxiliary design elements shall be incorporated between 
412 of 662

--- page 413 ---
Attachment A, Scope of Services ‐ Sanitary Sewer Pump Station Rehabilitation Design Services 
Page 12 of 15 
 
 
 
 
the 50% and 90% Design Submittal for a complete design package. Drawings for all disciplines 
shall be substantially complete. The submittal will include the location and arrangement of all 
significant existing and proposed structures and equipment, all existing utilities adjacent to or 
within the construction area, drawing index, legend, etc. Specifications shall be substantially 
complete and include all sections and all divisions including the legal, procedural, and general 
“front-end” sections. 
This submittal will include all finished, checked and complete plans and specifications. No new 
drawings and/or specification sections by the Consultant will be expected after this stage. All City 
comments on the previous submittal will  have been resolved, rejected, addressed and/or 
incorporated in this submittal. The project is essentially finished and submitted as complete. The 
only missing effort is the City final review and regulatory approval with comment incorporation. 
Consultant’s checking will include a review of all deliverables within each discipline (i.e. drawings, 
detailed specifications, project support documentation, etc.) and an inter-discipline coordination 
cross-check to correct discrepancies among the project documents. 
Discipline calculations, databases, construction costs estimates, schedule and other project 
support documentation will be updated to reflect the status of the Drawings and Detailed 
Specifications and will support the 90% design submittal workshops. 
An updated construction cost estimate (AACE Class 2) with basis of estimate narrative and 
construction sequencing plan and schedule shall be included with the deliverables. 
The 90% design deliverable shall include a statement signed by Consultant’s Project Manager 
that all deliverables have been reviewed or otherwise checked in full conformance with 
Consultant’s QA/QC Plan. 
The 90% design deliverable shall include 15 hard copies (using 11 x 17 drawings) and one print 
ready PDF on electronic media acceptable to the City of all deliverables. 
Task 5 – 100% Design and Bid Documents 
This task shall be conducted independently for each lift station; however, it is assumed that one 
set of design documents will be prepared for all lift stations, force mains, and gravity sewer mains. 
All design work shall be in conformance with the Clean Water Program design guidelines and 
standards which will be provided to the Consultant during the negotiation phase. 
Following receipt of all City and regulatory review comments on the 90% Design Submittals, the 
Consultant will prepare the Final (100%) Design and submit a 100% design submittal as a final 
check set. The final check set shall include all bid documents. Prior to submittal, the Consultant 
will have performed an inter-discipline cross-check of the design changes made since 90% design 
and corrected the related discrepancies. The Consultant will not add any new drawings or make 
any changes to the plans or specifications that are not a result of comments received by the City, 
regulatory review, or a final in-house quality control check. Consultant and City shall conduct a 
workshop to verify that all City comments have been addressed and all permit conditions are 
accounted for. After the City’s back-check review is complete, Consultant shall revise the 
documents and submit Final Bid Documents. 
The 100% back-check design deliverable shall include a statement signed by Consultant’s Project 
Manager that all deliverables have been reviewed or otherwise checked in full conformance with 
Consultant’s QA/QC Plan.

The 100% back-check design deliverable shall include 15 hard copies (11x17 drawings) and 
one print ready PDF on electronic media acceptable to the City of all deliverables. 
The Final Bid Documents will include finished, checked and completed plans and specifications 
and will incorporate all City comments from previous submittals, workshops and constructability 
reviews as appropriate. The Consultant will document in-house changes and submit them to the 
City along with the Final Bid Documents. 
The Final Design submittals will also include all final databases, reports, control strategies, 
construction schedules, cost estimates and project documentation. 
The process of assisting the City in obtaining the required permits will be completed. 
All plans and, detailed specifications and engineering calculations will be stamped and signed by 
a Registered Engineer in the State of California. 
An updated construction cost estimate (AACE Class 1) and construction sequencing plan and 
schedule will be included with the deliverables. 
The Final Bid Documents deliverable shall include 5 hard copies (using 11 x 17 drawings) and 
one print ready PDF on electronic media acceptable to the City of all deliverables. The City will 
print formal Bid Documents. 
Task 6 – Bid Period Assistance 
This task shall be conducted independently for each identified projects awarded to Consultant. 
The Consultant shall provide assistance during the bid period, including the following: 
• 
Participate in the pre-bid conference, if necessary 
• 
Prepare responses to bidders questions 
• 
Prepare addenda to Bid Documents, as needed, for providing clarification for items 
identified prior to bid opening. 
• 
Participate in reviewing alternate equipment and material proposals from the contractor, 
if applicable. 
• 
Participate in the evaluation of the submitted bids, furnish consultation and advice to City 
staff and assist with all the related equipment, cost and other analyses as required to 
finalize the award decision. 
• 
Prepare conformed drawings and specifications that incorporate all addenda. The Final 
Conformed Documents deliverable shall include 5 hard copies (using 11 x 17 drawings), 
2 full-size drawings sets, and one print ready PDF on electronic media acceptable to the 
City of all deliverables. 
 
 
Task A – Optional Services 
Optional Task A1.1 – Plat & Legal Descriptions for New Easements (2) 
This optional task includes preparation of plat and legal descriptions for 2 new easements. 
414 of 662

--- page 415 ---
Attachment A, Scope of Services ‐ Sanitary Sewer Pump Station Rehabilitation Design Services 
Page 14 of 15 
 
 
 
 
Optional Task A1.2 – Topographic Surveying of Pothole Locations 
This optional task includes up to one day of topographic surveying during utility locating and 
potholing. 
Optional Task A1.3 – Property Appraisal Services for New Easement (1) 
This optional task includes property appraisal services for a new easement at one property. 
This scope was developed under the assumption that a new easement may be required on the 
north side of the Arroyo Court pump station (212 S El Camino Real). 
Optional Task A1.4 – Easement Negotiation/Acquisition Services (1) 
This optional task includes easement negotiation/acquisition services for one new easement. 
This scope includes: 
1. Preparation of acquisition documents 
2. Negotiate to acquire a permanent easement acquisition 
3. If settlement with owner is reached pursuant to the City approved appraisal or 
City approved administrative settlement, the Consultant will prepare a 
Memorandum of Settlement for transmittal to City 
4. Establish a process of coordinating escrow closings and reviewing escrow 
instructions. 
Optional Task A1.5 – Utility E-Locates (3 days) 
This optional task includes utility locating services using electromagnetic field induction and 
ground penetrating radar. This scope includes up to 3 days of utility locating services. 
Optional Task A1.6 – Utility Potholing (25 Utilities) 
This optional task includes exploratory excavations to locate key buried utilities or other structures 
that are expected to interfere with the work. Consultant will be responsible for selecting the 
potholing excavation method (i.e. backhoe pit versus vacuum excavation). The Consultant will be 
responsible for obtaining all permits and access agreements to perform the excavations and 
providing any necessary traffic control and pavement or other surface repairs. The Consultant will 
develop the utility pothole list, perform all excavations and repairs, and perform all field surveys 
to establish the locations of the buried features. This scope includes vacuum excavation of up to 
25 separate utilities/structures. 
Optional Task A1.7 – Initial Study/Mitigated Negative Declaration 
If the City, in its role as CEQA lead agency, determines that the Project is unable to qualify for a 
categorical exemption, the Consultant will prepare an IS/MND. This document will consist of the 
project description, the Initial Study checklist, and an analysis of the Project with respect to each 
of the questions in Appendix G of the CEQA Guidelines. Following City review of the 
administrative draft, the final draft IS/MND will be published for a public review period of at least 
20 days (30 days if the MND is submitted to the State Clearinghouse). The Consultant will assist 
with preparing responses to any public comments and making any necessary revisions to the 
draft IS/MND, resulting in the final IS/MND. If possible, the MND will be tiered off the 2016 PEIR 
for the Clean Water Program. A Notice of Determination (NOD) will be filed with the State 
Clearinghouse following final Project approval by the City.

--- page 416 ---
Attachment A, Scope of Services ‐ Sanitary Sewer Pump Station Rehabilitation Design Services 
Page 15 of 15 
 
 
 
 
Optional Task A1.8 – Water Main Relocation at Kehoe/16th Avenue Channel 
This optional task includes the coordination and design to relocate the existing water main on 
the north side of the 16th Avenue channel culverts. This task may be necessary if it is 
determined that the new force main cannot be installed within the roadway at the channel 
crossing (between box culvert and street surface). 
 
 
SCOPE ASSUMPTIONS 
This scope of work was developed under the following assumptions: 
1. The design of all lift stations, force mains, and gravity sewer mains will be prepared 
under one set of construction documents. 
2. CCTV video inspection of existing gravity sewer mains will be provided by the City, if 
deemed necessary. CCTV video inspection can be provided by the Consultant for an 
additional scope and fee if requested. 
3. The wetwell/drywell at all lift stations will be abandoned in place and lift stations to be 
replaced will receive new precast concrete wetwells. 
4. SCADA systems will be based on Motorola ACE3600 and will be coordinated with the 
City’s preferred SCADA consultants. 
5. Cathodic Protection Assumptions: 
a. The need for cathodic protection is not anticipated for the proposed 
improvements. 
b. Design of cathodic protection is not included within this scope; however, it can be 
provided for an additional fee if necessary.

File ID: 22-5868
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Drew Corbett, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Library Department
MEETING DATE:
March 21, 2022 
SUBJECT:
City Library Fines – Late Fee Elimination 
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve the elimination of late fines at all San Mateo public libraries. 
BACKGROUND:
After observing no adverse effects from not charging late fines during the pandemic, the Library is now ready to 
permanently eliminate them. After a thorough review of the matter, the SMPL Library Board of Trustees voted 4-1 at the 
July 26, 2021 meeting in support of fine elimination. Fine elimination was reviewed by the Council at the January 3, 2022 
special meeting. The relatively small amount collected no longer justifies the time and labor involved in charging late fines. 
Eliminating late fines will also help ease the financial burden on some library patrons and align us with our neighboring 
library systems, the overwhelming majority of whom have already ended the practicing of charging late fines. Fees will still 
be charged for unreturned library materials. Patrons will have short grace period and receive a reminder before being 
billed for unreturned items.
BUDGET IMPACT:
Because late fines have not been charged during the pandemic, the current operational budget and long-term financial 
plan already account for the loss of fine revenue.  There are no additional budgetary impacts of this action.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This item is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity that will not result in 
direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.
ATTACHMENTS
None
STAFF CONTACT
James Moore, City Librarian
jmoore@cityofsanmateo.org
(650) 522-7802

SUBJECT:
Service Employees International Union Non-Merit Part Time Unit – Memorandum of Understanding Extension
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to extend the Memorandum of Understanding with the Service Employees International Union (SEIU) 
Non-Merit Part Time Unit through June 10, 2023.
BACKGROUND:
This bargaining unit represents approximately 350 per diem workers throughout the City who work in excess of 416 hours 
per fiscal year. The current MOU expired on September 4, 2021. Negotiations with SEIU Local 521 were delayed due to a 
change in their local representative and COVID-19. 
The City’s Human Resources Department recommends extending this contract for another 21 months, from September 4, 
2021 through June 10, 2023. SEIU Local 521 has agreed to this extension, which will allow the parties time to negotiate a 
successor contract with the SEIU Maintenance Unit and assess the impact those negotiated changes may have on the SEIU 
Non-Merit Part Time Unit.
BUDGET IMPACT:
There will be no budget impact as a result of the SEIU Non-Merit Part Time Unit contract extension.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This item is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is a government fiscal activity which does not involve any 
commitment to any specific project which may result in a potentially significant physical impact on the environment. 
(CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

SUBJECT:
San Mateo Management Employees Association - Memorandum of Understanding Amendment
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to amend the Memorandum of Understanding with the San Mateo Management Employees 
Association to modify the salary increases.
BACKGROUND:
The San Mateo Management Employees Association (SMMEA) represents approximately 100 employees in management 
and supervisory positions throughout the City. 
Prior to the pandemic, the City’s General Fund Long-Term Financial Plan assumed annual employee salary increases of two 
percent (2%). Due to the immediate and severe impact to transient occupancy tax and sales tax revenues caused by the 
COVID-19 pandemic, the City chose to tap into the Rainy-Day Reserve to prevent layoffs and conduct concession 
bargaining. SMMEA was the first bargaining unit to agree to concessions that were equivalent to zero percent (0%) salary 
increases in fiscal years 2022 and 2023 to support the Financial Stability Plan. This agreement was reached on September 
2, 2020. The Memorandum of Understanding included a reopener clause that if the General Fund actual revenues for FY 
2021-22 and FY 2022-23 were greater than or equal to $141.4M, the City would reopen negotiations for potential 
compensation increases. 
The Adopted 2021-22 Budget assumed zero percent (0%) salary increases for members of SMMEA. The mid-year budget 
update presented to the City Council on January 18, 2022, projected increased General Fund revenues of $10.7 million and 
decreased expenditures of $2.8 million with projected revenues of $155.2M. This increase triggered the reopener clause 
and provides funds to support salary increases. 
Negotiations with the San Mateo Management Employees Association concluded on February 9, 2022, and the 
membership subsequently ratified the proposed changes to its Memorandum of Understanding. Consistent with the 
direction provided by the City Council, negotiators for the City and the bargaining unit have reached agreement. These 
agreements include: 
Salary: The bargaining unit will receive a 4.0% salary increase effective April 3, 2022, and a 4.0% increase effective April 2, 
2023. 
Deferred Compensation: The City’s contribution will return to 1.5% on April 3, 2022.
BUDGET IMPACT:
Approval of the compensation resolution results in an increase of $237,493 in total compensation for SMMEA members 
425 of 662

--- page 426 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
for the remainder of the fiscal year. It is projected that there are sufficient budget appropriations in the 2021-22 operating 
budget to absorb the additional cost. Updated salary and benefit amounts for these positions will be incorporated into the 
2022-23 budget.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This item is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is a government fiscal activity which does not involve any 
commitment to any specific project which may result in a potentially significant physical impact on the environment. 
(CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

LETTER OF UNDERSTANDING 
Between the San Mateo Management Employees’ Association and the 
City of San Mateo 
7.4  Compensation Increases During the Term of this Agreement 
Effective April 5, 2020, the Unit will receive an across the board increase of 2.5%. Effective April 
4, 2021, the Unit will receive an across the board increase of 2.0%. Effective April 3, 2022, the 
Unit will receive an across the board increase of 14.0%. Effective April 2, 2023, the Unit will 
receive an across the board increase of 14.0%. Effective March 31, 2024, the Unit will receive an 
across the board increase of 1.0%.  
In the event that the City’s General Fund actual revenues for fiscal years 2021-22 and 2022-23 are 
greater than or equal to $141.4M and $144.6M, respectively, the City will reopen negotiations 
with the Association for potential compensation increases. The City’s ability to potentially provide 
additional compensation will also factor in expenditures, particularly those related to pension and 
healthcare costs, that are greater than expected. 
25.4 
Deferred Compensation for Members of the Bargaining Unit 
Employees are eligible to participate in the City-offered deferred compensation 457 plans. The 
City shall pay biweekly contributions of an amount equal to 1.5% of the employee’s base salary 
into his/her deferred compensation 457 plan for all Association members. Effective June 27, 2021, 
the City’s contribution shall be 0.50%. Effective June 25, 2023, the City’s contribution shall be 
0.75%. The City’s contribution shall return to 1.5% on June 23April 3, 20242022. 
Effective May 7, 2017, on a biweekly basis the City shall match up to an additional 1.0% of an 
employee’s contribution into his/her deferred compensation 457 plan for all Association members. 
Effective with the City’s change to the calculation of the FLSA overtime rate to exclude ‘pool 
money’ (the City contribution to the cost of medical insurance premiums) from the calculation, for 
employees in non-exempt classifications only hired on or before the effective date of the change, 
the City shall make bi-weekly contributions of an amount equal to zero point nine percent (0.9%) 
of the employee’s base salary into his/her deferred compensation 457 plan in recognition that ‘pool 
money’ will no longer be included in calculation of the FLSA overtime rate. This contribution 
ceases when the employee leaves the bargaining unit or moves into an exempt classification.

SUBJECT:
San Mateo Police Officers’ Association – Memorandum of Understanding Amendment
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to amend the Memorandum of Understanding with the City of San Mateo Police Officers’ Association 
for a term through January 6, 2024.
BACKGROUND:
The City of San Mateo Police Officers’ Association (hereafter “Association”) represents 109 employees. 
The contract with this bargaining unit expired on June 30, 2021. Negotiations with the Association concluded on February 
25, 2022, and the membership subsequently ratified the proposed changes to its Memorandum of Understanding. 
Consistent with the direction provided by the City Council, negotiators for the City and the bargaining unit have reached 
agreement. These agreements include: 
Term: The contract will be for a period of two and one-half years, expiring on January 6, 2024. 
Salary: The bargaining unit will receive a 4.0% salary increase effective April 3, 2022, and a 4.0% increase effective July 9, 
2023. 
Retirement Health Savings (RHS) Contributions: The first full pay period upon Council approval the City will contribute $500 
per employee per month to the PORAC Retiree Medical Trust (The Trust), which replaces the City contribution to the 
existing 401(h) and the RHS account. 
Training Differential: Effective April 3, 2022, the City will increase the differential from 5.0% to 8.0% for non-Senior 
Dispatchers when assigned to serve as a member of the Training Team. 
Other: There are language changes relating to departmental operations and housekeeping language that amend some of 
the terms and conditions of the contract.
BUDGET IMPACT:
A supplemental budget appropriation of available fund balance in the General Fund in the amount of $233,777 for the 
Police Department’s 2021-22 operating budget is required to accommodate the negotiated increases.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This item is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is a government fiscal activity which does not involve any 
429 of 662

(CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)

WHEREAS, the amendments to the Memorandum of Understanding Between the City of San Mateo and the 
San Mateo Police Officers Association (Unit) are related to salary, term, other compensation and miscellaneous 
language changes relating to departmental operations, and language clarity for the Unit; and  
 
 
WHEREAS, the parties have met and conferred, and have reached tentative agreement to amend the Unit’s 
Memorandum of Understanding (MOU); and 
 
 
WHEREAS, the Unit has ratified the amendments to the MOU (Tentative Agreement); and 
 
 
WHEREAS, an appropriation of additional funds to the Fiscal Year 2021-22 Budget is necessary to finance 
these increases; and 
 
 
WHEREAS, the MOU is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is a government fiscal activity which 
does not involve any commitment to any specific project which may result in a potentially significant physical 
impact on the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).) 
 
 
NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, HEREBY RESOLVES 
that: 
 
1. The amendments to the Memorandum of Understanding for the City of San Mateo Police Officers 
Association Unit, set forth in the Tentative Agreement attached as Exhibit A, are approved and 
summarized as follows: 
 
Term:  The contract will be for a period of two and one half (2.5) expiring on January 6, 2024. 
Salary:  The bargaining unit will receive a 4.0% salary increase effective April 3, 2022, and a 4.0% 
increase effective July 9, 2023.  
Retirement Health Savings (RHS) Contributions: The first full pay period upon Council approval the 
City will contribute $500 per employee per month to the PORAC Retiree Medical Trust (The Trust), 
which replaces the City contribution to the existing 401(h) and the RHS account. 
Training Differential: Effective April 3, 2022, the City will increase the differential from 5.0% to 8.0% 
for non-Senior Dispatchers when assigned to serve as a member of the Training Team. 
Other:  There are language changes relating to departmental operations and housekeeping language 
that amend some of the terms and conditions of the contract. 
 
2. A supplemental appropriation of $233,777 into the Fiscal Year 2021-22 Budget for the updated 
salary and benefits is authorized to finance these increases.

Article 34 – Salaries
34.1
Compensation Increases During the Term of this Agreement
Effective the pay period of October 21April 3, 2018 2022 all represented classifications 
will receive an across the board increase of two four percent (24%). Furthermore, in 
recognition of the need to recruit and retain highly qualified personnel, the Police Officer 
classification shall receive an additional one percent (1%) increase.
Effective June July 309, 2019 2023 all represented classifications will receive an across 
the board increase of two four percent (24%).  Furthermore, in recognition of the need to 
recruit and retain highly qualified personnel, the Police Officer classification shall receive 
an additional one percent (1%) increase.
Effective June 28, 2020 all represented classifications will receive an across the board 
increase of two percent (2%).  Furthermore, in recognition of the need to recruit and retain 
highly qualified personnel, the Police Officer classification shall receive an additional one 
percent (1%) increase.
The salary of a Police Officer Trainee will remain fixed at 85% of step one of Police 
Officers.

2022, in order for the above provisions to occur.

4.5.2 Court Appearance. 
 
F. 
Employees who obtain a "standby relationship" with court officials will not be 
entitled to court overtime pay for hours spent away from the court awaiting call or 
recall to the court.  They shall be paid a minimum hour flat 1.251.5 hours at the

described in this MOU. Employees must report within 90 minutes of being called 
to physically appear in court.   If the employee is called to court during the standby 
period, the employee will be paid at the established standby rate for actual time 
spent on stand-by.  In addition, tIf required to appear, the employee will be paid 
Court Overtime for the appearance as established in this article.

Article 25 - Master Officer 
 
The purpose of the Master Office Program is to recognize those officers who have distinguished 
themselves at the highest level of professionalism through specialized skills and who exhibit the 
highest degree of productivity and leadership by utilizing their specialized skills in their day-to-
day activities in accordance with SMPD Policy. 
 
 
 
Qualification Requirements: 
 
 
A. 
Master Officer I 
• Eligible after seven (7) years of San Mateo Police Department service as a 
Police Officer. 
• Possession of an Advanced POST Certificate. 
• Accumulation of two (2) “points” of specialty assignments. 
• Receives/Maintains an “exceeds standards” yearly evaluation. 
• Paid a 3.0% differential. 
 
B. 
Master Officer II 
• Eligible after fourteen (14) years of San Mateo Police Department service as a 
Police Officer. 
• Possession of an Advanced POST Certificate. 
• Accumulation of three (3) “points” of specialty assignments. 
• Receives/Maintains an “exceeds standards” yearly evaluation. 
• Paid an additional 2.0% differential, for a total of 5% differential. 
 
C. 
Master Officer III 
• Eligible after twenty-one (21) years of San Mateo Police Department service 
as a Police Officer. 
• Possession of an Advanced POST Certificate. 
• Accumulation of four (4) “points” of specialty assignments. 
• Receives/Maintains an “exceeds standards” yearly evaluation. 
• Paid an additional 2.0% differential, for a total of 7% differential. 
 
All earned Master Officer Differentials will be paid in addition to any other earned 
differentials. 
 
 
Date:  ___________________________

27.6 
Vacation Scheduling 
 
The time at which employees may take their vacation shall be determined by the 
Department Head, with due regard for the wishes of the employee and the needs of the 
service. However, the City recognizes that premium vacation time is a sought after 
commodity, and therefore agrees that to the extent possible, vacation shall be allowed for 
at least two (2) police officers at a time form each of the four (4) shifts.

15.4 
Removal of Documents from Personnel File 
 
Only records related to Internal Affairs Investigations resulting in a “sustained” finding 
will be kept in an employee’s Personnel File. Records related to Internal Investigations 
resulting in “Not Sustained”, “Exonerated”, or “Unfounded” findings shall not be placed 
in the employee’s Personnel File, but will be separately maintained for the appropriate 
retention period in the employee’s Internal Affairs File. 
 
After a five (5) year period, if there has not been subsequent discipline, the City shall 
remove from the employee’s Internal Affairs File, any written record of an Internal Affairs 
Investigation resulting in “Exonerated” or “Unfounded” findings upon employee written 
request to the Chief of Police. The City shall notify the employee when the record has been 
removed. 
 
After a five (5) year period, if there has not been subsequent discipline, an employee may 
petition the City to remove from the employee’s Internal Affairs File any record of a “Not 
Sustained” finding, or from the employee’s Personnel File and any record of a “Sustained” 
finding except those sustained findings for allegations of dishonesty, moral turpitude, use 
of excessive force, or intentional destruction of private property. Any such request shall be 
submitted to the Chief of Police. The City shall notify the employee of its decision, whether 
or not to remove the record. 
 
After a five (5) year period, if the employee has not been involved in any subsequent “Party 
Most at Fault” or “Avoidable” (“Avoidable” for purpose of administrative review or 
progressive discipline) traffic collisions, the city shall remove from the employee’s 
personnel file, or any other file used by the employer for any personnel purpose, any written 
record of prior traffic collisions upon employee written request to the Chief of Police. The 
City shall notify the employee that the written record has been removed.

Article 26 – Holidays 
 
26.1 
Holidays 
 
The holidays to be observed are as follows: 
 
 
A. 
New Year's Day 
 
 
 
January 1st. 
 
B. 
Martin Luther King's Birthday 
 
3rd Monday in January 
 
C. 
Presidents’ Day 
 
 
 
3rd Monday in February 
 
D. 
Memorial Day  
 
 
 
Last Monday in May 
 
E. 
Independence Day 
 
 
 
July 4th 
 
F. 
Labor Day 
 
 
 
 
1st Monday in September 
 
G. 
Veteran's Day  
 
 
 
November 11 
 
H. 
Thanksgiving Day 
 
 
 
4th Thursday in November 
 
I. 
Day after Thanksgiving 
 
 
4th Friday in November 
 
J. 
Christmas Day 
 
 
 
December 25th 
K. 
Every day approved by the City Council as a public holiday, public fast, 
thanksgiving or day of mourning. 
L. 
Floating holidays are to be taken on working days mutually agreeable to the 
employee and the department.  CSO’s and sworn safety personnelEmployees 
receive three (3) floating holidays annually. 
 
26.2 
Weekend Holidays 
 
When a holiday falls on Sunday, the following Monday shall be observed. Employees who 
are not assigned a special holiday schedule shall be compensated for established holidays 
that fall on a Saturday by permitting approximately one-half (1/2) of the employees to 
celebrate the holiday on the Friday immediately preceding the Saturday holiday and the 
balance of the employees to celebrate the holiday on the following Monday.  The 
employees shall receive seven calendar days’ notice of the holiday schedule.  City offices 
shall remain open on both days.  If it is inconvenient to grant either day as a holiday, the 
equivalent time off shall be granted under the conditions provided in Section 27.5. 
 
26.3 
Special Holiday Schedule 
 
Employees who are assigned a special holiday schedule because they cannot take holidays 
when they fall shall be compensated for holidays as follows: 
 
A. 
Such employees shall be compensated for thirteen (13) holidays a year by the 
granting of 4.0 hours of holiday credit per pay period.  Such time off shall be taken 
off under the conditions specified in Section 27.5. This holiday credit is not reported 
DocuSign Envelope ID: F871D53F-7B99-4493-B6F5-319DEDB51B43
439 of 662

--- page 440 ---
to CalPERS as compensation. 
 
B. 
Such employees shall be compensated for special holidays declared by competent 
authority by the granting of equivalent time off under the conditions specified in 
Section 27.5. 
 
26.4 
Authorized Holidays 
 
Other merit system employees shall be compensated for holidays authorized or those 
declared by competent authority by the granting of equivalent time off under the conditions 
specified in Section 26.5. 
 
26.5 
Holidays Worked 
 
 
If employees are required by the City to work on any of the above holidays, they shall be 
granted equivalent time off for holiday compensation under the following conditions: 
 
A. 
Such time off shall be taken at the discretion of the Department Head, with due 
regard for the desires of the employee and the workload of the department. 
 
B. 
Such equivalent time off shall never accumulate to more than forty (40) hours. 
 
 
In the event one (1) or more holidays fall within an annual vacation leave of an employee 
who receives holiday time off, such holidays shall not be charged as vacation leave. 
 
26.6 
Holiday Payment 
 
Employees who have completed probation who have taken, or are scheduled to take, at 
least eighty (80) hours of vacation during a calendar year may, at their option, make an 
irrevocable election to sell back up to one hundred four (104) hours of accumulated 
holiday, vacation or annual leave or a combination thereof at the employee's established 
rate of pay plus all applicable differentials. 
 
Requests to sell back must be received by Payroll no later than December 31st for the 
following year elections. No employee can sell back more than their current annual 
vacation accrual in the calendar year. Elected hours not requested for distribution (sell 
back) by December 1st will be automatically distributed in the next pay period (sell back 
cannot be more than the hours available at time of distribution). 
 
Employees joining the bargaining unit with leave balances in excess of the maximum 
accumulation of leave hours will automatically sell back the number of hours required to 
meet the maximum accumulation allowed. 
 
26.7 
Holiday Allowance for Employees at Termination 
 
Employees who terminate shall be paid in a lump sum for all accrued holiday leave with

Article 4 – Overtime 
 
4.3 
Overtime Payment/Compensatory Time 
 
 
An employee who accrues compensatory time for overtime worked under the provisions 
of Section 54.1 above may waive direct payment of overtime and in lieu thereof may 
accumulate time off at the one and one-half (1-1/2) rate to a maximum of one hundred and 
thirty-two (132) hours. 
 
 
 
Article 12 – Leave of Absence Without Pay 
 
12.1 
Family Care Leave 
 
 
In accordance with the Federal Family and Medical Leave Act effective August 5, 1993, 
and the California Family Rights Act effective October 4, 1993, the City will grant job 
protected unpaid family and medical leave to eligible workers for up to twelve (12) weeks 
per twelve (12) month period for any or more of the following reasons:. 
 
A. 
The birth of a child and in order to care for such child or the placement of a child 
with the worker for adoption or foster care (leave for this reason must be taken 
within the twelve (12) month period following the child's birth or placement with 
the employee); or 
 
B. 
In order to care for an immediate family member (spouse, child, domestic partner 
or parent) of the worker if such immediate family member has a serious health 
condition.  For purposes of this section, "child" is anyone under eighteen (18) years 
who is the worker's biological, adopted, or foster child, stepchild, legal ward, or an 
adult dependent child.  "Parents" include biological, foster, and adoptive parents, 
stepparents, and legal guardians; or 
 
C. 
The worker's own serious health condition that makes the worker unable to perform 
the functions of their position. 
 
In general, Family Medical Leave is available to workers for:  the birth or adoption of a 
child or placement of a foster child; care for an immediate family member of the worker if 
such immediate family member has a serious health condition; or the worker’s own serious 
health condition that makes the worker unable to perform the functions of their position. 
 
The City will shall adhere to applicable Federal and State laws regarding Family and 
Medical Leave.  For specific conditions governing Family Medical Leave, please refer to 
DocuSign Envelope ID: 4C91BB1F-EAFF-4E5F-8736-368456276225
442 of 662

--- page 443 ---
the City Policy. 
 
The following provisions apply to Family Care Leave: 
 
A. 
Eligible worker means having been employed for the City for twelve (12) months 
and having worked at least one thousand two hundred fifty (1,250) hours during the 
twelve (12) month period immediately preceding the commencement of the leave. 
 
B. 
Medical verification is required for worker or ill family member for the medical 
leave period. 
 
C. 
Workers are required to give at least thirty (30) days written notice in the event of 
a foreseeable leave.  In unexpected or unforeseeable situations, a worker should 
provide as much written notice as is practicable. 
 
D. 
Workers may use accrued vacation as part of the family leave period.  Use of sick 
leave is not required but may be used under the same regulations governing the use 
of sick leave.  Use of sick leave does not extend the maximum four (4) month period 
of family care leavePregnancy Disability Leave Act. 
 
E. 
Should both parents work for the City, each may be granted up to twelve (12) weeks 
leave. 
 
F. 
Maternity leave under state law is accounted for separately.  Pursuant to State Law, 
an employee is eligible for up to four (4) months leave. 
 
G. 
Health benefit premiums shall be made in accordance with the Federal Family and 
Medical Leave Act and the California Family Rights Act.  Under the current law, 
the City will continue health coverage under the same conditions as coverage would 
have been provided if the worker had been continuously employed during the leave 
period. 
 
H. 
Workers taking family care leave will be returned to the same or comparable job 
with the same pay at the conclusion of their leave. 
 
I. 
Family care leave will not constitute a break in service for purposes of any 
employee benefit plan.  Employees returning from leave shall return with no less 
seniority than accumulated on the date leave began. 
 
J. 
Where seniority is used for purposes of vacation and/or shift bidding, family care 
leave shall not result in a loss of department seniority.

Article 15 - Disciplinary Procedure 
 
15.1 
Definition of "Disciplinary Action." 
 
The term "disciplinary action" shall include the following actions which may be taken by 
a Department Head: 
 
A. 
Letter of reprimand issued to Police Officers, provided that no pre-disciplinary 
(Skelly) proceedings are required; 
 
B. 
Suspension without pay up to thirty (30) calendar days; 
 
C. 
Withholding in-grade salary increase (merit pay) under Article 4 shall not be 
considered discipline and not subject to the principles of Article 14; 
 
D. 
In-grade salary reduction; 
 
 
E. 
Demotion; or 
 
 
F. 
Dismissal. 
 
 
Article 24 – Duty Differentials 
 
24.1 
Bomb UnitHazard Pay 
 
 
 
The Chief may establish a bomb unit.  Officer(s) assigned this hazardous detail shall 
receive five percent (5%) biweekly in addition to their regular pay. This is Special 
Compensation paid to employees who are routinely and consistently exposed to toxic, 
radioactive, explosive, or other hazardous substance or perform hazardous activities to 
implement health or safety procedures. 
 
24.2 
Standby Pay 
 
 
Officers placed on standby alert by the Police Chief or their designee shall receive extra 
compensation at the rate of twelve and one-half percent (12 1/2%) of their hourly rate. 
 
 
For purposes of this section, standby alert shall include "on call" status by detectives or 
other Officers designated by the Chief of Police or their designee. 
 
24.3 
Canine DifferentialCare 
 
 
Officers assigned canine responsibilities will receive two (2) hours credit which will accrue 
at the level of three hours (to comply with FLSA) leave per work week, in recognition of 
the time spent in the care, grooming, feeding, and activities related to canine handling. 
 
DocuSign Envelope ID: 4C91BB1F-EAFF-4E5F-8736-368456276225
444 of 662

--- page 445 ---
24.4 
Other Duty Differentials (Special Compensation) 
 
Officers assigned to the following special assignments Traffic Detail, Canine, PAL/Youth 
Services, Downtown Unit, Training ManagerCoordinator, Crime Reduction Unit, and 
Press Information Officer and School Resource assignments shall receive 5% biweekly in 
addition to their regular pay. 
 
Motorcycle Patrol Premium - This is Special Compensation paid to police officers who are 
routinely and consistently assigned to operate and/or patrol on motorcycle. 
 
Canine Officer/Animal Premium - This is Special Compensation paid to police officers 
who are routinely and consistently assigned to handle, train, and board a canine or horse. 
Compensation shall not include veterinarian fees, feed, or other reimbursable expenses for 
upkeep of the animal. 
 
Juvenile Officer Premium - This is Special Compensation paid to police officers in 
PAL/Youth Services who are routinely and consistently assigned to enforce laws that 
restrict the activities of juveniles. 
 
Police Investigator Premium - This is Special Compensation paid to police officers in the 
Downtown   Unit   who   are routinely   and consistently   assigned to analyze   crimes or 
investigate accidents. 
 
Gang Detail Assignment Premium - This is Special Compensation to police officers in the 
Crime Reduction Unit (CRU) who are routinely and consistently assigned to enforce laws 
relating to a group of individuals banded together for unlawful activities. 
 
D.A.R.E. Premium - This is Special Compensation to School Resource police officers who 
routinely and consistently provide training to students on drug abuse resistance. 
 
Detective Division Premium - This is Special Compensation to police officers who are 
routinely and consistently assigned to a detective or investigative division or intelligence 
duties in the Investigative Services Division or when assigned full-time in an outside Task 
Force. 
 
A five percent (5%) work differential will be paid to those Detectives in the Investigative 
Services Division, to include when assigned full-time to an outside Task Force. 
 
24.5 
Training DifferentialPremium 
 
This is Special Compensation paid to employees who are routinely and consistently 
assigned to train employees. 
 
 
Police Officers assigned as field training officers shall receive eight percent (8%) biweekly 
in addition to their regular pay for such duty and associated overtime. 
 
DocuSign Envelope ID: 4C91BB1F-EAFF-4E5F-8736-368456276225
445 of 662

--- page 446 ---
Community Service Officers - in non-specialty assignments who are assigned training of 
field CSO’s - shall receive eight percent (8%) biweekly in addition to their regular pay for 
such duty and associated overtime. 
 
When the manager/sergeant supervisor responsible for the communications center assigns 
a non-Senior Dispatcher to serve as a member of the Training Team, the employee will 
receive a 5% differential while training entry-level probationary Dispatchers relative to the 
technical dispatch duties. Dispatchers who are not part of the Training Team are not eligible 
to receive the differential. 
 
24.6 
Bilingual Differential (Special Compensation) 
 
 
Based on the City and community need and the language proficiency of the employee, the 
Human Resources Director may approve bilingual pay for Police Officers’ Association 
classifications upon the recommendation of the Department Head.  Approval will only be 
given where the employee’s bilingual skill is regularly used in the course of City business. 
 
Bilingual premium pay shall be $181.96 biweekly for eligible Police Officers, $125.44 for 
eligible Community Service Officers and $90.00 biweekly for eligible Senior 
Dispatchers/Dispatchers, and shall become payable the first day of the pay period following 
proficiency certification. Effective October 11, 2015, bilingual premium pay shall be 
increased to $200.00 biweekly for eligible Police Officers and $138.46 biweekly for 
eligible Community Service Officers, Senior Dispatchers and Dispatchers. If a bilingual 
qualified employee works less than full-time, the biweekly differential will be prorated.  If 
an employee is off work for over 30 consecutive calendar days, the employee will not 
receive the bilingual differential for the remainder of the leave; this will not take effect if 
the employee is out on 4850 disability leave. 
 
In order to ensure that employees retain bilingual proficiency, a periodic requalification 
may be required and will be administered by the Human Resources Department. 
 
24.7 
Community Service Officer – Differential 
 
A. 
A Community Service Officer serving in one of the positions designated in this 
subsection shall receive an eight percent (8%) increment/differential above his or 
her base salary for the regular position of Community Service Officer.  This 
increment/differential will be on base salary which does not include any other 
differential such as bilingual pay, etc. 
 
B. 
The Chief of Police shall have exclusive authority to appoint or to remove at their 
pleasure Community Service Officers assigned to the following areas:  
Investigations; Court Liaison; and Traffic; and Support Services. 
 
The Community Service Officer assigned to Traffic is routinely and 
consistently assigned to read parking meters and cite drivers who have 
DocuSign Envelope ID: 4C91BB1F-EAFF-4E5F-8736-368456276225
446 of 662

--- page 447 ---
violated parking laws; this differential is reported as special compensation 
(Parking Citation Premium). 
 
The Community Service Officer assigned as the Court Liaison is routinely and 
consistently assigned to handle police records a n d  case files, including 
h a n d l i n g  records requests from the District Attorney’s office; this differential 
is reported as special compensation (Police Records Assignment P r e m i u m ). 
 
The Community Service Officer assigned to Support Services in the Police 
Activities League (PAL) is routinely and consistently assigned to a lead or 
supervisory   position   over   other   employees, agency-sponsored   participants, 
or volunteers; the PAL/Support Services differential is reported as special 
compensation (Lead Worker/Supervisor P r e m i u m ). 
 
C. 
Neither appointment to nor removal from such assignments shall be considered to 
be a grievance or discipline and no appeal may be taken from the Chief’s decision. 
 
D. 
Up to six (6) positions may be assigned by the Chief of Police.  The Chief shall not 
be obligated to fill all six (6) positions. 
 
24.8 
Shift Differential (Special Compensation) 
 
Employees in the classifications of Dispatcher I/II and Senior Dispatcher who work the 
“Overlap”, “Swing Shift” or “Midnights” shall be compensated by the payment of an 
additional five percent (5%) of their base pay for that shift.  Shift differential does not apply 
to overtime hours worked, however, employees working one of the designated shifts as 
part of a shift trade shall receive the shift differential. 
 
 
Article 27 - Annual Leave Program

vacation with pay on the day following completion of one (1) year of continuous 
service.  Thereafter, for each additional biweekly pay period of service, the 
employee shall accrue vacation with pay at the beginning of the following biweekly 
pay period as 
 
 
Article 28 – Sick Leave 
 
28.4 
Protected Sick Leave 
 
 
Protected sick leave may be taken, and if taken, shall be charged to sick leave or, with the 
concurrence of the department head, to vacation, holiday, or compensatory time off (CTO) 
DocuSign Envelope ID: 4C91BB1F-EAFF-4E5F-8736-368456276225
447 of 662

--- page 448 ---
leave, for the following reasons: 
 
• The diagnosis, care, or treatment of an existing health condition of, or 
preventative care for, an employee. 
• The diagnosis, care, or treatment of an existing health condition of, or 
preventative care for, an employee’s family member, as defined in California 
Labor Code 245.5(c). 
• 
An employee who is a victim of domestic violence, sexual assault, or stalking. 
 
Not more than seventy-two (72) hours of protected sick leave shall be granted in any one 
(1) year. However, if extenuating circumstances exist at the discretion of the Department 
Head and the Human Resources Director, a reasonable extension of the seventy-two (72) 
hour limit may be granted by the Department Head.  Any additional leave so granted shall 
be charged against the employee's accumulated sick leave. 
 
 
Article 29 - Annual Leave Program 
 
B.5) 
Sick Leave Balance Upon Conversion.  Any balance of sick leave hours shall be retained 
by the employee.  Sick leave hours shall be retained until used or paid in accordance to 
Memorandum of Understanding.  The maximum sick leave hours which can be converted 
for payment upon retirement under annual leave is one hundred fifty (150) days. 
 
B.12) Effective with the first full pay period in January, 1992, the limit for payment for unused 
accumulated sick leave for employees participating in the annual leave program upon 
service retirement shall be raised from one hundred twenty (120) days to one hundred fifty 
(150) days at fifty percent (50%) of the total number of days accumulated; i.e., a maximum 
of seventy-five (75) days. 
 
 
B.17) Protected Sick Leave. 
 
Protected sick leave may be taken and, if taken, shall be charged to annual leave for the 
following reasons: 
 
• The diagnosis, care, or treatment of an existing health condition of, or preventative care 
for, an employee. 
• The diagnosis, care, or treatment of an existing health condition of, or preventative care 
for, an employee’s family member, as defined in California Labor Code 245.5(c). 
• 
An employee who is a victim of domestic violence, sexual assault, or stalking. 
 
Not more than seventy-two (72) hours of such family protected sick leave shall be granted 
in any one year for the diagnosis, care, or treatment of an existing health condition of, or 
preventative care for, an employee’s family member.  However, if extenuating 
circumstances exist in the opinion of the Department Head, a reasonable extension of the 
seventy-two (72) hour limit may be granted.  Additional leave so granted shall be charged 
DocuSign Envelope ID: 4C91BB1F-EAFF-4E5F-8736-368456276225
448 of 662

--- page 449 ---
against the employee's accumulated sick or annual leave, at the employee's option.

4.7 
Patrol Officers' Alternative Scheduling Plan 
 
A. 
Definitions:  The following definitions shall apply to this Section only: 
 
1) 
"Work Cycle" shall mean a twenty-eight (28) day period, beginning on a 
day determined by the City.  This work cycle definition is intended to be 
consistent with definitions of a "work" period under FLSA. 
 
2) 
"Work Day" shall mean continuous period of on-the-job time in accordance 
with the Department’s schedule. 
 
B. 
Scheduling.  Officers assigned to duty in Patrol will be scheduled for work days of 
eleven (11) hours and twenty (20) minutes in length, and will work on a four-on, 
four-off cycle, with a scheduled short 8-hour day to stay within FLSA. Hours 
worked beyond eight (8) on the ‘short day’ are compensated as overtime at time 
and one-half. If off-work on the 8-hour day, eight (8) hours of leave are recorded 
and no overtime. Overtime is not reported to CalPERS as compensation.   The City 
will credit individual Officer accounts at the first of the year and debit at the end of 
each work cycle as follows: 
 
1) 
Any work hours worked in excess of one hundred sixty (160) working hours 
and less than one hundred seventy-one (171) working hours during a work 
cycle shall be paid for at time and one-half in accordance with the FLSA. 
 
2) 
Compensation for any work performed beyond the work day of eleven (11) 
hours and twenty (20) minutes will be paid overtime at the rate of time and 
one-half at the MOU Overtime Rate. 
 
3) 
Court appearance overtime will be compensated for as provided in Article 
4.5, except that pursuant to Article 4.3, the employee will determine the 
method of payment. 
 
C. 
The Police Chief may make changes to the scheduling plan up to and including 
terminate this programtermination at any time after meeting with the Officers' 
Association and providing written notice of schedule change to the Officers' 
Association.
