CITY OF SAN MATEO
RESOLUTION NO._____(2022)
ADOPTING SIDEWALK AND PEDESTRIAN MALL OUTDOOR DINING PERMIT PROGRAM AND 
APPROVING A REVISION TO THE COMPREHENSIVE FEE SCHEDULE FOR 2021-2022 TO RENAME THE 
APPLICABLE PERMIT FEE
WHEREAS, the City of San Mateo (the “City”) has established a pedestrian mall on B Street between 
1st and 3rd Avenues; and
WHEREAS, the City has an existing Outdoor Restaurant Seating and Merchandise Display 
Encroachment Permit Program (the “Program”) allowing for businesses to use City sidewalks for outdoor 
dining; and
WHEREAS, staff recommends expanding the Program to include the pedestrian mall (the “Expanded 
Program”); and
WHEREAS, the Expanded Program will allow for outdoor dining installations on the sidewalk and in 
the pedestrian mall; and 
WHEREAS, participation on the Expanded Program will require businesses to obtain an 
encroachment permit and to pay a permit fee; and
WHEREAS, the City wishes to change the name of the required permit fee from “Outdoor Restaurant 
Seating and Merchandise Display Permit” to “Sidewalk and Pedestrian Mall Outdoor Dining Permit” in the 
City’s Master Fee Schedule.
NOW, THEREFORE, THE COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, HEREBY RESOLVES that:
1.
The establishment of the Sidewalk and Pedestrian Mall Outdoor Dining Program is categorically 
exempt from CEQA under the category of "existing facility" because it consists of a minor 
alteration to the operation of an existing City Street involving negligible or no expansion of the 
former use. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15301).
2.
In accordance with California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) Guidelines section 
15378(b)(5), the amendment of the fee schedule to change the name of the fee is not a 
project subject to CEQA in that it is an administrative activity that will not impact the 
environment.
3.
The Sidewalk and Pedestrian Mall Outdoor Dining Permit Program attached as Exhibit A is 
approved.
4.
The name of the applicable fee is changed to “Sidewalk and Pedestrian Mall Outdoor Dining 
Permit” in the City’s Master Fee Schedule.
540 of 662

--- page 541 ---
5.
All permitted sidewalk and outdoor dining installations must adhere to the Sidewalk and 
Pedestrian Mall Outdoor Dining Permit Program.
6.
This resolution shall take effect immediately upon its adoption.
541 of 662

--- page 542 ---
Exhibit A
SIDEWALK AND PEDESTRIAN MALL 
OUTDOOR DINING PERMIT PROGRAM
Outdoor dining is a great way to add liveliness to City streets and support local business operations. To that 
end, the City has created programs allowing businesses to expand their operations with additional restaurant 
seating in the public right-of-way, which includes City sidewalks and streets. The City has created two distinct 
programs to provide food service businesses the opportunity to expand a portion of their operations outside 
directly in front of their business frontage:

Sidewalk and Pedestrian Mall Outdoor Dining Encroachment Permit – This permit allows ground floor 
food service businesses to provide outdoor seating and related furnishings in the following areas if the 
applicant meets certain City standards and conditions of use:
o
Commercial district areas on sidewalks (except when fronting El Camino Real).
o
The pedestrian mall on B Street between 1st and 3rd Avenue.

Parklet Encroachment Permit – This permit allows food service businesses to construct seating areas in 
on-street parking spaces directly in front of their business. Parklets are allowed in all commercial 
districts except along El Camino Real. Parklet Encroachment permits are not allowed inside the 
pedestrian mall (B Street between 1st and 3rd Avenue). If you are interested in expanding your business 
into the on-street parking area, please review the City’s Parklet Program Guidelines and Parklet 
Encroachment Permit Application. 
Sidewalk and Pedestrian Mall Outdoor Dining Encroachment Permits are not available to food service 
businesses located in private shopping centers or businesses along El Camino Real because these locations are 
not under the City’s control.  Terms and Conditions for such permits are listed below and may be updated by 
staff from time to time as appropriate.
Food service businesses interested in offering outdoor dining along a sidewalk or within the Pedestrian Mall 
must include all submittal requirements listed below, submit a Sidewalk and Pedestrian Mall Encroachment 
Permit Application and receive approval prior to placing any furnishings in the public right-of-way.  
For More Information. Questions or additional information regarding the Sidewalk and Pedestrian Mall 
Outdoor Dining and Parklet Programs, please contact Jennifer Chen, the City’s Economic Development 
Manager at (650) 533-7009.
542 of 662

--- page 543 ---
Sidewalk and Pedestrian Mall Outdoor Dining Encroachment Permit 
Application Submittal Requirements
The following documents and fees are required in order to process a permit for use of the sidewalk or 
pedestrian mall for outdoor dining:
1.
Sidewalk and Pedestrian Mall Encroachment Permit Application
2.
Site Plan Drawings and Photos of Business Frontage.  Site plan drawings may be drawn by hand 
and must include the following information (see sample drawings provided as Attachment 1):
o
Label the location of the business on diagram and storefront boundaries.
o
For sidewalk outdoor dining only: Show existing sidewalk obstructions (fire hydrants, trees, 
light poles, bike racks, utility boxes, manholes, newspaper racks, etc.) and dimensions 
between obstructions. 
o
For pedestrian mall outdoor dining only: show the location of the emergency access lane.
o
Show the layout of all proposed furnishings (planters, tables, chairs, umbrellas, heaters, 
fencing, etc.) 
o
Photographs of the business frontage
3.
Application Fee. The applicant is responsible to pay the annual permit fee prior to permit 
expiration. Fees are non-refundable.  The current fee amount ($371 as of July 1, 2021) is set forth 
in the City’s Comprehensive Fee Schedule. 
4.
Certificate of Liability Insurance. Applicants shall provide proof of commercial general liability (or 
comprehensive) and property damage insurance and endorsements showing the City of San 
Mateo as additional insured on the policy and stating that the insurance is primary. See attached 
insurance standards requirements. Encroachment Permits issued shall be valid only during the 
term of liability insurance coverage. Applicants are responsible to submit updated insurance 
documents prior to the expiration date and annual fee.  Failure to do so will result in revocation 
of the permit.
Please submit all documents electronically by sending an email to pwencroachment@cityofsanmateo.org or 
in person at the Public Works counter at San Mateo City Hall at 330 West 20th Avenue, San Mateo, CA 94403 
between 8am to 12pm and 1pm to 4:30pm.
543 of 662

--- page 544 ---
Sidewalk and Pedestrian Mall Outdoor Dining Encroachment Permit 
Terms and Conditions:
1. General Conditions of Use. Outdoor seating proposed on public sidewalks and within the pedestrian mall 
areas must meet the following terms and conditions: 

Comply with Chapter 17.08 and 27.87 of the San Mateo Municipal Code and other local, state 
and federal laws governing accessibility.

Adjacency to Storefront.  Outdoor seating must be located along the frontage of the storefront 
applying for the permit. Businesses will only be allowed to use a portion of the adjacent business 
owner’s frontage to expand their outdoor dining area if they obtain a letter from the current 
adjacent business owner stating that they have agreed to allow their frontage to be used. 
Permission to use the space in front of an adjacent business can be revoked by that adjacent 
business at any time.

Sidewalk Clearance. Outdoor seating areas must maintain a minimum sidewalk clearance and 
pedestrian through zone of 4-feet from any physical obstruction. This includes light poles, parking 
meters, fire hydrants, news racks, trees, entryways of adjacent businesses, or other barriers. 
Tables or seating may be placed near the curb if a minimum of 2-foot clearance to the curb is 
maintained. Tables or seating shall not be placed within the gutter.

ADA Accessibility.  All seating areas must conform to the applicable provisions, rules, regulations 
and guidelines of the California Building Code (Title 24, Chapter 11b) and Americans with Disabilities 
Act.

Outdoor Cooking Not Allowed. No outdoor cooking is allowed. Examples of types of foods that is 
considered outdoor cooking are hotpots, table-top barbeques, raclettes, fondues, and similar. In 
addition, outdoor restaurant busing stations are not allowed.

Live Music. No amplified lived music is allowed without a Special Community Events permit from 
the City of San Mateo.
2.  Furnishings. All outdoor furnishings (tables, chairs, umbrellas, planters, fencing, etc.) must be heavy enough 
to withstand high winds in order to protect guests and pedestrians. All furnishings may need to be stored inside 
the business from time to time to accommodate maintenance, cleaning, special events, or during periods of 
inclement or hazardous weather. Please ensure that weights used to anchor fixtures and furnishing do not 
create tripping hazards for pedestrians.

Bolting Not Allowed.  At no time shall any seating, tables, or umbrellas be bolted or affixed in any 
way to the public sidewalk, roadway, landscaping, or any structure (including but not limited to: 
buildings, fire hydrants, street trees, streetlights, parking meters, or traffic poles, etc.).

Fences/Railings/Stanchions. Businesses wanting to mark the boundary between the outdoor 
seating area and pedestrian path of travel are allowed only if the materials can easily be removed 
at the request of the City for special events and/or required maintenance. The height of these 
installations should not exceed 36 inches from the ground to the top of the railing, fencing or 
stanchions.

Umbrellas.  Umbrellas shall be fire-retardant, pressure-treated or manufactured of fire-resistant 
material. No portion of an umbrella shall be less than eighty (80) inches above the ground. 
544 of 662

--- page 545 ---

Canopies Not Allowed. Tents, canvas, roofs, shade structures are not allowed on the sidewalk or in 
the pedestrian malls.  

Portable Heaters. Space heaters are permitted if they are an outdoor approved type, are located 
in accordance with the manufacturer's recommendations, and are located at least 2 feet from the 
edge of any umbrella canvas, any tree foliage, and any other flammable object or material. Heaters 
are not allowed directly under umbrellas but can be placed near them if clearances are 
maintained.

Electrical connections.  All wiring and electrical cords must be exterior rated, GFCI protected, and 
UL listed. Cords must not create tripping hazards on the sidewalk and may not be extended above 
the City right-of-way. The use of generators are prohibited. Businesses are not allowed to tap into 
existing City electrical connections such as twinkle light outlets or streetlight poles.  Utilization of 
existing City electrical connections shall be cause of termination of the permit.
3. Site Maintenance. Outdoor seating areas shall be maintained free of litter, refuse and debris. The area shall 
be scrubbed and mopped to remove any food or drink stains on a daily basis. Such cleaning shall be in 
accordance with the City’s Storm Water Management and Discharge Control Program, which prohibits any 
discharge other than storm water into the storm water drainage system. Failure to maintain the site or 
complaints received by the City related to lack of cleaning or upkeep shall be cause of termination of the 
permit.
4. Notice for Removal.  The City and/or utility companies may need to access the sidewalk or pedestrian mall 
area for maintenance, repairs, events, and/or other purposes. While the City and/or utility companies will try 
to give a 72-hour advance notice, it may not be possible when dealing with emergency repairs. Additionally, 
the City will not cover business losses related to the loss of seating or business interruptions due to 
construction.
6. Transfer. Applicant may only use the dining area for their business. Applicant may not lease/rent or receive 
funds for the seating area to another operator.
7. Term. The permit is valid for a maximum of one year.  Applicant must renew permits annually and pay for 
permit fees and update insurance information.
8. Certificate of Liability Insurance. Applicants must provide proof of commercial general liability (or 
comprehensive) and property damage insurance including endorsements showing the City of San Mateo as an 
additional insured on the policy and stating that the insurance is primary with regard to the City of San Mateo.  
The detailed insurance standards are provided in Attachment 2 to these Terms and Conditions. It is the 
applicant’s responsibility to update insurance prior to permit expiration. Failure to do so will result in revocation 
of the encroachment permit.
9. Termination. The City reserves the right to terminate the permit without cause. In addition, the permit may 
be revoked due to failure to comply with the terms and conditions of the permit.
10. Additional Provisions for Outdoor Seating Located on the Pedestrian Mall:
Businesses located along the pedestrian mall on B Street between 1st and 3rd Avenues will be required to meet 
these additional conditions of use. 

No Permanent Structures Allowed. Businesses located in the pedestrian mall are not allowed to build 
545 of 662

--- page 546 ---
any permanent structures. However, platforms will be allowed, provided that they can be designed to 
be easily removed upon 72 hours advance notice and stored off-street to allow for maintenance and 
City-sponsored events. Platforms must meet the following design criteria:
o
Materials - Platforms must be constructed from durable materials that can withstand wear and tear 
of elements. Pouring concrete for parklet platforms is not allowed. You may use concrete pavers on 
a platform structure instead. Surface materials must be textured or treated with a non-skid coating 
to ensure a safe walking surface. Loose particles, such as sand or loose stone, are not permitted.
o
Platform Threshold - The platform threshold must be flush and even with sidewalk and must not 
leave a gap greater than 1/2 inch, nor a vertical separation greater than 1/4 inch. 
o
Drainage - Platforms should allow for curbside drainage flow. A 6-inch height by 12-inch width 
minimum clear gutter space must be provided along the entire length of the proposed platform. 
Openings at either end of the parklet may be covered with screens to prevent debris buildup 
beneath the platform and in the gutter. It is the responsibility of the parklet permittee to clean the 
cover or screen to prevent any backup of storm water.  All parklets must provide access through the 
parklet platform or threshold to the gutter adjacent to the curb. 
o
Bolting Not Allowed - At no time may platforms be bolted or affixed in any way to the roadway or 
any structure (including but not limited to: buildings, fire hydrants, street trees, streetlight, parking 
meters, or traffic poles, etc.). 
o
ADA Accessibility - All accessibility elements of the proposed platform shall be designed, 
constructed and/or conform to the applicable provisions, rules, regulations and guidelines of the 
California Building Code and Americans with Disabilities Act.

Canopies Not Allowed. Tents, canvas, roofs, shade structures are not allowed in the pedestrian mall 
even if they are temporary/removable.  Umbrellas are the only forms of shade protection allowed.

ADA Considerations. Due to the slope of the street within the pedestrian mall, restaurants placing 
dining facilities in the pedestrian mall may also need to have tables available on the sidewalk in order to 
satisfy ADA accessibility requirements.  It is the permittee’s responsibility to ensure ADA compliance.

Special Events. When the City-sponsored events are planned, the City will provide businesses with 72 
hours advance notice to remove all furnishings from the pedestrian malls. If a business does not comply 
its encroachment permit will terminate and the City will remove the furnishings at the permit-holders 
expense and the furnishings will not be returned. Businesses wanting to host a special event will need to 
apply for a Special Community Events permit from the City.
Emergency Access Lane.  The center access lane must be kept clear at all times for emergency vehicles 
access and for short-term commercial vehicle loading, which is allowed from 1 am to 10 am daily.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
STANDARDS FOR INSURANCE
SIDEWALK AND PEDESTRIAN MALL OUTDOOR DINING ENCROACHMENT PERMIT 
Permittee shall procure and maintain for the uration of the permit insurance against claims for injuries to 
persons or damages to property which may arise from or in connection with Permittee’s operation and use of 
the public right-of-way. The cost of such insurance shall be borne by Permittee.
MINIMUM SCOPE AND LIMIT OF INSURANCE 
Coverage shall be at least as broad as:
Commercial General Liability (CGL):  Insurance Services Office Form CG 00 01 covering CGL on an 
“occurrence” basis, including liquor liability coverage, products and completed operations, property damage, 
bodily injury and personal & advertising injury with limits no less than $2,000,000 per occurrence. If a 
general aggregate limit applies, either the general aggregate limit shall apply separately to this 
project/location (ISO CG 25 03 or 25 04) or the general aggregate limit shall be twice the required occurrence 
limit.
Workers’ Compensation:  Workers’ Compensation insurance as required by the State of California, 
with Statutory Limits, and Employer’s Liability Insurance with limits of no less than $2,000,000 per accident 
for bodily injury or disease. (for Applicants with employees).
Property Insurance:  Property insurance against all risks of loss to any tenant improvements or 
betterments, at full replacement cost with no coinsurance penalty provision.
If Permittee maintains broader coverage and/or higher limits than the minimums shown above, the City of 
San Mateo requires and shall be entitled to the broader coverage and/or higher limits maintained. Any 
available insurance proceeds in excess of the specified minimum limits of insurance and coverage shall be 
available to the City of San Mateo.
OTHER INSURANCE PROVISIONS:
The insurance policies are to contain, or be endorsed to contain, the following provisions:
Additional Insured Status
The City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents, are to be covered as additional 
insureds on the CGL policy with respect to liability arising out of work or operations performed by or on 
behalf of the permittee including materials, parts, or equipment furnished in connection with such work 
or operations.  General liability coverage can be provided in the form of an endorsement to the 
Permittee’s insurance (at least as broad as ISO Form CG 20 10).
Primary Coverage
For any claims related to this permit, the Permittee’s insurance coverage shall be primary insurance 
coverage at least as broad as ISO CG 20 01 04 13 as respects the City, its elected and appointed officials, 
employees, and agents.  Any insurance or self-insurance maintained by City, its elected or appointed 
officials, employees, or agents shall be excess of the Permittee’s insurance and shall not contribute with 
it.
Liquor Liability
If Permittee will be supplying alcoholic beverages, the general liability insurance shall include liquor liability 
coverage covering the sale of alcohol.  Permittee must have a valid liquor sales license.
548 of 662

--- page 549 ---
Notice of Cancellation
Each insurance policy required above shall provide that coverage shall not be canceled, except with 30 
days’ prior written notice to City.
Waiver of Subrogation
Permittee hereby grants to City a waiver of any right to subrogation which any insurer of said permittee 
may acquire against City by virtue of the payment of any loss under such insurance.  Permittee agrees to 
obtain any endorsement that may be necessary to affect this waiver of subrogation, but this provision 
applies regardless of whether or not the City has received a waiver of subrogation endorsement from 
the insurer.
Acceptability of Insurers
Insurance is to be placed with insurers authorized to conduct business in the state with a current A.M. 
Best’s rating of no less than A:VII, unless otherwise acceptable to the City.
Self-Insured Retentions
Self-insured retentions must be declared to and approved by the City.  At the option of the City, either:  
the permittee shall obtain coverage to reduce or eliminate such self-insured retentions as respects the 
City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents; or the permittee shall provide a 
financial guarantee satisfactory to the City guaranteeing payment of losses and related investigations, 
claim administration, and defense expenses.  The policy language shall provide, or be endorsed to 
provide, that the self-insured retention may by satisfied by either the named insured or City.
Verification of Coverage
Permittee shall furnish the City with original Certificates of Insurance including all required amendatory 
endorsements (or copies of the applicable policy language effecting coverage required by this clause) 
and a copy of the Declarations and Endorsement Page of the CGL policy listing all policy endorsements 
to City before permitted activities begin.  However, failure to obtain the required documents prior to 
the work beginning shall not waive the permittee’s obligation to provide them.  The City reserves the 
right to require complete, certified copies of all required insurance policies, including endorsements, 
required by these specifications, at any time.  
Special Risks or Circumstances
City reserves the right to modify these requirements at any time, including limits, based on the nature 
of the risk, prior experience, insurer, coverage, or other special circumstances.

SUBJECT:
Revenue Enhancement – Follow-up Discussion
RECOMMENDATION:
Provide direction to staff whether to move forward with a revenue enhancement community survey for real property 
transfer tax and/or a sales tax ballot measure.
BACKGROUND:
On October 4, 2021, the City Council held a study session to evaluate revenue enhancement options, including real 
property transfer tax, utility users’ tax, business tax, and sales tax. The Council directed staff to conduct a statistically-
significant survey of likely voters to evaluate support for a sales tax (transactions and use tax) or real property transfer tax 
(RPTT) measure. After a competitive bid process, True North Research has been selected to conduct the survey should the 
City wish to continue. Since that time, the City’s financial picture has been updated and staff is seeking direction on how to 
proceed. 
As reported in the mid-year budget update on January 18, 2022, the City’s General Fund is projected to have a favorable 
budget variance for the 2021-22 fiscal year. However, the City is anticipating to draw down on the Rainy Day Reserve in 
the coming years and continues to have significant capital improvement needs. The community and Council have also 
identified several key priorities that require additional resources.
Budget Picture
The Council originally considered revenue enhancement measures at a time when the City’s General Fund Long-Term 
Financial Plan was expected to be severely impacted as a result of the COVID-19 pandemic, including a 90% reduction in 
the City's transient occupancy tax (TOT) and a 10% reduction in sales tax revenues. 
In response to the immediate and severe financial impact of the pandemic, the City implemented its Fiscal Sustainability 
Plan to reduce the cost of General Fund operations, which principally included strategic and permanent elimination of $1.0 
million in vacant positions and $3.0 million in employee compensation concessions. The City also received $19.27 million in 
one-time federal stimulus funds from the American Rescue Plan Act, and in November 2020 voters approved increasing 
the City’s hotel TOT rate by 2%. However, as a result of the impact of the pandemic on business travel, which is the 
primary component of the City’s TOT base, the additional revenue anticipated from the TOT rate increase has not come to 
fruition.
As more fully described in the mid-year budget update on January 18, 2022, the City’s current 2021-22 fiscal year General 
Fund is expected to end with $155.2 million in revenues and $146.6 million in expenditures. The General Fund Long-term 
Financial Plan indicates the City will need to draw down on Rainy Day Reserve over the next 4 years, however, over the 
next 10 years, the General Fund is projected to have enough resources to maintain core services at existing levels and for 
552 of 662

--- page 553 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
the Rainy Day Reserve to be restored to pre-pandemic levels by 2032.
Funding Needs
While the General Fund is expected to be able to maintain existing service levels, there are a number of capital 
improvements and priorities that will require additional resources. Estimates for existing capital needs as well as two 
recently completed efforts highlight where additional resources are needed that potential revenue enhancement ballot 
measures could serve as a funding source. This also includes the City Council’s 2022-23 Strategic Plan and the 2022 
Community Opinion Survey, which were presented to Council on March 7, 2022.
In the statistically-significant community survey, residents identified several key City services and priorities including: parks 
and open space areas; enhancing sustainability efforts in planning for the future; improving infrastructure including streets 
and roads; providing more affordable housing; and addressing traffic congestion. 
The following council objectives will also require significant additional resources:

Marina Library structural issues

Meet the 100% trash reduction mandate

Implement a Green Fleet by 2030

Develop funding strategy for existing infrastructure plans 

Implement bicycle and pedestrian projects and programs that address safety and align with adopted City plans
Below are preliminary estimates (not accounting for inflation) for several capital and priority projects that have also been 
identified and for which there aren’t sufficient resources:

Recreation Facilities Strategic Plan – $90 million 

Road maintenance to sufficiently fund paving program and prevent future failed streets – $1 to $2 million annually
General Tax Revenue Enhancement Options
General tax measures require a simple majority of voter approval and must be placed on a ballot during a regular election, 
meaning the next opportunities are either November 2022 or November 2024. As a general tax, the revenue would not be 
tied to specific projects, however, it would go into the City’s General Fund, which is used to provide a range of services and 
improvements. Depending on Council direction, the City would consider placing one of the following two options on a 
ballot: 
Sales Tax – 1/4% Cent Increase
The City’s 1/4% transactions and use tax was first approved by San Mateo voters in 2009 via Measure L, which was set to 
expire in March 2018. In November 2015, voters overwhelmingly approved Measure S, which extended the 1/4% 
transactions and use tax through March 2048. The table below lists every component that constitutes the total sales tax 
rate of 9.625% in the City of San Mateo. State law imposes a 2% limit for add-on sales taxes above the State base sales tax 
rate, which leaves 0.25% in remaining add-on sales tax capacity.

Based on the latest projections for Measure S from the City’s sales tax consultant, a successful new 1/4% cent transactions 
and use tax ballot measure would generate approximately $6.5 million to $7.0 million in revenues per fiscal year.
Changes to Real Property Transfer Tax 
As a charter city, San Mateo has an existing voter-approved real property transfer tax (RPTT) rate of 0.5% that applies to 
residential and commercial properties. This tax is only paid upon transfer or sale of a property, and in residential sales is 
customarily split between a buyer and seller.
As noted during the study session in October 2021, other charter cities in the Bay Area, including San Francisco, Berkeley, 
Oakland, Richmond, and San Jose have recently shifted from a flat rate to a tiered structure. If the City were to shift to the 
tiered structure, it could generate between approximately $5.6 million to $6.8 million in additional revenues per fiscal year 
based on the transactions that were subject to the City’s RPTT in 2020-21.
It is important to note that for properties at the lower tier, which constitute the majority of total sales, a new tiered 
system would not affect RPTT rates. According to the San Mateo County Association of Realtors, in 2021, in the City of San 
Mateo, the median sale price of a single-family home and of a condo was $1.84 million and $880,000, respectively. 
Below are examples of tiered rate structures and estimates on what they could generate:

BUDGET IMPACT:
If the City Council were to forgo a revenue enhancement community survey, the General Fund would save $27,900 in cost. 
Placing any measure on the ballot is expected to cost roughly $100,000 including the survey, and related communications 
and elections expenses.
If a 1/4% transactions and use tax was ultimately approved by the City Council and San Mateo voters, it would generate 
between $6.5 million to $7.0 million in additional General Fund revenue. If an RPTT tiered structure was ultimately 
approved, it would generate between $5.6 million to $6.8 million in additional General Fund revenue. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This revenue enhancement discussion is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative 
activity that will not result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 
15378(b)(5).)

MEETING DATE:
March 21, 2022 
SUBJECT:
Stormwater Funding – Survey Findings
RECOMMENDATION:
Receive an update on the Stormwater Funding Survey findings and staff recommendations for implementation of a 
Stormwater Fee; and provide direction and related input.
BACKGROUND:
Managing the City’s stormwater system consists of a wide range of efforts including the operation and maintenance 
(O&M) of the Marina Lagoon and other creeks, storm drains, and pump stations, as well as the planning and 
implementation of capital improvement projects, trash management activities, and regulatory compliance with the 
Municipal Regional Stormwater Permit. The overall funding needs to effectively manage the City’s stormwater system are 
greater than what is currently available from the General Fund. 
At the February 2021 Sustainability and Infrastructure Commission (SIC) meeting, staff presented the draft Stormwater 
Funding Analysis Report.  The Stormwater Funding Analysis Report, along with feedback from the SIC, were presented to 
the City Council during the May 17, 2021 study session.  Council directed staff to proceed to the next steps towards 
implementing a potential stormwater fee.  On August 16, 2021, City Council approved a contract amendment with the 
consultant, SCI Consulting Group, that included tasks for a public opinion survey, development of a Fee Report, public 
outreach, and Proposition 218 ballot process.  The Stormwater Survey was completed in November 2021 and presented to 
the SIC March 9, 2022. This report presents a review of stormwater activities, Stormwater Funding Analysis, Stormwater 
Survey results, and takeaways from the SIC.
Overview of Storm System Activities: 
Routine operation and maintenance activities for storm sewers includes cleaning and repairs of curbs, inlets, catch basins, 
lateral and main lines, outfalls, and nine stormwater pump stations. The City has four main creeks that convey water 
towards the bay and/or the Marina Lagoon: San Mateo Creek, Leslie Creek, Borel Creek, and Laurel Creek. Creek 
maintenance includes routine removal of nuisance vegetation, bank stabilization, outfall maintenance, and other related 
activities.  Storm system capital improvement projects include those from the 2018 Marina Lagoon Dredging Assessment, 
the Trash Load Reduction Plan, a Storm Drain Condition Analysis, a Storm Drain Master Plan update, creek maintenance 
permitting efforts, and several other projects.
Marina Lagoon is an amenity for the entire City and beyond. Managing the lagoon as a flood control basin as its primary 
purpose includes maintaining operating levels, water exchange rates, the pump station, and other infrastructure. 
Management also includes removal of nuisance aquatic plant control, water quality protection, wildlife management, 
permit compliance, and regulation of encroachments such as private docks, beaches, banks, and dredging.
556 of 662

--- page 557 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Stormwater Funding Analysis and Survey:
The overall funding needs to effectively manage the City’s stormwater system are greater than what is currently available 
from the General Fund, which is approximately $3 million annually for general stormwater program operations and 
maintenance.  The financial analysis completed in 2021 identified the following additional needs and bring the total 
stormwater needs to $8.4M annually: 

$625,000 for additional O&M; 

$1.9 million for Marina Lagoon dredging (annualized life-cycle costs derived from estimated dredging costs of as 
much as $80 million); and

$2.9 million for other capital projects ($68 million amortized over 30 years).  
The first step was conducting a public opinion survey on stormwater fees, which has been completed.  The surveys were 
conducted by mail to mimic the balloting process.  Surveys were sent to property owners who were targeted through a 
stratified random selection process.  The survey tested rates and issues for (1) a city-wide stormwater fee for all services 
included in the Stormwater Funding Analysis Report and (2) a city-wide benefit assessment focused solely on Marina 
Lagoon improvements such as dredging.  This resulted in two separate, yet simultaneous surveys.
For the city-wide stormwater fee, the Stormwater Funding Analysis Report determined annual fees ranging from $120 to 
$192 and those two rates were tested in the survey.  For the Marina Lagoon survey, the Stormwater Funding Analysis 
Report identified annual assessment rates of $10 for most of the City and $50 or $150 for properties within walking 
distance or fronting the Marina Lagoon, respectively.  This rate structure was predicted to only raise approximately 
$500,000 annually toward a total need of $1.9 million.  Therefore, a second, larger rate range of $23 to $350 per year was 
also tested, although estimated revenues of $1.2 million would still be less than the $1.9 million needed annually. 
The surveys were accompanied by supplemental information and also included questions about projects and issues that 
might affect how a voter would view a ballot initiative. Respondents were asked whether a project or issue would cause 
them to change their support of the initiative.  The surveys and information were provided in both English and Spanish and 
can be referenced in Attachment 1. 
Survey Results
A total of 10,000 surveys were mailed in November 2021 (5,000 each for Stormwater and the Marina Lagoon), and 1,550 
were returned in time to be included in the results. The return rate of 15.5% is typical for mailed surveys and could be 
considered better than average considering it was the lead-up time for the holidays.
The results showed neutral support for the Stormwater fee and marginal support for the Marina Lagoon assessment. The 
Stormwater Fee had 49.9% support at the $120/year level, and 46.8% support at the $192/year level.  The Marina Lagoon 
assessment had 54.6% support at the $10/year level, and 51.6% support at the $23/year level.  Either type of funding 
initiative would require a simple 50% majority. The margins of error on these results were 3.4% and 3.6%, respectively.  
Respondents were more likely to support a city-wide Stormwater Fee that 1) protects the City from local street and 
property flooding by properly maintaining and operating its aging storm drainage infrastructure, 2) removes trash and 
pollution from San Francisco Bay and local creeks, and 3) has dedicated funds for stormwater and could not be taken away 
by the State, County, or even the City Council.
Conversely, respondents were less likely to support a city-wide Stormwater Fee that would specifically fund Marina Lagoon 
issues such as 1) dredging and other stormwater improvements 2) preventing unwanted vegetation and other pollutants, 
and 3) as a recreational amenity and flood control facility.
The most supported projects and issues related to the Marina Lagoon Assessment survey included 1) removing trash and 
pollution to San Francisco Bay and local creeks 2) providing flood control capacity to protect streets and properties from 
local flooding, 3) ensuring safe, clean, healthy water in creeks and San Francisco Bay for future generations, and 4) 
dedicated funds for stormwater that could not be taken away by the State, County, or even the City Council. Respondents 
557 of 662

--- page 558 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
were less likely to support a Marina Lagoon Assessment that would only pay for some of the dredging and may not be 
enough to fully fund Lagoon dredging, likely requiring a separate stormwater fee.
Analysis
While the support levels for the Marina Lagoon Assessment were slightly greater than for a Stormwater Fee, the lower 
revenue potential from the assessment combined with the strong negative argument of insufficient funding to complete 
identified improvements suggested that a Marina Lagoon-centric assessment would not be worth pursuing at this time.
The Stormwater Fee results are slightly less than 50% at the $120/year rate.  The consultant typically recommends moving 
forward on an initiative only when the support level minus the margin of error is at least 50%, which was not met. 
However, a lesser rate of under $100/year, or approximately $8/month may very well have enough support to pass.  The 
projects and issues that resonate with the community were well rounded (infrastructure, environmental and fiscal 
prudence), which forms a solid basis for an outreach and education program.
Summary of Input from Sustainability and Infrastructure Commission
Staff presented the survey results to the SIC on March 9, 2022.  The SIC were consistently supportive of proceeding with 
implementation of a robust public outreach and education program, developing a fee report, and pursuing the Stormwater 
Fee at a level less than $100/year.  The SIC also provided feedback on future outreach strategies.  Other input included 
recommendations to explore partnerships with Foster City for continued funding of Marina Lagoon, and to consider 
enhancing pedestrian walkways surrounding the lagoon.
Recommendations
Staff is recommending the following for consideration and is requesting Council feedback and direction on proceeding with 
implementation.

Conducting a robust public outreach and education program that would take place over a six-to-nine month 
period;

Developing a Fee Report that illustrates the services and improvements that would be funded at $8/month;

Pursuing the Stormwater Fee at a level less than $100/year (approximately $8/month); and

Beginning the Proposition 218 ballot process after the outreach and education program concludes.
The timing for commencing the outreach and education program will depend on whether Council provides direction to put 
a general tax measure on the ballot in 2022, which is also a consideration at the March 21st Council meeting.  Should 
Council opt to pursue a general tax measure, which would begin with polling a sampling of voters to gauge support levels, 
staff recommends holding off on starting the outreach and education program until the conclusion of that process.  
Depending on the results of the stormwater fee process, staff can further consider a Marina Lagoon Assessment in the 
future.
BUDGET IMPACT:
There is no budget impact due to the presentation of this information item.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This informational update is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity that 
will not result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

Why Am I Receiving This Survey?
Investing in Local Infrastructure
The City of San Mateo operates and maintains 
various  stormwater  facilities  and performs 
activities necessary to prevent ﬂ ooding, preserve 
surface water quality, enhance recreational 
activities, and ensure compliance with all legal 
requirements. Local facilities include: Marina 
Lagoon and other creeks and channels; pipes 
and pumping systems; and trash capture 
devices. Activities include: operations and 
maintenance; capital improvements and master 
planning; environmental compliance; and system 
management and administration.
See Reverse Side for More Information    
 CITY OF SAN MATEO
Information Fact Sheet
Clean Creeks and Flood Protection Initiative
Like most cities across the Nation, San Mateo 
viewed their storm drainage system as a simple 
conveyance for  rain runoﬀ .  But over the past 
30 years, environmental health and safety 
requirements  have  brought  stormwater  programs 
on par with other municipal utilities like water and 
sewer systems. And like those utilities, a dedicated 
and sustainable revenue stream is the most fair 
and reliable way to fund the City’s investment into 
this critical infrastructure. 
The City of San Mateo is considering proposing 
a funding measure to help pay for the critical 
needs in managing the stormwater facilities, and 
seeks input from local property owners on your 
priorities for stormwater activities.  
Current operations are estimated at $3.0 million 
per year.  However, in order to sustainability fund 
the Stormwater Program and meet adequate levels 
of service an additional $5.4 million is needed per 
year. This is includes an additional $0.6 million 
for additional operations and maintenance, $1.9 
million for Marina Lagoon dredging, and $2.9 
million for other important capital projects and 
programs.
Long-Term Financial Sustainability
Funds from this local funding measure would 
be used to continue to protect our streets from 
local ﬂ ooding, ensure clean water for enjoyment, 
restore and maintain Marina Lagoon, and protect 
this critical City feature for current and future 
generations.
Please read the following information, then 
complete the enclosed survey and return it in 
the postage paid envelope as soon as possible. 
Your answers will help guide our eﬀ orts towards 
protecting the City and its residents from local 
ﬂ ooding and protecting water quality. 
Water levels in the south end of the Lagoon have decreased dramatically 
due to silt deposits, resulting in loss of navigable water depth for boating, 
loss of wildlife habitat, and decreased stormwater retention capacity greatly 
reducing the City’s ﬂ ood control capacity. 
To fund the Stormwater Program in sustainable 
manner, a separate, dedicated revenue stream 
is recommended that would no longer compete 
with other General Fund priorities and would be 
shared by property owners in a fairer way. It is 
estimated a monthly fee between $10 and $16 for 
the average home would fund this program.
The City’s Stormwater Program is funded largely 
from the City’s General Fund where it must 
compete with other community priorities such as 
public safety, roads, and parks.  Due to this, and the 
limited nature of the General Fund, the Stormwater 
Program has been operating at a minimum level 
of service with few investments in critical projects 
aimed at reducing ﬂ ood risks and performing pro-
active maintenance on Marina Lagoon.

City of San Mateo - Clean Creeks and Flood Protection Initiative

Stormwater Improvement Plan    
Protecting Property
Sustainable Stormdrains
Protecting the Bay
The City’s stormwater system is comprised of 
130 miles of storm drains, 20 miles of open creeks 
and drainage channels, 1 ﬂ ood control lagoon,  9 
pumping stations, and a 3 mile bayfront levee. 
Routine operation and maintenance activities 
include cleaning and repairs of curbs, inlets, catch 
basins, lateral and main lines, and outfalls. Various 
factors, such as clogged catch basins or pipes that 
do not adequately convey standard rainwater 
ﬂ ow, cause ﬂ ooding in various areas throughout 
the City, making it diﬃ  cult for pedestrians, 
bicycles, motorcycles, cars, and emergency 
vehicles to navigate.
Routine Operations
Among the stormwater management activities 
in the City of San Mateo, one has emerged as 
paramount: stewardship of Marina Lagoon. 
As the receiving body of most of the City’s 
stormwater ﬂ ows, the Lagoon has an important 
drainage role.  In addition, it plays a large role as a 
recreational amenity for swimming, boating, and 
other water activities. However, both the drainage 
and recreational aspects are being degraded due 
to the accumulation of silt and sediment that cause 
navigation and health problems. An analysis 
that showed that dredging Marina Lagoon to its 
original state would cost as much as $85 million 
– more than all other stormwater capital needs 
combined. If left unatt ended, Marina Lagoon 
would continue to degrade and cease to be a 
recreational amenity - the Gem of the City.
To respond online, or para responder en español, visit www.inputlocal.com and enter code found on front of survey
City engineers and maintenance crews have 
eﬀ ectively maintained our stormwater system 
on a limited budget for many years.  However, 
much of the system is no over 50 years old and 
reaching the end of its design life. The areas where 
improvements need to be made include segments 
of 16th Avenue, 9th Avenue, Laurel Creek, Coyote 
Point, Detroit Drive as well as 13 high priority 
projects are aimed at reducing ﬂ ooding throughout 
the City.

Inversión en infraestructura local 
La ciudad de San Mateo está considerando proponer 
una medida de financiación para ayudar a pagar las 
necesidades fundamentales en el control de las 
instalaciones de desagüe pluvial, y busca conocer la 
opinión de los propietarios locales sobre las 
prioridades en actividades de este servicio. 
Lea la siguiente información, complete la encuesta 
adjunta y envíela dentro de un sobre con franqueo 
pago lo antes posible. Sus respuestas nos ayudarán a 
enfocar los esfuerzos hacia la conservación de la 
calidad del agua y la protección de la ciudad y sus 
residentes contra las inundaciones locales. 
Sustentabilidad financiera a largo plazo El 
Programa 
de 
desagüe 
pluvial 
de 
la 
ciudad 
(Stormwater Program) está financiado, en gran parte, 
por el Fondo general de la ciudad, donde debe 
competir con otras prioridades de la comunidad, 
como la seguridad, carreteras y parque públicos. Por 
lo tanto, y debido a la naturaleza limitada del fondo 
general, este programa ha operado a un nivel de 
servicio mínimo, con pocas inversiones en proyectos 
esenciales dirigidos a la reducción de riesgos por 
inundación y al mantenimiento proactivo de Marina 
Lagoon. 
Se calcula que las operaciones actuales utilizan 
$3 000 000 por año. Sin embargo, se necesita un 
adicional de $5 400 000 por año para financiar el 
programa de manera sustentable y cumplir los niveles 
adecuados de servicio. Esto incluye un adicional de 
$600 000 
para 
operaciones 
y 
mantenimiento 
complementarios, $1 900 000 para el dragado de 
Marina Lagoon y $2 900 000 para otros proyectos de 
capital y programas importantes. 
 
Para financiar el programa de desagüe pluvial de 
forma sustentable, se recomienda una fuente de 
ingresos independiente y específica que no compita 
con otras prioridades del fondo general y capte el 
interés de los propietarios de una manera más 
razonable. Se calcula que una cuota mensual de entre 
$10 y $16 por hogar promedio podría financiar el 
programa. 
La ciudad de San Mateo opera y mantiene distintas 
instalaciones de desagüe pluvial y realiza acciones 
necesarias para evitar inundaciones, conservar la 
calidad del agua superficial, fomentar las actividades 
recreativas y asegurar el cumplimiento de todos los 
requisitos legales. Las instalaciones locales son: Marina 
Lagoon y otros arroyos y canales; tuberías y sistemas 
de bombeo; y dispositivos de recolección de basura. 
Las acciones que se llevan a cabo son: operaciones y 
mantenimiento; mejoras del capital y planificación 
principal; cumplimiento de las medidas ecológicas; y 
administración del sistema. 
Como la mayoría de las ciudades del país, San Mateo 
consideraba al sistema de desagüe pluvial un simple 
medio de transporte para el escurrimiento del agua de 
lluvia. No obstante, durante los últimos 30 años, las 
necesidades de seguridad e higiene ambiental han 
puesto a los programas de aguas pluviales a la par de 
otros servicios municipales como los sistemas de agua 
corriente y alcantarillado.  Al igual que estos servicios, 
la implementación de una fuente de ingresos específica 
y sustentable es la forma más justa y confiable de 
financiar 
la 
inversión 
de 
la 
ciudad 
en 
esta 
infraestructura fundamental. 
Los fondos de esta medida de financiación se 
destinarían a continuar con la protección de nuestras 
calles contra las inundaciones, garantizar agua limpia 
para la recreación, restaurar y mantener Marina 
Lagoon y proteger este elemento esencial de la ciudad 
para las generaciones actuales y futuras. 
 
Los niveles de agua en el extremo sur de Marina Lagoon se han reducido drásticamente 
debido a los depósitos de lodo, lo cual llevó a la pérdida de profundidad del agua de 
navegación, a la pérdida de hábitats de la fauna y a la reducción de la capacidad de 
retención de aguas pluviales, por lo que se redujo también la capacidad de control de las 
inundaciones de la ciudad.

Ciudad de San Mateo - Iniciativa de arroyos limpios y protección contra inundaciones 
Ficha informativa 
 
 
Marina Lagoon - La joya de la ciudad Entre 
las actividades de administración de las aguas 
pluviales en la ciudad de San Mateo, una de ellas 
cobró especial importancia: la administración de 
Marina Lagoon. La laguna tiene una importante 
función en el desagüe de la ciudad, ya que se trata del 
cuerpo receptor del caudal de la mayor parte de las 
aguas pluviales. Además, cumple un rol importante 
como servicio recreativo donde se puede realizar 
natación, navegación y otras actividades acuáticas. 
Sin embargo,  tanto su función de drenaje como su 
función recreativa están deteriorándose debido a la 
acumulación de lodo y sedimento que causan 
problemas en la salud y la navegación. Según un 
estudio, dragar Marina Lagoon para recuperar su 
estado original costaría hasta $85 000 000,  
más que todo el capital que se necesita para otros 
servicios de desagüe pluvial. Si no se atiende esta 
cuestión, la laguna continuará deteriorándose hasta 
dejar de ser un espacio recreativo, la joya de la 
ciudad. 
 
 
Operaciones de rutina 
El sistema de aguas pluviales de la ciudad se 
compone de 209 kilómetros de desagües pluviales, 32 
kilómetros de arroyos y canales de desagüe abiertos, 
1 laguna para el control de inundaciones, 9 estaciones 
de bombeo y un dique de 3 millas en la bahía. Las 
actividades rutinarias de operación y mantenimiento 
incluyen la limpieza y reparación de cunetas, tomas 
de entrada, sumideros, líneas principales y laterales y 
emisarios. Las inundaciones se generan en distintas 
zonas de la ciudad debido a varios factores, como 
sumideros tapados o tuberías que no cumplen con la 
capacidad de caudal de agua necesaria, lo que 
dificulta la circulación de peatones, bicicletas, 
motocicletas, automóviles y vehículos de emergencia. 
 
Otras fallas del sistema 
Los equipos de mantenimiento e ingenieros de la 
ciudad han logrado mantener nuestro sistema de 
desagüe pluvial de forma eficaz con un presupuesto 
escaso durante muchos años. No obstante, gran parte 
del sistema no tiene más de 50 años y ya está 
alcanzando el límite de vida útil. Las zonas donde se 
necesitan arreglos y mejoras son algunos tramos de la 
16th Avenue, 9th Avenue, Laurel Creek y Coyote 
Point y Detroit Drive. Además, se cuenta con 13 
proyectos de prioridad alta que apuntan a la 
reducción de las inundaciones en la ciudad.

Why Am I Receiving This Survey?
Investing in Local Infrastructure
All of us in San Mateo are dependent on our local 
storm drainage system to keep our streets, homes, 
and property safe from local flooding, primarily 
due to seasonal rain storms. However, among the 
stormwater management activities in the City of San 
Mateo, one has emerged as paramount: stewardship 
of Marina Lagoon. As the receiving body of most of 
the City’s stormwater flows, Marina lagoon has an 
important drainage role and acts as a flood control 
basin, ultimately providing protection to properties 
and roadways from large winter storms.  
In addition, Marina lagoon plays a large role as a 
community recreational amenity for scenic walks, 
swimming, boating, and other water activities. 
However, both the drainage and recreational aspects 
are being degraded due to the accumulation of silt and 
sediment coming from upstream sources.  As a result, 
the City of San Mateo is considering proposing a funding 
measure to help pay for these critical infrastructure 
needs, and seeks input from local property owners 
on your priorities for restoration and maintenance of 
Marina Lagoon, and local flood protection. Please read 
the following information, and complete the enclosed 
survey. Your answers will help guide our efforts 
toward restoring and maintaining Marina Lagoon, and 
protecting the City and its residents.   
In recognition of the challenges facing Marina Lagoon, 
the City of San Mateo conducted an analysis in 2018 
showing that a full dredging of Marina Lagoon to its 
original state, which has not been done since 1965, 
would cost as much as $85 million – more than all other 
stormwater capital needs combined. Additionally, the 
analysis found overall stormwater funding needs are 
greater than what is currently available through the 
General Fund.
In response to this information, the City is currently 
exploring funding options to effectively manage both 
Marina Lagoon, and the City’s overall stormwater 
system.  The included survey questionnaire will help 
the City evaluate the community’s priorities regarding 
investment in Marina Lagoon and protecting this 
Beach-going is a popular activity at 
Marina Lagoon
See Reverse Side for More Information    
Marina Lagoon as an Amenity 
and Infrastucture
Marina Lagoon featuers nine miles of waterfront, and 
receives most of the City’s stormwater runoff
Marina Lagoon 
Restoration Initiative
Marina Lagoon serves a dual purpose as the City’s 
primary flood control basin, while providing local 
recreational activities such as scenic strolls, beach 
activities, and boating. Marina Lagoon’s function 
within the City’s Stormwater infrastructure and as 
a recreational attraction comprises an inter-related 
system requiring significant resources to excel in its 
performance of both functions.

--- page 567 ---
Challenges Facing Marina 
Lagoon
To respond online, or para responder en español, visit www.inputlocal.com and enter code found on front of survey
Long-Term Financial Sustainability
The City currently spends approximately $500,000 
annually for managing Marina Lagoon, including 
operations, maintenance, water quality and vegetation 
management; money that comes from the City’s General 
Fund where it must compete with many other community 
services such as public safetey, roads, and parks.  But 
all this is a losing battle without removing the silt and 
sediment that has built up over the past five decades. The 
lack of a dedicated, sustainable funding source is resulting 
in degradation of Marina Lagoon as a recreational amenity 
and stormwater infrastructure facility.
A local funding measure would go a long way toward 
restoring Marina Lagoon, and protect this critical City 
feature for current and future generations. 
Information Fact Sheet
Funds from a local ballot measure would allow the 
City to restore and properly maintain Marina Lagoon. 
Following is a list of improvements to Marina Lagoon 
that additional funding could provide:
•	
Vegetation growth management
•	
Improved water quality due to dredging
•	
Silt excavation for proper tide gate functionality
•	
Increased stormwater retention capacity due to dredging
•	
Restoration of navigable water depth for boating and kayaking
•	
Restoration of bird and other wildlife habitat
What Will a Funding Measure    
Provide?
Marina Lagoon receives water conveyed from three 
of the City’s four main local creeks; Leslie Creek, 
Borel Creek, and Laurel Creek, consumating its 
role as a stormwater retention and flood control 
basin.Tidal gates at the southern and northern 
ends of Marina Lagoon act as an inlet and outlet. 
In 1964 when the last full dredging was completed, 
Marina Lagoon infrastructure provided a navigable 
body of water for recreational use, and a wildlife 
sanctuary for birds and animals.  
However, 40 years have passed since the last 
partial dredging in 1981, and both the drainage 
and recreational aspects of Marina Lagoon are 
being degraded due to the accumulation of silt 
and sediment coming from upstream sources and 
the Bay, causing other environmental problems 
such as algae blooms, water quality, and limited 
navigable water depth for boats. 
Algae blooms are increasing due to 
higher temperatures associated with 
climate change 
Water levels in the south end of Marina 
Lagoon have decreased dramatically 
due to silt deposits, resulting in loss 
of navigable water depth for boating, 
loss of wildlife habitat, and decreased 
stormwater retention capacity greatly 
reducing the City’s flood control 
capacity. 
Marina Lagoon drains water from most of the City’s creeks and pipes, 
providing critical stormwater retention for the City
Marina Lagoon 
Restoration Initiative

¿Por qué recibo esta encuesta? 
Todas las personas de San Mateo dependemos de 
nuestro sistema de alcantarillado pluvial local para 
proteger nuestras calles, nuestros hogares y nuestras 
pertenencias 
de 
las 
inundaciones 
locales, 
principalmente debido a las tormentas de estación. 
Sin 
embargo, 
dentro 
de 
las 
actividades 
de 
administración de las aguas pluviales en la ciudad de 
San Mateo, una de ellas cobró especial importancia: la 
administración de Marina Lagoon. La laguna tiene 
una importante función en el desagüe de la ciudad, ya 
que se trata del cuerpo receptor del caudal de la 
mayor parte de las aguas pluviales y actúa como 
cuenca para controlar las inundaciones y, en última 
instancia, protege las propiedades y las carreteras de 
las grandes tormentas invernales. 
Además, Marina Lagoon cumple un rol importante 
como servicio recreativo comunitario donde se 
pueden realizar pintorescas caminatas, natación, 
navegación 
y 
otras 
actividades 
acuáticas. 
Sin 
embargo, tanto su función de drenaje como su 
función recreativa están deteriorándose debido a la 
acumulación de lodo y sedimento que proviene de 
fuentes río arriba. Como consecuencia, la ciudad de 
San Mateo está pensando en proponer una medida de 
financiamiento para ayudar a pagar estas necesidades 
de infraestructura fundamentales y busca conocer la 
opinión de los propietarios locales sobre sus 
prioridades en cuanto a la recuperación y el 
mantenimiento de Marina Lagoon y a la protección 
local contra las inundaciones. Lea la siguiente 
información y complete la encuesta que se adjunta. 
Sus respuestas ayudarán a guiar nuestros esfuerzos 
para recuperar y mantener la laguna Marina y para 
proteger a la ciudad y sus residentes. 
 
Ir a la playa es una actividad popular en 
Marina Lagoon. 
Marina Lagoon como 
establecimiento e infraestructura 
Marina Lagoon cumple un doble fin, ya que es la 
principal cuenca para el control de las inundaciones 
de la ciudad y, al mismo tiempo, ofrece actividades 
recreativas locales, como caminatas pintorescas, 
actividades de playa y navegación. La función de 
Marina Lagoon dentro de la infraestructura pluvial 
de la ciudad y como atracción recreativa comprende 
un sistema interrelacionado que requiere de muchos 
recursos para poder cumplir con sus dos funciones de 
manera correcta. 
 
Marina Lagoon tiene una orilla de nueve millas y 
recibe la mayor parte del agua de escorrentía de la ciudad 
 
Inversión en infraestructura local 
Tras reconocer las dificultades que atraviesa Marina 
Lagoon, la ciudad de San Mateo llevó a cabo un 
análisis en 2018, a partir del cual se demostró que 
dragar por completo la laguna para recuperar su 
estado original, lo cual no se ha hecho desde 1965, 
costaría $85 millones, más que todas las demás 
necesidades 
de 
capital 
para 
aguas 
pluviales 
combinadas. Además, a partir del análisis se 
determinó que los fondos que se necesitan para 
invertir en las aguas pluviales son mayores que el 
monto que está disponible actualmente a través del 
fondo general. 
Como respuesta a esta información, la ciudad está 
explorando 
opciones 
de 
financiamiento 
para 
administrar de manera eficaz tanto Marina Lagoon 
como el sistema general de aguas pluviales de la 
ciudad. La encuesta que incluimos ayudará a la 
ciudad a evaluar las prioridades de la comunidad en 
relación con las inversiones en Marina Lagoon y en su 
protección.

--- page 569 ---
Iniciativa de recuperación 
de Marina Lagoon 
Ficha informativa 
Las dificultades que 
atraviesa Marina Lagoon 
 
Marina Lagoon recibe el agua que drena de la mayoría de los riachuelos y las 
tuberías de la ciudad, por lo que constituye un sistema de retención 
de aguas pluviales fundamental para la ciudad. 
 
Marina Lagoon recibe el agua proveniente de tres 
de los cuatro riachuelos principales de la ciudad 
(los riachuelos Leslie, Borel y Laurel), por lo que 
consuma su función como cuenca de retención de 
aguas pluviales y de control de inundaciones. Las 
compuertas de marea en los extremos norte y sur 
de la laguna actúan como entrada y salida. En 
1964, cuando se llevó a cabo el último dragado 
completo, la infraestructura de Marina Lagoon 
ofrecía un cuerpo de agua navegable para uso 
recreativo y un santuario de vida silvestre para 
aves y animales. 
No obstante, ya han pasado 40 años desde su 
último dragado parcial en 1981, y tanto su función 
de drenaje como su función recreativa se están 
deteriorando debido a la acumulación de lodo y 
sedimento que proviene de fuentes río arriba y de 
la bahía, lo cual genera otros problemas 
ambientales, como la proliferación de algas, el 
deterioro de la calidad del agua y la limitación de 
la profundidad del agua para navegar. 
Sustentabilidad financiera a largo 
plazo 
En la actualidad, la ciudad invierte aproximadamente 
$500 000 al año en la administración de Marina 
Lagoon, lo cual incluye operaciones, mantenimiento y 
administración de la calidad del agua y la vegetación. 
Este dinero proviene del fondo general de la ciudad, 
donde debe competir con muchos otros servicios 
comunitarios, 
como 
la 
seguridad 
pública, 
las 
carreteras y los parques.  Pero todo está destinado a 
fracasar si no se elimina el lodo y el sedimento que se 
acumuló en las últimas cinco décadas. La falta de una 
fuente de financiación exclusiva y sostenible está 
llevando 
al 
deterioro 
de 
la 
laguna 
como 
establecimiento recreativo y de infraestructura para 
las aguas pluviales. 
 
Una medida de financiamiento local podría contribuir 
mucho a la recuperación de la laguna y a la 
protección de este atractivo fundamental de la ciudad 
para las generaciones actuales y futuras. 
¿Qué garantizará una 
medida de financiamiento? 
Los fondos provenientes de una medida electoral 
local le permitirían a la ciudad recuperar y mantener 
Marina Lagoon de manera adecuada. La siguiente es 
una 
lista 
de 
mejoras 
en 
la 
laguna 
que 
el 
financiamiento adicional podría ayudar a garantizar: 
• 
Administración del crecimiento de la vegetación. 
• 
Mejora de la calidad del agua gracias al dragado. 
• 
Excavación del lodo para mejorar el funcionamiento de las 
compuertas de marea. 
• 
Mayor capacidad de retención de las aguas pluviales gracias al 
dragado. 
• 
Recuperación de la profundidad del agua para poder navegar en 
bote y hacer kayak. 
• 
Recuperación del hábitat para aves y otros tipos de fauna. 
 
Los niveles de agua en el extremo sur de Marina Lagoon 
se han reducido drásticamente debido a los depósitos de 
lodo, lo cual llevó a la pérdida de profundidad del agua de 
navegación, a la pérdida de hábitats de la fauna y a la 
reducción de la capacidad de retención de aguas 
pluviales, por lo que se redujo también la capacidad de 
control de las inundaciones de la ciudad. 
 
 
La proliferación de algas ha 
aumentado debido a las altas 
temperaturas relacionadas con 
el cambio climático. 
 
 
Si desea responder en línea o en español, visite www.inputlocal.com e ingrese el código que se encuentra en 
el frente de la encuesta

Agenda Number:  28
Section Name: PUBLIC HEARING
File ID: 22-5867
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Drew Corbett, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Community Development Department
MEETING DATE:
March 21, 2022 
SUBJECT:
330 Villa Terrace – Property Owner Appeal of Relocation Benefits
RECOMMENDATION:
Deny the appeal and uphold the notice to pay relocation benefits to the tenant who resides at 330 Villa Terrace due to 
unlawful substandard living conditions pursuant to San Mateo Municipal Code Chapter 7.50 “Property Owner Obligations 
with Respect to Tenants Displaced from Unsafe or Substandard Units.”
BACKGROUND:
 History:
The subject property is currently developed with a single-family dwelling, a detached accessory dwelling unit, and an 
accessory storage structure.  City records show that in 1992 the accessory storage structure (identified as “Unit A” in this 
report and attachments), which had previously been illegally converted to a residential unit, was converted back to an 
accessory storage structure. Therefore, Unit A may only be used for storage and similar accessory uses. There is currently 
one tenant residing in Unit A, and neither the use nor the previous residential unit modifications to the structure are 
permitted. The monthly rent is $1,695. According to the property owner this tenant has resided in Unit A for six (6) years.
On January 27, 2022, Code Enforcement received a complaint of a raw sewage leak outside of Unit A. Code Enforcement 
Officer II (CEO) Kristen Aiu immediately responded to the site to inspect the exterior and interior of the structure. During 
the inspection CEO Aiu observed the following conditions: 

The interior of the storage structure had been converted to habitable space without City approval. The alterations 
included the addition of a bathroom and kitchen with plumbing, mechanical, and electrical improvements.

The electrical system was determined to be an immediate hazard due to hazardous wiring in the main electrical 
box. This required immediate repair by a licensed electrician and the work has been completed with a permit.

Interior walls are deteriorated especially in the bathroom where moisture has caused mold, rot, and decay of the 
floor and walls.

There were no working smoke detectors or carbon monoxide alarms. 

The interior ceiling is damaged and sagging in various places due to water intrusion from the leaking roof. 

The exterior of the structure was severely dilapidated and contained dry rot sewage residue around the sewer 
clean out. 
City Response:
Due to the immediate hazardous conditions of the electrical panel, the property owner hired a licensed electrician to make 
repairs. A permit was obtained from the City and that work was finalized on February 28, 2022. While the urgent electrical 
repair was taking place, CEO Aiu researched City and San Mateo County property records and discovered that Unit A is not 
a legal dwelling unit and therefore would need to go through the established development review and permitting process 
to have a third dwelling unit on the property. Considering the requirements involved with the development review 
572 of 662

--- page 573 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
process, permitting process, and correcting the substandard conditions, staff determined that the structure could not 
foreseeably be brought into code compliance within 90 days from the date of an order to vacate. Therefore, the tenant 
would be considered permanently displaced due to the substandard living conditions and would be eligible for relocation 
benefits. 
On February 14, 2022, a notice was issued to the property owner declaring the substandard conditions and ordering Unit A 
to be vacated by no later than February 28, 2022. Included in that notice, pursuant to San Mateo Municipal Code (SMMC) 
sections 7.50.090(a), the Property Owner was ordered to pay the tenant for permanent relocation in the following 
amounts:
1.
The HUD rate for a one-bedroom unit in San Mateo County is $2,923 x 3 = $8,769. 
2.
Actual moving costs and related expenses not to exceed $1,000 to be paid within 60 days of receipt of expenses.
3.
In addition, an immediate vacation allowance in the amount of $1,000 because the tenant received fewer than 30 
days advanced notice before being displaced pursuant to SMMC section 7.50.090(d). 
Appeal:
The property owner has appealed the tenant relocation payments asserting the tenant will not be deemed displaced 
pursuant to SMMC 7.50.030(b)(6) since the property owner has offered a replacement dwelling with the same 
accommodations, size, and rent, located in Redwood City.
Analysis:
In accordance with SMMC 7.50.030, the tenant shall be eligible for relocation payments from a property owner if the 
tenant household is displaced from its dwelling or room due to the City's code enforcement activities. Specifically, SMMC 
7.50.030(a)(2) states that the tenant household shall vacate its dwelling or room (whether or not the property owner 
requires vacation) after the City has issued a notice to vacate, notice to abate life-threatening condition, or declaration of 
substandard condition covering that dwelling or room.  SMMC 7.50.030(b)(6) states that the tenant shall not be deemed 
displaced due to code enforcement activities if the property owner offers, in writing, to move the tenant household 
immediately into a replacement dwelling or room in the same building or complex, allowing the owner to waive the 
relocation payments. 
The replacement dwelling offered by the appellant does not comply with this requirement, as it is in Redwood City, and 
not in the same building or complex. Further, the tenant has informed CEO Aiu that she does not agree to this offer from 
the property owner. According to SMMC section 7.50.030, the tenant is displaced, is eligible for relocation benefits in the 
form monetary compensation or a dwelling in the same building or complex, and neither of which have been offered or 
provided by the property owner. Therefore, staff recommends the appeal be denied and for tenant relocation benefits be 
provided to the tenant according to City requirements.
BUDGET IMPACT:
There is no budget impact related to this report. If Council finds that City funds should be used to provide relocation 
benefits to the tenant, the money would be allocated from the Emergency Relocation Assistance Fund that was 
established to advance tenant assistance in urgent situations and then billed to the property owner. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This appeal is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity that will not result in 
direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

02/14/2022 
WARNING LETTER TO ABATE A PUBLIC NUISANCE 
AND DECLARATION OF A SUBSTANDARD DWELLING CONDITIONAND  
NOTICE TO VACATE  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
This notice is part of the ongoing Code Enforcement case for your property located at 330 VILLA TER 
in San Mateo, California.  As outlined in the previous notice 01/27/2022, the City of San Mateo Code 
Enforcement received a complaint regarding violations of the San Mateo Municipal Code (SMMC) 
existing on your property. Based on that information, Code Enforcement Staff conducted an 
inspection of the property on 01/27/2022, specifically the building identified on the property as Unit 
A. In addition to the inspection, I have reviewed the permit historical records of the property and 
have determined that the structure identified as Unit A is not permitted for habitable use. This is a 
formal notice from the City of San Mateo to correct the violations. 
 
Substandard Conditions: 
 Prohibited occupancy of an accessory structure. 
 Unpermitted construction/alterations of the accessory structure include plumbing, electrical, 
and mechanical (the structure is identified on the site as Unit A). 
 
Required Corrections and Compliance Date: 
Because this structure is not a permitted habitable space and the steps necessary to legalize it as 
such will take longer than 90 days, the City of San Mateo hereby orders that the accessory structure 
be vacated no later than 4:00pm on Monday, February 28, 2022. Once this structure has been 
vacated it shall remain vacant until such time that it can be legalized as permitted dwelling unit.   
Enclosed is an informational planning application packet outlining the process to add a lawful third 
dwelling on your property.  
 
If you choose not to legalize this structure as a dwelling unit, no later than March 14, 2021 you must 
apply for all necessary permits to restore the structure back to the last permitted use as a “storage 
structure only” 
 
Whichever option you choose, there is a specified process that must be followed to obtain the 
necessary permits. Please let me know which option you choose, and I will be happy to provide you 
with guidance on the required process.

Property‐Owner Obligations with Respect to Tenant Displaced from Unsafe or Substandard Units: 
Under section 7.50.090 (a) and (d) of the SMMC, the tenant living in the accessory structure is 
entitled to permanent relocation (“Permanent Displacement”) payments equal to three times the 
current monthly HUD rate for a one‐bedroom unit in San Mateo County, as follows:  
 
1. The HUD rate for a one‐bedroom unit in San Mateo County is $2,923 x 3 = $8,769 
2. An Immediate vacation allowance of $1,000 because the tenant received fewer than 30 days’ 
advanced notice before being displaced. 
3. Actual moving costs and related expenses not to exceed $1,000. 
 
Total amount due to the tenant immediately: $9,769  
 
Total due within 60 days: Reimbursement for actual moving costs and related expenses not to exceed 
$1,000. 
 
Written Appeal (SMMC 7.50.100(a)): 
1. A property owner or tenant household may contest a decision, order, or determination 
regarding relocation payments or a notice of penalty or fine assessed this chapter by 
submitting an appeal in writing together with the appeal fee listed in the City’s fee schedule. 
The appeal shall set forth the factual basis for disputing the decision, order, or determination. 
2. Appeals must be addressed to the Director, and must be received within ten (10) days of the 
date appearing on the decision, order, or determination regarding relocation benefits or the 
notice of penalty or fine. A copy of the appeal must be provided by the appellant to any 
tenant household(s) or property owner(s) directly affected by the appeal on or by the same 
date that the appeal is received by the Director. 
 
Applicable Code Section:  
SMMC 7.14 Property Maintenance Code (IPMC 404.4.4 Prohibited Occupancy): 
Kitchens and uninhabitable spaces shall not be used for sleeping purposes. 
 
SMMC 7.14 Property Maintenance Code (IPMC 102.3) and California Health & Safety Code 17920.3 (n): 
Repairs, additions, or alterations to a structure or change of occupancy, shall be done in accordance with the 
procedures and provisions of the latest edition of the California Building Code(s). 
 
SMMC 7.16.030 Public nuisances adversely affecting the public peace and safety (b): 
All buildings and alteration of buildings, or parts thereof, made or erected in violation of City ordinances or 
any applicable County, State, or Federal laws prescribing the manner and materials for the construction or 
alteration, including construction without required permits. 
 
Permits required SMMC 23.07.010, SMMC 23.06.070 
 
Pursuant to SMCC §1.10, Administrative Citations: Each and every day a violation of this code exists 
constitutes a separate and distinct offense. Each section of the code violated constitutes a separate 
violation for any day at issue. However, the issuances of the administrative citations are not the 
exclusive remedy.

In addition to administrative penalties, the City may assess administrative costs against the violator.

matter, including, but not limited to, code enforcement staff and associated indirect costs, costs of 
inspection, investigation, attorneys’ fees. All administrative penalties and costs shall be borne by the 
owner of the property and the cost thereof shall be a lien upon and against the property and such 
lien shall continue in existence until the same shall be paid.

State Reasons for Appeal and Particular Areas of Concern (if an appeal of Planning Applications conditions of approval,
please list each condition appealed and the reason for your appeal):

The February 23, 2022 e-mail to Mr. Michaud threatens that if he did not make relocation
payments to the tenant at 330 Villa Terrace, "the City may exercise its discretion to pay the
relocation payments". This threat is not supported by the Code as Mr. Michaud had made an
offer in writing to move the tenant immediately to a replacement dwelling in compliance with
Section 7.50.030(b)(6).

TIMOTHY S. O'HARA
