February 24, 2022

Kristin Aiu

Code Enforcement, Community Development
330 W. 20" Avenue

San Mateo, CA 94403

Re: 330 Villa Terrace
Dear Ms Aiu:

I represent Terry Michaud and Kim Lucia, owners of 330 Villa Terrace.

This is to advise you that they are attempting to comply with the demands made in your
e-mail of February 14. On February 18 Terry Michaud personally delivered an offer in writing
to Martha Obrien, the tenant, to move her immediately to a replacement dwelling. The offer
complied with Municipal Code Section 7.50.030 (b)(6) which states:

“b) Notwithstanding subsection (a) above, a tenant household shall not be deemed
displaced due to code enforcement activities in any of the following cases:

6. The property owner offers, in writing, to move the tenant household immediately into a
replacement dwelling or room in the same building or complex , and all of the following
are true: (a) the replacement dwelling or room comparable in size, condition and
amenities to the formerly occupied dwelling or room; (b) the replacement dwelling or
room complies with all applicable zoning, building and housing codes; (c) the
replacement rent is no greater than the rent charged for the formerly occupied dwelling or
room; and (d) the offer was made prior to the time the tenant household has taken
definitive steps to move; “

A copy of the offer is enclosed.

On February 23 you sent an e-mail to Mr. Michaud informing him that Ms Obrien
refused to accept the offer.

Your e-mail also threatened that if he did not “pay the relocation payments within 24
hours of today, the City may exercise its discretion to pay the relocation payments.” Your threat
is not supported by the Code. In Section 7.50.030 it states:

&nbsp;
580 of&nbsp;662


--- page 581 ---
“(a) A tenant household shall be eligible for relocation payments from a property owner

the City's code enforcement activities.”

Because Mr. Michaud made an offer that complied with 7.50.030(b) the tenant is not “deemed
displaced.”.

For this reason, Mr. Michaud is filing an appeal to the Director in compliance with
Section 7.50.100. A copy of his appeal is enclosed.

Please understand that Mr. Michaud intends to cease using the subject unit as a dwelling
and intends to bring it into compliance with the City code.

He has begun this process by having Ms Obrien served with a 60 day notice terminating
her tenancy in compliance with California Civil Code Section 1946.2. A copy of the notice is
enclosed.

Very truly yours,
Timothy S. O'Hara
TOH//vf
Encl.
1. Section 7.50.030(b)(6) offer

2. Section 7.50.100 appeal
3. Civil Code Section 1946.2 notice

OFFER TO MOVE TENANT TO A REPLACEMENT DWELLING

This Offer to immediately move you to a Replacement Dwelling is made effective
February 16, 2022 by Terry Michaud and Lucia Kim, Trustees and Marc A. Rochette, co-owner,
hereinafter (“Owners”) to Martha Obrien, Tenant.

WHEREAS, the Owners, owners of the property at 330 Villa Terrace, San Mateo,
California have received a notice to abate a public nuisance, declaration of a substandard
dwelling condition, and notice to vacate the separate cottage you now occupy;

WHEREAS, the unit you now occupy (Unit A) is approximately 350 sq. feet, containing
one bedroom, a bath, and a kitchen having a 24 inch gas stove (approx..) but no separate dining
area, and no parking on site which you rent on a month-to-month basis for $1,645.00 payable
in advance;

WHEREAS, the Owners own a 650 sq. ft. (approx..) unit in a security building at 65
Hemlock Street, Unit 14, Redwood City, which is in a better condition and which has one
bedroom, a bath and a kitchen having a 30 inch (approx..) electric stove with a separate dining
area, and which includes a separate covered carport;

WHEREAS, the Unit on Hemlock complies with all applicable zoning, building and
housing codes;

WHEREAS the Municipal Code of the City of San Mateo requires relocation payments if
an offer of a comparable unit, on comparable terms is not offered (Section 7.50.030) and
requires the right to reoccupancy under certain conditions (Section 7.50.080);

THEREFORE the Owners hereby offer to immediately move you to 65 Hemlock Street,
Unit 14, Redwood City on the following terms:

1. You immediately surrender possession of Unit A at 330 Villa Terrace and agree to
lease unit 14 at 65 Hemlock for a period of 6 months at the rental rate of $1,645 per
month payable in advance commencing immediately;

2. Owners agree to reimburse you for your relocation expenses according to proof in
an amount not to exceed $1,000.00;

3. The Owners and you hereby agree that displacement from the unit at 330 Villa
Terrace will be permanent, and you waive any right under Municipal Code Section
7.50.080 to reoccupy the unit at 330 Villa Terrace;

4. You may elect to receive a refund of your $1,000 security deposit or to have it
applied to the $1,000 security deposit for Unit 14 at 655 Hemlock;

5. Mutual Waiver & Release. The Owners and Martha Obrien mutually agree to a full
and final release of all claim and defenses and as a further consideration and
inducement for thissettlement, agree to waive the provisions of California Civil Code
section 1542, which provides as follows:

be governed by, the laws of the State of California.

Counterparts. This Agreement may be executed in counter-parts with the same

date that the Agreement is fully executed by all Parties.

to certain conditions. You may or may not be able to reclaim property without incurring additional costs, depending on

7.50.030 Tenant eligibility for relocation payments.
(a) A tenant household shall be eligible for relocation payments from a property
owner under 
ifthe tenant household is displaced from its dwelling or
room due to the City's code enforcernent activities. For purposes of 
, a
tenant household shall be deemed to be displaced from its dwelling or room due to
code enforcement activities in either of the following circumstances:
1. The tenant household receives a notice from the property owner requiring the
household to vacate or quit the dwelling or room at any time after the city or a court
has issued a notice to vacate, notice to abate life-threatening condition or a declaration
of substandard condition covering that dwelling or room; or
2. The tenant household vacates its dwelling or room (whether or not the property
owner requires vacation) after (a) the city or a court has issued a notice to vacate,
notice to abate life-threatening condition, or declaration of substandard condition
covering that dwelling or room, and (b) the abatement period has expired without
correction of the noncomplying condition (if a time period to abate the noncomplying
condition is specified in such notice or declaration and the City or court does not order
earlier vacation).
(b) Notwithstanding subsection (a) above, a tenant homehold shall not be deemed
displaced due to code enforcement activities in any of the following cases:
6. The property owner offers, in writing, to move the tenant household
immediately into a replacement dwelling or room in the same building or
complex , and all of the following are true: (a) the replacement dwelling or
room comparable in size, condition and amenities to the formerly occupied
dwelling or room; (b) the replacement dwelling or room complies with all
applicable zontng, building and housing codes; (c) the replacement rent is no
greater than the rent charged for the formerly occupied dwelling or room; and
(d) the offer was made prior to the time the tenant household has taken
definitive steps to move: or

7.50.090 Amount of relocation payments.
(a) Permanent Displacement. An eligible tenant household that will experience
permanent displacanent as defined above shall receive a monetary relocation payment
fiom the property owner equal to three times the current monthly United States
Departrnent of Housing and Urban Development (H[ID) Fair Market Rent for a unit
of comparable size and type to the dwelling or room from which the displacement
occurs, plus a payment not to exceed one thousand dollars ($1,000.00) for actual
moving costs and related expenses incurred by the tenant household and substantiated
by reasonably probative documentation. The property owner shall treat any non-
complying dwelling or room that does not have one or more separate bedrooms as ar
"efficiency unit" for pwposes of determining the applicable HUD Fair Mmket Rent
and calculating the required monetary relocation payment on that basis. ln addition,
the tenant household is entitled to a refund and/or accounting for any security deposit
held by the property owner pursuant to 
. For
purposes of 
, ''HUD Fair Market Rent" means the amount specified in the
schedule of Fair Market Rents for existing housing published by the U.S. Deparhnent
of Housing and Urban Development under Section 8 of the United States Housing Act
of 1937, as amended, applicable to the City of San Mateo and current as of the date
the City or court issues the notice to vacate, notice to abate life-tlreatening condition,
or declaration of substandard condition.

San Mateo County, CA is part of the San Franclsco, CA HUD Metro FMR Area, which
consists of the following counties: Marin County, CA; San Francisco County, CA; and San
Mateo County, CA. All information here applies to the entirety of the San Francisco, CA
HUD Metro FMR Area.
Fair Market Rent Calculation Methodotogy
Show/ H i de M ethod olog y Na rrative
Fair Market Rents for metropolitan areas and non -metropolitan FMR areas are developed as
follows:
1. 2015-2019 S-year American Community Survey (ACS) estimates of 2-bedroom
adjusted standard quality gross rents calculated for each FMR area are used as the
new basis for FY2O22 provided the estimate is statistically reliable. For Fy2O22, the
test for reliability is whether the margin of error for the estimate is less than 50Vo of
the estimate itself and whether the ACS estimate is based on at least 1OO survey
cases. HUD does not receive the exact number of survey cases, but rather a
categorical variable known as the count indicator indicating a range of cases. An
estimate based on at least 100 cases corresponds to a count indicator of 4 or higher.
If an area does not have a reliable 2015-2019 s-year, HUD checks whether the area
has had at least minimally reliable estimate in any of the past 3 years, or estimates
that meet the 50o/o margin of error test described above, If so, the FY2022 base rent
is the average of the inflated ACS estimates.
If an area has not had a minimally reliable estimate in the past 3 years, the estimate
State for the area's corresponding metropolitan area (if applicable) or State non-
metropolitan area ls used as the basis for FY2O22.
2. HUD calculates a recent mover adjustment factor by comparing a 2019 1-year 40th
percentile recent mover 2-bedrooom rent to the 2015-2019 s-year 40th percentile

the hole. | inspected inside the unit and saw the water heater
inside the kitchen but was unable to locate the year of the
water. It did appear to be newer. | then inspected the bathroom
toilet and saw water residue behind the toilet and saw the toilet
crookedly placed. Ms. O'Brien stated that there had been
several plumbers who have stopped by but the issue
continually occurs. Ms. O'Brien thinks the plumbers aren't fixing
the sewer. Ms. O'Brien also mentioned that the electricity goes
off when the heater and another item like a toaster is on. |
inspected the sub panel outside on the house and saw some
faulty wiring and other issues. When back in the office | had
internal meeting with CEM and the Building Official, Mike T. It
was determined that the subpanel is a fire hazard and needs to
be immediately addressed. Per Mike T. if the-electrical is not
inspected/assessed by a licensed electrician within 24/48
hours, then the house could be red tagged and possibly
relocation. It was also decided that all panels on the parcel
should be inspected as well. | received the property owner,

notified him of the electrical and what needed to be done. | then
prepared an abatement notice which was emailed the the tenant

to all tenants.

. 02/02/2022, |, Kristen Aiu, CEO CSM, met with Carlos from CA Kristen Aiu
Electric LIC # 874972 at City Hall. He explained that he
conducted an inspection of just the maim panel for unit A and B.
Carlos stated he has temporarily made the panel safe and is no
longer a fire hazard. Mike T. was also present and was
satisfied with the temporary emergency repairs and the
proposed improvements to make the panels safe. Regarding
unpermitted conversion of a storage structure to habitable
space, per Mike T, we cannot regulate the size of the panel for
the garage/storage structure. Therefore the proposed work for
the electric is allowed and will proceed. | asked Carlos if he had
inspected all the units inside, he stated he had been at the
property 2/3 times since Thursday. Has looked at Unit B's light
fixture and there were no issues, just replaced the fixture.

Other than that, he hasn't inspected the interior of all units. |

notified Carlos, that | required the owner to have him inspected
all electrical on the property but we will discuss with the owner.
Permit was issued and letter from Carlos included in case files.

RESEARCH:

After reviewing the past permits on the property, there was little
information on the structures. What was found was a 1991
permit that states, cottage to be converted to a storage unit.
Based on this permit and the lack of other permits on file, | have
concluded that Unit A is not a legal habitable unit and will either
need to be converted back to a storage structure or apply to be
legalized as a separate unit. This violation also does not qualify
for delayed enforcement since there is already an existing ADU
(unit B) on the property. Unit A would need its own separate
address to possible be allowed. Need to confirm with planning
if they would allow this.
